Purpose: To respond to Council’s September 9, 2025, motion regarding exempting Brentwood Park properties with Restrictive Covenants from Provincial SSMUH requirements.

Meeting April 28, 2026 
File: DEV-01 
COUNCIL REPORT 
 
TO: 
MAYOR & COUNCILLORS 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT  
SUBJECT: 
BRENTWOOD PARK SSMUH MOTION – RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS 
AND STATUTORY BUILDING SCHEMES 
 
PURPOSE: 
To respond to Council’s September 9, 2025, motion regarding exempting 
Brentwood Park properties with Restrictive Covenants from Provincial 
SSMUH requirements. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion – Restrictive Covenants 
and Statutory Building Schemes”, dated April 28, 2026, be received for 
information.  
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
On September 9, 2025, Council adopted a resolution to review all bylaw and other 
regulatory amendments to have the Brentwood Park neighbourhood exempted from the 
Province’s SSMUH mandates until Restrictive Covenants have been cleared by the 
owner/developer.   
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are private property interests 
registered on title that the City is not party to.  The City of Burnaby is bound by 
Provincial law and regulations to zone for SSMUH development, and Restrictive 
Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are not included as exemptions from 
SSMUH requirements.   
Under the Community Charter’s authority to regulate buildings and structures, Council 
may direct an amendment to the Burnaby Building Bylaw to require compliance with 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes as part of Building Permit 
approval.  Such a requirement would have to apply City-wide to all properties with 
registered Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes, including residential, 
commercial, industrial, etc.  Implementation of this requirement would result in added 
challenges, including limitations to suites and duplexes, as well as increased application 
cost and process time for the City and applicants and impacts on different types of 
properties and in many parts of the City outside the Brentwood Park residential 
neighbourhood identified in the September 9, 2025, resolution.  For these reasons it is 
not recommended by staff. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
The report provided aligns with the following provincial and municipal laws, bylaws, and 
policies, including: 

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
• Local Government Act (2015) 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) 
• Burnaby Housing Needs Report (2021; interim update 2024) 
• HOME: Burnaby’s Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021) 
• Mayor’s Task Force on Community Housing Final Report (2019) 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025) 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
2.1  Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion 
At the September 9, 2025 ,Open Council meeting, Council adopted the following 
motion: 
WHEREAS the Brentwood Park neighbourhood is an established neighbourhood 
with a distinct character that is protected by Restrictive Covenants that do not 
contemplate the kinds of development generated by provincial mandates related 
to Small‑Scale Multi‑Unit Housing (SSMUH);  
THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT staff move forward expeditiously with all 
bylaw and other regulatory amendments to have the Brentwood Park 
neighbourhood exempted from the Province’s SSMUH until Restrictive 
Covenants have been cleared by the owner/developer. 
The motion directs staff to explore mechanisms to exempt Brentwood Park from 
SSMUH mandates on the basis of existing Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building 
Schemes registered on title. This report provides information on Restrictive Covenants 
and Statutory Building Schemes generally, the potential to amend the Building Bylaw to 
require compliance with a Statutory Building Scheme or Restrictive Covenant 
requirement relating to construction as part of Building Permit issuance, and the 
practical implications of including such a requirement in the Building Bylaw.   
2.2  Overview of Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are private property interests 
registered on title under the Land Title Act to allow private parties (as opposed to 
municipalities and other public authorities) to restrict the use and character of a group of 
properties by regulating matters such as minimum lot sizes, building setbacks, 
architectural character, land use restrictions, types of dwellings, etc. Restrictive 
Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes were particularly popular in the 1950s and 
1960s prior to the adoption of most comprehensive zoning bylaws.  The Brentwood 
Park neighbourhood contains a significant number of lots encumbered by Restrictive 
Covenants or Statutory Building Schemes dating from this era. Although they are 
seldom used at this time, Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes remain 
valid under the Land Title Act and continue to apply to properties where these charges 
are registered on title and not subsequently discharged.  
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes exist in many other Burnaby 
neighbourhoods, including but not limited to Capitol Hill, Suncrest, Deer Lake, Garden 

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
Village, Government Road, and Lochdale.  The exact location and total number of 
affected properties is currently unknown.  Many of these Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes are outdated, ambiguous, and were intended by the initial 
developer/subdivider to apply to the use and character of early buildings within a 
development in order to maintain the development’s consistency, primarily as a 
marketing feature for future sales within the area.   
2.3  Applicability and Enforceability  
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are private property rights and 
represent obligations imposed on property owners that run with the land, i.e. are 
enforceable against future owners of a property even though they did not enter into the 
agreement with the initial developer/subdivider.  Where the initial party no longer exists 
(which is often the case), Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes can be 
enforced by the owners of adjacent properties with the same charges on title if they 
wish to maintain the requirements under the charges.  The City is not party to the 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes and cannot enforce them 
directly.  Currently, City staff review land titles as part of Planning (i.e. Rezoning, 
Preliminary Plan Approval and Subdivision) and Building Permit applications and 
notifies applicants of existing Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes on 
title and encourages the owners to resolve obligations under these charges prior to 
advancing development and/or construction. As part of the Building Department’s 
current Building Permit application process, the applicant is required to sign a 
declaration acknowledging that the City is not a party to the private charges and that 
issuance of a Building Permit does not absolve the applicant from their legal 
responsibilities under the charge.  To date, such acknowledgements have not precluded 
SSMUH development.  Other municipalities within Metro Vancouver take similar 
approaches and do not enforce requirements under Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes as part of their Building Permit process.  
2.4  Provincial SSMUH Legislation 
The Province’s SSMUH legislation passed in December 2023 requires municipalities to 
permit SSMUH developments—such as triplexes, fourplexes, and small multiplexes — 
on most residential lots formerly zoned for single family and duplex dwellings. The 
legislation is mandatory and sets minimum numbers of dwelling units that must be 
permitted under municipal zoning bylaws based on lot size and other criteria.  The City’s 
zoning bylaw cannot contradict these mandatory requirements.  There are limited 
exemptions under the Provincial SSMUH legislation and regulation, and Restrictive 
Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are not included as permitted exemptions.  
However, the Province has made it clear that Statutory Building Schemes remain valid, 
and it is the responsibility of the property owner to uphold the restrictions in the building 
scheme.  The Provincial Policy Manual & Site Standards for SSMUH (Provincial Policy 
Manual) provides the following guidance in respect to Statutory Building Schemes (at 
pages 25-26):  
Statutory building schemes are another form of restriction registered on a 
parcel’s title that could impact the potential to achieve the residential densities 

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 4 
 
prescribed by the SSMUH legislation. Statutory building schemes are generally 
reciprocal, in that the restrictions on each lot are imposed for the benefit of the 
other lots in the development. 
Restrictions imposed by the building scheme run with the land and bind future 
owners/renters in the subdivision. Typical restrictions or requirements deal with 
building sizes, styles, finishes or colors, but can also restrict the use of buildings. 
Local governments are not generally party to, or responsible for the 
administration of the building scheme. 
Provided the building scheme is valid, an existing statutory building scheme 
registered on title that limits the use of a property to one dwelling unit will take 
precedence over the unit densities prescribed through zoning updates made in 
accordance with the SSMUH legislation. This does not prevent a local 
government from zoning land subject to a statutory building scheme for a higher 
density, but the first responsibility of the owner(s) of that land is to uphold the 
terms of the building scheme. 
The Province has been monitoring the situation with regards to Restrictive Covenants 
and Statutory Building Schemes that restrict the full implementation of the Provincial 
SSMUH requirements.  However, changes to legislation or regulation have yet to be made 
overriding such charges.  
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1  Potential Building Bylaw Amendments 
In respect to Brentwood Park, Council’s specific motion at the September 9, 2026, Open 
meeting was as follows:   
THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT staff move forward expeditiously with all 
bylaw and other regulatory amendments to have the Brentwood Park 
neighbourhood exempted from the Province’s SSMUH until Restrictive 
Covenants have been cleared by the owner/developer. 
Due to lack of legislative authority, staff are unable to implement the direction in 
Council’s motion.  Notably, a bylaw amendment relating to Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes cannot address the Brentwood Park neighbourhood only 
and the City cannot compel property owners to discharge a private charge.  
As an alternative, it could be within the Council’s authority to regulate buildings and 
structures (under s. 8 of the Community Charter) to amend the Burnaby Building Bylaw 
to allow a Building Permit to be refused if it does not comply with a Restrictive Covenant 
or Statutory Building Scheme registered on title.  In this case, the recourse for an 
applicant would be that they comply with the conditions of the Restrictive Covenant or 
Statutory Building Scheme, or seek to have the charge removed from title (either 
through a Court process or consent of the original charge holder or all applicable 
adjacent property owners subject to the same Restrictive Covenant or Statutory Building 
Scheme) prior to advancing a Building Permit application.  

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 5 
 
Should Council wish to proceed with directing staff to bring forward an amendment to 
the Building Bylaw, the requirement would need to apply throughout the City as a 
condition in the Building Bylaw could not be limited to one geographic area.  As such, if 
the bylaw amendment were implemented, all properties with Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes throughout the City would be required to demonstrate 
compliance with conditions contained in those charges or have the charges removed 
from title as part of advancing a Building Permit application.   
3.1.1  Potential Planning Considerations / Challenges 
With respect to broader planning policy considerations and challenges, the enforcement 
of third-party Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes that restrict the 
development of R1 – SSMUH Development would be inconsistent with the following 
bylaws, laws and regulations which permit and/or require SSMUH development on all 
residential lots.  
• Burnaby Zoning Bylaw R1 SSMUH District 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan SSMUH Designations 
• Local Government Act, Section 481.3 [zoning bylaws and small scale multi-family 
housing] 
• Local Government Zoning Bylaw Regulation  
Notwithstanding, as Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are not 
registered on all lots, and since they are private charges and owners have the 
opportunity to seek the discharge of these charges, should Council direct staff to make 
amendments to the Building Bylaw as discussed in section 3.1 above, it would not be in 
direct conflict with the above-referenced bylaws, legislation and regulations.   
It is noted, however, that upholding Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building 
Schemes that the City is not party to, may be interpreted by other neighbourhoods 
subject to SSMUH legislation that Council is seeking additional means of restricting 
SSMUH development more broadly outside of the conditions to exempt SSMUH 
development set within the Local Government Act and Local Government Zoning Bylaw 
Regulation.  This may lead to additional requests of other neighbourhoods to further 
restrict SSMUH development in Burnaby, creating greater pressure on the City not to 
align with the intent of the Provincial SSMUH legislation.  Further action in this regard 
would not only frustrate SSMUH development, which broadly is intended to allow for 
greater housing choice in accordance with the findings of the Mayor’s Task Force on 
Community Housing, Home Strategy, and the City’s Housing Choices program, but may 
restrict the ability to develop suites or duplexes which are also limited by such 
covenants. Further, the application of such a compliance requirement in the Building 
Bylaw will affect properties throughout the City beyond lots zoned for SSMUH, i.e. 
commercial and industrial properties with Restrictive Covenants or Statutory Building 
Schemes registered on title. The requirements under those charges may not align with 
the intent of the City’s commercial, employment and industrial zoning districts, 
especially following the implementation of proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026 as part of the 
Zoning Bylaw Rewrite project.  

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 6 
 
3.1.2  Potential Building Considerations / Challenges 
There are some significant administrative challenges for the Building Division if 
compliance with Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes registered on 
title is a requirement of Building Permit approval. While the City is aware of multiple 
neighbourhoods and properties with Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building 
Schemes (mostly from previous applications relating to properties with these charges on 
title), we do not have, nor can we simply obtain, a comprehensive list of the potentially 
thousands of affected properties.  Further to this, the Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes may contain conditions and restrictions that are not subject 
to City reviews, including landscape standards, material standards, and use conditions 
that are not checked by the Building Division.  Further some Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes relate to commercial and industrial properties outside the 
scope of SSMUH controls.  As such, should Council direct staff to bring forward 
amendments to the Burnaby Building Bylaw to require compliance with Statutory 
Building Schemes or Restrictive Covenants as part of Building Permit approval, 
changes to the Building Permit application process will be required.  For SSMUH 
developments, such changes could include amendments to the Residential initial 
application process to require an applicant engage a lawyer to prepare a title summary 
at the applicant’s expense to identify any charges that restrict construction or include 
specific construction requirements, note all conditions contained within the charges, and 
identify those that specifically pertain to zoning and building requirements, such as 
number of dwelling units, bulk, massing, etc.  Furthermore, the applicant would be 
required to either agree to comply with the conditions of the Restrictive Covenants or 
Statutory Building Scheme, or seek the discharge of these encumbrances prior to 
advancing a full Building Permit application.  The results of this additional review for all 
properties with Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes registered on title 
will undoubtedly add time and expense to the development process for the City and 
applicants. 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Not applicable at this time.  Should Council direct staff to prepare amendments to the 
Burnaby Building Bylaw applicable through the City to require compliance with 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes as part of Building Permit 
approval, staff would update the Building Division’s process documents and information 
on the City’s website.  
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Should amendments to the Burnaby Building Bylaw be made to require compliance with 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes as part of Building Permit 
approval, additional submission requirements and review of applicable Restrictive 
Covenant and Statutory Building Scheme requirements would likely add time and cost 
to the development application process, both in terms of City staff and applicant 
resources. For these reasons, and others noted in this report, amending the Burnaby 

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 7 
 
Building Bylaw to uphold private third-party Restrictive Covenants and Statutory 
Building Schemes is not recommended by staff.  
Respectfully submitted, 
Lee-Ann Garnett, Deputy General Manager Planning and Development, on behalf of 
E.W. Kozak, General Manager Planning and Development 
ATTACHMENTS 
None. 
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS 
This report was prepared by Johannes Schumann, Senior Director Development 
Services and May Leung, City Solicitor, and reviewed by Jason Watt, Chief Building 
Inspector.

COMMISSION REPORT 
 
TO: 
COMMUNITY HERITAGE COMMISSION (CHC) 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
AS-FOUND REPORT AND STATEMENT OF SIGNIFICANCE FOR 
HERITAGE RESOURCES AT 3789 ROYAL OAK AVENUE 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide Council with an as-found report and Statement of Significance 
for heritage resources located at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue. 
 
REFERENCES 
Address: 
 
3789 Royal Oak Avenue 
Legal:  
 
PID: 023-992-972 
 
 
 
Lot 1: District Lot 73, 74 and 81 Group 1 
 
 
 
New Westminster District Plan LMP36185 
Current Zoning: 
P4 Cemetery District 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “As-found Report and Statement of Significance for 
Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue,” dated April 9, 2026, be received 
for information. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
Documentation of the Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel through an as-found report 
and preparation of a Statement of Significance for the memorial park is consistent with 
the Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025). 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
The Forest Lawn Memorial Park at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue (Attachment 1) is identified 
in the City’s heritage inventory Burnaby’s Heritage: An Inventory of Buildings and 
Structures (2011). The inventory is a list of heritage resources that have significant 
heritage value and are recommended for further consideration for inclusion on the 
Community Heritage Register and for permanent protection. The property is the subject 
of a development application (Rezoning Application #24-07) that includes the demolition 
of the Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel (‘the Chapel’). Submission of a Statement of 
Significance for the memorial park and an as-found report for the Chapel were 
conditions of development approval. This report advances the as-found report and 
Statement of Significance to Council for information. 

As-found Report and Statement of Significance for Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue 
CHC Meeting April 9, 2026  .......................................................... Page 2 
 
2.1  Forest Lawn Memorial Park 
The Forest Lawn Memorial Park opened to the public in September 1936.  The 
cemetery’s original design featured a curvilinear road network and manicured lawns 
with flush, bronze grave markers to create a space that felt more like an open park than 
a traditional cemetery. Since that time, the memorial park has continued to evolve, 
including the development of an assortment of public monuments, gardens, 
architectural structures, community-designated burial sections, and other memorials 
and commemorations. 
 
Prior to 1955, funeral services were performed at a Chapel of Peace, located outside 
the gates of the memorial park on the northeast corner of Royal Oak Avenue and Sprott 
Street. The Chapel of Peace building was purchased by an Anglican congregation in 
1955 and moved to 5584 Kincaid Street (where it is protected by Heritage Designation 
Bylaw #13162 and continues to operate as a place of worship).  
 
Development of an on-site chapel at Forest Lawn Memorial Park began soon after, with 
architectural plans for the Chapel completed in 1961. An official opening was held in 
June 1962 for the building, which was originally known as the “Little Chapel of Flowers.” 
The Chapel was designed by architects McCarter & Nairne, who are responsible for 
several significant buildings in the region (including Vancouver’s Marine Building).  
Forest Lawn Cemetery is listed on the City’s Heritage Inventory. The inventory listing 
identifies the Chapel and the landscape design of the Forest Lawn Memorial Park as 
components of its heritage value. 
2.2  Rezoning Application  
Rezoning Application #24-07 received Third Reading at the January 13, 2026 Council 
meeting. It is anticipated that the proposal will be advanced for final adoption later this 
year. 
The rezoning application seeks to rezone a portion of the property from P4 Cemetery 
District to CD Comprehensive Development District to redevelop Forest Lawn Memorial 
Park’s funeral home facilities. The proposed development includes demolition of five 
buildings and construction of a new, two-storey facility to consolidate existing services 
into one building at the northeast corner of the site. The Chapel building is among the 
buildings that is proposed to be demolished. 
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
As the property is not formally protected by a Heritage Designation Bylaw or listed on 
the Community Heritage Register, retention of the chapel is not required. 
However, as the rezoning application provides an opportunity for the owner to consider 
the heritage values of the property, staff requested the following as conditions of 
development approval: 

As-found Report and Statement of Significance for Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue 
CHC Meeting April 9, 2026  .......................................................... Page 3 
 
• Documentation of the Chapel building through an as-found report that is 
acceptable to the City; and 
• Preparation of a Statement of Significance for the site to identify heritage values 
and character defining elements of the memorial park. 
These documents have subsequently been received and accepted by staff. They are 
attached here for the information of Council. A brief description of these documents is 
provided below. 
3.1  As-found Report 
An as-found report is a record of a heritage resource created to retain information about 
a heritage building, site, or structure as part of the historical record. The as-found report 
for the Chapel was produced by Luxton Heritage Consultants and includes a historical 
overview and photographs of the building, contemporary site photos, the original 
architectural plans, and an as-built floor plan with interior photo references (Attachment 
2). A set of as-built drawings and elevations was also included (Attachment 3). 
Advancing the as-found report as an attachment to a Council report ensures its 
retention as a permanent City record that is accessible to the public. It also provides an 
opportunity for Council and the Community Heritage Commission to be aware of the use 
of as-found reports as a best practice for heritage resource management when a 
resource cannot be retained. 
3.2  Statement of Significance 
A Statement of Significance (SOS) is a heritage resource management tool that 
describes a historic place. 
A SOS describes why a historic resource is important (its heritage value) and identifies 
the specific features of the resource that contribute to its value (the ‘character defining 
elements’). The statements are cornerstones of a “values-based” approach to heritage 
management: they identify which components of a resource should be retained based 
on how those components contribute to the overall value. By identifying character-
defining elements, an SOS helps property owners avoid interventions that could harm 
the overall heritage value of a property.  
A values-based approach can also ensure that features that might not be original to a 
site or may not stand out for their architectural uniqueness can still be recognized as 
important if they have value to the community. In the case of the Forest Lawn Memorial 
Park, special features include commemorative monuments that mark community-
designated burial sections that serve as spaces within the cemetery for cultural and 
religious communities.  
The SOS can be used to guide the owners of the memorial park to avoid impact on 
character-defining elements when altering the cemetery landscape. It can also help the 
City identify when a development permit impacts a character-defining element, reducing 
the time needed to review applications that do not impact heritage character. For future 

As-found Report and Statement of Significance for Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue 
CHC Meeting April 9, 2026  .......................................................... Page 4 
 
applications, when a planned development has potential to impact a character-defining 
element, the property owners could contact the City early in their planning to explore 
options to support retention. 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Not applicable. 
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Not applicable. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
E.W. Kozak, General Manager, Planning and Development

Attachment 3 – As-built drawings, Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel 
Attachment 4 – Statement of Significance for Forest Lawn Memorial Park

REPORT 
 
The Environment Committee, at its meeting held on April 13, 2026, received and

On behalf of the Environment 
Committee, 
 
 
Councillor J. Keithley 
Chair 
 
 
Councillor M. Santiago 
Vice Chair 

 
Meeting April 13, 2026 
File: EM-08 
COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
ENVIRONMENT COMMITTEE (EC) 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER ENGINEERING  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
SOLID WASTE AND RECYCLING (SWR) 2025 ANNUAL REPORT 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide a summary of the City of Burnaby solid waste and recycling 
collection system performance. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Solid Waste and Recycling (SWR) 2025 Annual Report” 
dated April 13, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
Burnaby is one of twenty-one member municipalities that coordinate with Metro 
Vancouver on the plans and initiatives shaping our regional waste management system. 
The regional Integrated Solid Waste and Resource Management Plan (ISWRMP) was 
approved by the BC Ministry of Environment in 2011 and is currently undergoing a 
process of updating to build off current successes while accelerating waste diversion, 
reducing GHG emissions, and promoting a circular economy. 
 
Federal and Provincial environmental initiatives have helped to further shape waste 
management in the region, including the: 
 
• Canada-wide Action Plan on Zero Plastic Waste 
• Single-use Plastic Prohibition Regulations 
• CleanBC Roadmap to 2030 
• CleanBC Plastics Action Plan 
• Single-use and Plastics Waste Prevention Regulation 
 
At the City of Burnaby level, waste management is primarily supported by the following 
City bylaws: 
 
• The Burnaby Solid Waste and Recycling Bylaw 2010 
• The Burnaby Bylaw Enforcement Bylaw 2009 
• The Burnaby Construction and Demolition Waste Diversion Bylaw  
• The Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw 
 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The SWR Annual Report summarizes the City’s current waste management program 
performance as well as planned enhancements for the coming years ahead. It includes 

Subject: Solid Waste and Recycling (SWR) 2025 Annual Report 
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 2 
 
data on recycling, green waste, and garbage from homes and businesses through 2025, 
highlighting key trends. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
The City of Burnaby provides comprehensive solid waste and recycling services to its 
customers. The services include curbside collection of various waste streams and large 
items, supplemented by drop-off options for additional recyclables at the Eco-Centre, 
and, more recently, by large scale annual cleanup events. In addition, the program 
devotes significant resources each year to keeping streets and public spaces clean 
through regular litter pick-up, streetscape receptacle servicing, and abandoned waste 
collection. 
3.1 Program Highlights and Performance 
In 2025, the City delivered strong environmental outcomes, expanded public 
services, and advanced key waste-diversion and sustainability initiatives across 
Burnaby. 
The Solid Waste and Recycling Division collected 74,489 tonnes of total material - 
including garbage, recyclables, green waste – from all sectors. This represents a 1% 
increase as compared to the previous year. The largest gain occurred in recyclable 
materials, which increased by 447 tonnes, followed by a modest rise in green waste. 
These results demonstrate that although total waste generation fluctuates with 
population growth and behavioural changes, the City continues to make steady 
gains in recycling performance and diversion outcomes. The overall diversion rate 
was observed at 61%. 
The Burnaby Eco-Centre played a significant role in maintaining this diversion rate. 
The facility recorded increased volumes across several key categories. Notable 
growth in scrap metal, used oil, donated clothing items, polystyrene (Styrofoam), 
electronics, small appliances, paints, and mattresses volumes were recorded. These 
increases demonstrate strong resident participation and the ongoing effectiveness of 
the City’s recycling stewardship partnerships.  
In June of 2022, the City adopted a Construction and Demolition (C&D) Waste 
Diversion Bylaw which requires builders to divert material from the landfill to reuse 
and recycling. Last year alone, 26,972 tonnes of C&D material was diverted. This 
brings the cumulative total material diverted to 44,592 tonnes since program 
inception, with an average diversion rate of 91% per building. 
 
3.2 Looking Ahead to the Future 
The City will continue to strengthen its waste reduction and diversion programs 
through a series of planned initiatives and service enhancements. These efforts will 
build on the successes of recent years while supporting the City’s long-term 
sustainability objectives. 

Subject: Solid Waste and Recycling (SWR) 2025 Annual Report 
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 3 
 
In the coming years, the City plans to: 
• Host additional cleanup events to provide residents with expanded 
opportunities to properly dispose of garbage, recyclables and reusable items. 
• Support new SSMUH developments and ensure the highest level of service 
consistency across all housing types. 
• Monitor Curbside Flexible Plastics collection pilots in partnership with Recycle 
BC to determine their long-term viability for Burnaby and evaluate the 
potential for broader implementation. 
• Continue supporting repair cafés and local reuse initiatives in collaboration 
with community partners to promote waste reduction through repair and 
material reuse. 
• Expand waste diversion and recycling outreach to additional schools, 
neighborhoods and community groups to strengthen public awareness and 
participation. 
• Launch the “Recycling Rock Star” recognition initiative, offering positive 
reinforcement to residents who consistently demonstrate excellent recycling 
practices. 
• Continue research and testing of bear-resistant equipment compatible with 
the City’s collection vehicles to further reduce wildlife attractants and improve 
community safety. 
• Expand the three-stream streetscape bin program at transit stops and explore 
enclosure designs that provide flexibility in limited spaces while remaining 
compatible with automated collection trucks. 
• Explore options to raise the minimum C&D diversion threshold levels. 
 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
In keeping with the goal of reducing waste, only a limited number of hard copies of the 
Solid Waste and Recycling 2025 Annual Report will be printed. An electronic copy of this 
report, as in previous years, will be available on the City website for viewing. 
 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
This report is provided for informational purposes only. There are no financial 
considerations at this time. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
May Phang, P.Eng., General Manager Engineering

SUBJECT:

PURPOSE: 
To provide an operational update on food sustainability measures 
within the City of Burnaby.  
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “SU: Enhancing Food Sustainability in Burnaby” dated April 
13, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
This report aligns with the Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) and Social Sustainability 
Strategy (2011). 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
On March 31, 2025, Environment Committee meeting a motion was adopted requesting 
staff to explore the feasibility of more plant-forward food options in city facilities and 
report back on new ways of informing, promoting and incentivizing patrons to choose 
more sustainable food options. The city operates several cafes and restaurants within 
civic facilities and parks, and over previous years have already introduced a range of 
practical measures to improve the availability of plant-based, vegetarian, vegan, and 
dairy-free options. 
At the same time, food-service operations must remain customer-focused, operationally 
sound, and financially responsible. For that reason, menu development continues to 
reflect customer demand while providing a variety of dietary options. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
Staff have adopted a balanced and operationally practical approach to food 
sustainability within City food-service operations. This approach focuses on maintaining 
customer choice, responding to demonstrable demand, and introducing or refining 
plant-based and vegetarian offerings where it is operationally feasible. 

Subject: SU: Enhancing Food Sustainability in Burnaby  
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 2 
 
3.1.1 Current Status 
Plant-based and vegetarian options have already been introduced at Riverway, Mintara 
a top Burnaby Mountain (“Mintara”) and other service locations. These offerings are 
included within regular menu planning so that customers have meaningful choice 
alongside traditional menu items. 
Additional vegetarian, vegan and dairy-free selections are also available through café 
and concession operations, including the Passion Coffee program. Where customers 
request dairy-free, vegetarian, or vegan accommodation that is not specifically listed on 
a menu, staff make reasonable efforts to address those requests when service 
conditions permit. 
Staff also continue to pursue local sourcing opportunities where feasible and to make 
operational choices that support sustainability objectives, including the use of an electric 
roasting system within the coffee program. 
3.1.2 Operating Approach 
Staff’s approach is deliberate and measured. The role of the food service operation is to 
provide customer choice and respond to demonstrated demand, while ensuring services 
remain operationally sound and financially responsible. 
Staff will continue to introduce, retain, and refine plant-based and vegetarian offerings 
where there is clear customer uptake, operational fit and acceptable menu performance. 
Staff will also continue to monitor demand and item performance by location, adjust 
offerings based on sales and feedback, accommodate reasonable dietary requests 
where feasible, and add further options incrementally where demand supports 
continued expansion. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
No specific communication or engagement is required as part of this report. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The city’s Food Services operations provide food and beverage options at civic facilities 
in a financially responsible manner while meeting customer demand. Staff have 
introduced plant-based food options across facilities at level aligned with current 
demand and continue to monitor interest in these offerings. As with any menu 
adjustments, expanding or promoting additional plant-forward options require careful 
consideration of potential impacts on revenue and costs. Food Services reviews menu 
performance and customer preferences to ensure an appropriate balance between 
financial sustainability and evolving dietary trends and will continue to evaluate 
opportunities accordingly.  
 

Subject: SU: Enhancing Food Sustainability in Burnaby  
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 3 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
Carmen Gonzalez, Deputy General Manager Parks, Recreation and Culture

REPORT

Meeting April 8, 2026 
File: TM-06 
COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
TRANSPORTATION COMMITTEE (TC) 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER ENGINEERING  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
CENTRAL BOULEVARD AND PARKADE INTERSECTION SAFETY 
IMPROVEMENTS  
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide Council with an overview of the proposed safety 
improvements at the intersection of Central Boulevard and Metropolis 
at Metrotown underground parkade access. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Central Boulevard and Parkade Intersection Safety 
Improvements” dated April 8, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The proposed safety improvements at the intersection of Central Boulevard and 
Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade access support the City of Burnaby’s 
Corporate Strategic Plan and goals for a safe, connected, healthy, and dynamic 
community. The project aligns with Council-adopted goals, policies, and targets 
identified in the Burnaby Transportation Plan including Vision Zero, and the City’s 
Climate Action Framework that supports the development of a sustainable, safe, and 
accessible transportation network. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Metrotown is one of Burnaby’s Town Centres and a key destination in the region. The 
growing residential population and sustained commercial activities have contributed to 
high pedestrian activities in the area. The signalized intersection of Central Boulevard 
and the Metropolis underground parkade access (the “project location”) provides a key 
connection between the SkyTrain station, bus loop and shopping centre for pedestrians 
and transit riders. The high pedestrian and vehicle traffic volumes have resulted in traffic 
safety concerns raised by the public and Metropolis. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
At the project location, the main pedestrian safety concerns pertain to conflicts between 
crossing pedestrians and turning vehicles. To mitigate these concerns, this project 
includes a new exclusive pedestrian signal phase (pedestrian scramble) as well as 
other intersection enhancements. Together, these improvements will enhance the safety 
environment at this location such that the measures are intuitive and inclusive for all 
users, as detailed below. 

Subject: Central Boulevard and Parkade Intersection Safety Improvements  
TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 2 
 
 
3.1 Pedestrian Scramble Signal Phase 
A pedestrian scramble phase is a type of traffic signal operation where all vehicular 
movements are stopped. During this signal phase, pedestrians can cross the 
intersection in all directions – including diagonally – while all vehicles are stopped. 
Vehicles can then proceed after the phase without any potential conflicts with 
crossing pedestrians. This time separation allows pedestrians to cross without 
conflicting vehicle movements and simplifies driver decision-making. 
3.2 Intersection Improvements 
Geometric enhancements are proposed to improve safety and accessibility for all 
road users. Curb ramp accessibility and pedestrian waiting areas will be improved 
together with more direct crossing alignments. The right-turn island on the northeast 
corner will be removed, which reduces the number of crossings needed for 
pedestrians, minimizing the exposure to traffic. Accessible pedestrian signals (APS) 
will also feature updated audible messaging to clearly indicate that pedestrians may 
cross in all directions during the pedestrian scramble signal phase. 
3.3 Project Timeline 
Detailed design is expected to be completed in April 2026. Construction is scheduled 
to commence shortly after with the completion targeted for Summer 2026. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
The project team will be providing stakeholders, including Metropolis at Metrotown, with 
notification ahead of construction. The project team will continue to address queries and 
concerns as they arise. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The funding for this project has been committed in the Engineering 5-year capital plan, 
2026 – 2030. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
May Phang, P.Eng., General Manager Engineering

Wang, P.Eng., PMP, Transportation Engineer, and reviewed by Eric Tam, P.Eng., PTOE, 
Senior Manager Transportation, and Amy Choh, P.Eng., PMP, Director Engineering 
Transportation.

6.1 
DELEGATION: New Westminster & District Labour Council - Day of 
Mourning 
Speaker: Sarah Bjorknas 
 
Sarah Bjorknas, representing the New Westminster & District Labour Council, 
appeared before Council to raise awareness of the National Day of Mourning.  
The speaker advised that each year, the Labour Council seeks support and 
recognition of April 28th as a National Day of Mourning for workers killed and 
injured on the job. 
The speaker further noted that in 2024, 136 people in British Columbia died as a 
result of their work. There were 6,593 claims reported for serious injuries. The 
speaker stressed that each of these numbers represents a member, a life 
changed forever or lost to be mourned by family and friends. Impacts from work 
place injuries go far beyond time spent at work. 
The speaker highlighted recent advances in tower crane safety noting the  
mandatory crane licensing and permitting program through WorkSafeBC. 
The speaker emphasized that Unions in BC have fought hard for gains in 
ensuring appropriate WorksafeBC coverage but know that prevention is the most 
important action to be taken, noting that licensing, permitting regulations, and 
strong enforcement are proven tools for reducing risk and further noted that 
strong unions make safer places. 
S. Bjorknas stated that workers across Canada are reminding communities that 
not all hazards are visible, and psychological health and safety is occupational 
health and safety.  
*Councilor Santiago arrived at 5:08 pm 
In conclusion, the speaker expressed the importance of collective action to  
improve workplace safety, and invited City Council and members of the public to 
the National Day of Mourning event being held on Tuesday, April 28, 2026 at     
11:00 am at Westminster Pier Park in New Westminster.

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ............................................. Page 2 
 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This report presents the proposed Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 (“Zoning Bylaw 2026”) 
for Council’s consideration and advancement to First and Second Readings. If adopted, 
Zoning Bylaw 2026 will replace the City’s current Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 (“Zoning 
Bylaw 1965”). The proposed bylaw (Attachment 1) is the culmination of a multiyear, 
multi-phase process and represents the City’s first comprehensive zoning review in 60 
years, serving as a key implementation tool for the Burnaby 2050 Official Community 
Plan (OCP). New Development Permit Area Guidelines (Attachment 2, Attachment 3, 
and Attachment 4), as enabled through the Burnaby 2050 OCP, are also presented as 
schedules to the proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026. Adoption of Zoning Bylaw 2026 will 
repeal Zoning Bylaw 1965, requiring many properties citywide to be assigned to the new 
zoning districts under Zoning Bylaw 2026. 
1.0   POLICY SECTION 
The proposed amendments outlined in this report align with the following provincial and 
municipal laws, bylaws, and policies, including: 
 
• Local Government Act (2015) 
• Metro Vancouver Regional Growth Strategy (2023) 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) 
• Climate Action Framework (2020)  
• Mayor’s Task Force on Community Housing Final Report (2019) 
• Burnaby Housing Needs Report (2021; interim update 2024) 
• Economic Development Strategy (2007) 
• HOME: Burnaby’s Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021) 
• Burnaby Transportation Plan (2021) 
• Urban Forest Strategy (2025) 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025) 
2.0  
BACKGROUND 
2.1  
Zoning Bylaw Rewrite 
In late 2023, the City initiated the Zoning Bylaw Rewrite project, the first comprehensive 
review and update of the City’s Zoning Bylaw in 60 years. The objectives of the Rewrite 
included:  
• Provide more flexible and modern regulations; 
• Reduce complexity to improve ease of understanding; 
• Enhance user-friendliness for residents, applicants, and City staff; and 
• Implement and align with City plans and policies, and Provincial legislation. 
 
 
23

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ............................................. Page 3 
 
Project Phasing 
The Zoning Bylaw Rewrite was carried out as a 4-phase project structured around a 
series of major amendments to implement the new Burnaby 2050 OCP land use 
framework and address new legislation on small-scale multi-unit housing, transit-
oriented areas, and other new development requirements. 
Phase 1 of the project introduced the R1 Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing District, 
adopted by Council on June 10, 2024.  
Phase 2 introduced the next major amendment, adopted by Council on June 10, 2025, 
which introduced a new height-based development framework, including new interim 
multi-family residential and commercial districts. Related to this phase of work was the 
introduction of new Community Benefit Bonus and Inclusionary Rental requirements, 
adopted by Council on October 14, 2025. 
Phase 3 introduced five additional height-based zoning districts for employment, 
industrial, park, institutional, and agricultural land uses, which represented the 
remaining new zoning districts required to implement the Burnaby 2050 OCP land use 
framework. This major amendment was adopted by Council on March 10, 2026. 
The first three phases represented an interim state that allowed the City to continue its 
transition to the height-based framework and support early implementation of the new 
Burnaby 2050 OCP land use framework while the full Zoning Bylaw 2026 was still being 
finalized. 
Phase 4 Repeal and Replacement 
Phase 4 of the Zoning Bylaw Rewrite is intended to complete the transition to a new 
Zoning Bylaw 2026, including establishment of Development Permit Area (DPA) 
Guidelines to implement the Form and Character, Streamside Protection and 
Enhancement, and Tenant Protection DPAs designated in the Burnaby 2050 OCP. 
Phase 4, the culmination of the project as outlined in this report, introduces: 
• a new Zoning Bylaw 2026 document, with improved formatting, organization, and 
visual aids; 
• updates to the supplementary regulations of Zoning Bylaw 1965, including 
administrative and parking-related sections; 
• finalized versions of the zoning districts introduced since Phase 1; 
• new Development Permit Area (DPA) Guidelines that will form schedules to 
Zoning Bylaw 2026; and 
• a strategy for assigning new zoning districts to properties citywide that are 
currently zoned with districts that will be repealed with Zoning Bylaw 1965. 
The above-noted items would all come into effect through adoption of Zoning Bylaw 
2026, which would contain the new DPA Guidelines and an updated Official Zoning Map 
of Burnaby. The proposed effective date of the new Zoning Bylaw 2026 is July 1, 2026. 
 
 
24

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ............................................. Page 4 
 
Figure 1 – Zoning Bylaw Rewrite Project Timeline and Phasing 
 
2.2  
Implementation Approach 
On June 10, 2025, Council adopted a height-based approach to development for the 
City of Burnaby, as outlined in the Council report titled “Proposed Bylaw Amendments – 
Height-Based Development Framework,” dated April 22, 2025. This direction introduced 
new height-based zoning districts aligned with the Burnaby 2050 OCP land use 
framework. At that time, Council also directed staff to facilitate height-based 
development applications through Comprehensive Development (CD) rezonings until 
the City’s new OCP and Zoning Bylaw 2026 (which includes the DPA Guidelines) were 
adopted. 
The height-based development framework shifts the City’s regulatory approach from 
density-based controls toward built form, with a greater emphasis on building height, 
massing, and the relationship of development to the public realm. While this framework 
prioritizes physical form, land use designations within the Burnaby 2050 OCP and 
corresponding zoning districts in Zoning Bylaw 2026 will continue to establish permitted 
uses. Working together, the OCP and Zoning Bylaw 2026 (which includes the DPA 
Guidelines) provide the primary tools for implementing this framework. 
Consistent with the Development Approval Process (DAP) project, the framework is 
intended to streamline development regulations, improve transparency, and better align 
development outcomes with the Burnaby 2050 OCP land use framework. The approach 
is designed to achieve the following objectives: 
• Increase transparency and clarity regarding permitted building height and form; 
• Simplify development regulations and the approvals process; 
• Support greater flexibility for mixed-use development; 
• Enhance urban design standards and the relationship between buildings and the 
public realm; 
• Align with the Burnaby 2050 OCP and new provincial legislation related to small-
scale multi-unit housing, development financing tools, and development in 
Transit-Oriented Areas; and 
• Maintain opportunities to support affordable housing. 
 
 
25

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ............................................. Page 5 
 
To support these objectives, the framework introduces several key changes to the City’s 
development regulations, including: 
• Establishing permitted building heights within OCP land use designations and 
corresponding zoning districts; 
• Removing Floor Area Ratio (FAR) as a primary development control mechanism; 
• Introducing Form and Character DPA Guidelines as a key regulatory tool; 
• Aligning major planning policies with the new framework; 
• Reducing the number of zoning districts to improve clarity and efficiency; and 
• Enhancing opportunities for mixed-use development. 
With adoption of Zoning Bylaw 2026 (which includes the Form and Character DPA 
Guidelines) and repeal of Zoning Bylaw 1965 (which includes the former FAR-based 
zoning districts), the new land use framework will be fully implemented. 
Table 1 provides a comparative summary of the current and proposed implementation 
approach following adoption of Zoning Bylaw 2026.

CD Rezoning 
CD rezoning continues to be used to 
support mixed-use development and 
provide greater control over design 
outcomes. This includes being able 
to reference new height-based 
districts as underlying districts for CD 
sites. 
With new zoning districts that support a 
greater mix of uses (including through 
overlay zoning districts) and new DPA 
guidelines providing more design control 
and riparian area and tenant protection, 
CD rezoning is used less often. CD 
rezoning will typically be used for unique 
development proposals not contemplated 
or reasonably regulated by another 
standard zoning district, such as master 
plan sites. 
 
 
 
2.2.1  Transition for In-Progress Applications 
Preliminary Plan Approvals 
To support the completion of recently approved and active applications made under 
Zoning Bylaw 1965, and also to provide clear direction on how much time an applicant 
has to act on an approved application, and when an application must be approved 
before it will be cancelled and a new application must be submitted under Zoning Bylaw 
2026, a one year transition period is proposed for issued and valid Preliminary Plan 
Approval (PPA) as well as active PPA applications. The following transition approach is 
proposed for these situations: 
• If, prior to the Zoning Bylaw 2026 effective date (July 1, 2026), a complete PPA 
application is received, or if a PPA was issued and remains valid on July 1, 2026, 
then Zoning Bylaw 2026 will not apply to the land that is the subject of the PPA 
application or issued PPA for 12 months (until July 1, 2027) and instead Zoning 
Bylaw 1965 will apply.  
o The related building permit (BP) must be applied for within 12 months of 
the effective date of Zoning Bylaw 2026 (on or before July 1, 2027), after 
which the PPA under Zoning Bylaw 1965 will cease to be valid and the 
submission of a new application under Zoning Bylaw 2026 and the new 
DPA Guidelines will be required. 
Rezoning Applications 
The approach for the management of rezoning applications made before the adoption of 
Burnaby 2050 OCP is outlined in section 4.3 of the Council report titled “Burnaby 2050 
Official Community Plan – Final Version”, dated September 9, 2025, and is as follows: 
• If a rezoning application was made prior to the adoption of Burnaby 2050 OCP 
(Dec. 9, 2025) and it does not align with new OCP, then it would need to be 
adopted by Dec. 9, 2026, after which it would be cancelled and a new rezoning 
application will be required, which will be subject to Burnaby 2050 OCP (per 
September 2025 OCP report), and Zoning Bylaw 2026 and new DPA Guidelines. 
 
 
 
27

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement

Rezoning Bylaws for In-Progress Rezoning Applications 
As of July 1, 2026, being the effective date of Zoning Bylaw 2026 and repeal of Zoning 
Bylaw 1965, a number of amendment bylaws of Zoning Bylaw 1965 for in-progress 
rezoning applications (“in-progress rezoning bylaws”) will have received at least one 
reading but not been adopted.  Provided there are no changes to the land use 
regulations applicable to the proposed development on the lands under an in-progress 
rezoning bylaw, it is proposed that minor alterations be made to the in-progress 
rezoning bylaw as may be required to allow the bylaw to be adopted under Zoning 
Bylaw 2026 as follows: 
• For those in-progress rezoning bylaws that have received first or second reading 
as of July 1, 2026, it is proposed that minor alterations be made to the text of the 
in-progress rezoning bylaw either between first and second reading, or between 
second and third reading, as applicable, of the in-progress rezoning bylaw   
• For those in-progress rezoning bylaws that have received third reading as of 
July 1, 2026, it is proposed that Council rescind third reading of the in-progress 
rezoning bylaw and give third reading to the in-progress rezoning bylaw, as 
amended.

Section 4.1 is proposed to remain unchanged and be included in Zoning Bylaw 2026 as

Forthcoming Development Procedures Bylaw 
A subsequent report will be brought forward to Council to introduce a new Burnaby 
Development Procedures Bylaw to repeal and replace the current Development 
Procedures Bylaw 2022 (Bylaw No. 14427). Under section 460 of the Local Government 
Act, a local government that adopts an OCP or Zoning Bylaw must define procedures 
for applications to amend the OCP or Zoning Bylaw or permits under Part 14 of the 
Local Government Act (such as development permits, development variance permits, 
temporary use permits, etc.). Currently these procedures are separately set out in the 
Burnaby OCP, Zoning Bylaw 1965, and Development Procedures Bylaw 2022. It is 
proposed that these and other types of development applications be set out in a 
consolidated Development Procedures Bylaw. This consolidated approach will support 
implementation of Zoning Bylaw 2026 and the City’s updated development framework.

Committee of the Whole 
On February 23, 2026, staff presented to Committee of the Whole key Phase 4 changes 
and implementation considerations proposed as part of the final repeal and replacement 
of Zoning Bylaw 1965 with Zoning Bylaw 2026. Key changes and new regulations 
discussed, included new approaches to: 
• minimum building separations, maximum floorplate sizes, and minimum dwelling 
unit sizes; 
• minimum permeable areas and R1 District tree requirements; and 
• off-street parking, loading, and bicycle parking. 
Section 3.1.2 of this report summarizes those topics where revisions were made in 
response to Committee of the Whole feedback.

3.1  
Proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026 
3.1.1  Zoning Bylaw 2026 Document 
The proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026 will replace the City’s current Zoning Bylaw 1965 and 
earlier Zoning Bylaw Rewrite amendments with a fully consolidated and modernized 
regulatory document that introduces a clearer and more intuitive document structure. 
Improvements include enhanced formatting, a clearer hierarchy of information, and the 
increased use of visual aids such as tables and diagrams. Large blocks of text present 
in Zoning Bylaw 1965 have been reorganized into shorter sections with clearer 
headings and sub-sections to improve readability and interpretation for applicants, staff, 
and the public. 
Document Structure 
The proposed bylaw is organized into the following major Parts: 
• Part 1 Administration 
• Part 2 Definitions 
• Part 3 Zoning Districts 
• Part 4 General Regulations 
• Part 5 Rental Housing and Bonus Incentives 
• Part 6 Use-Specific Regulations 
• Part 7 Parking, Loading, and Mobility 
• Schedules: Development Permit Area Guidelines 
Part 1 Administration 
The Administration section consolidates introductory provisions formerly located 
throughout Sections 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 of Zoning Bylaw 1965, to establish the 
administrative framework for Zoning Bylaw 2026, and includes enforcement provisions, 
and interpretation rules. Part 1 also contains the new Official Zoning Map of Burnaby 
that reflects proposed zoning district reassignment as further described in section 3.3 of 
this report. 
Part 2 Definitions 
The Definitions section will be updated primarily to remove outdated terms no longer in 
use, improve clarity and consistency of existing definitions, and introduce new 
definitions required to support the updated regulatory framework and DPA Guidelines. 
Part 3 Zoning Districts 
Part 3 will contain the City’s new zoning districts, which Council previously adopted as 
new interim zoning districts in Zoning Bylaw 1965. These districts will be reformatted 
within the new Zoning Bylaw 2026 and include minor adjustments to align with the 
proposed DPA Guidelines and improve consistency with other regulatory updates.

Part 5 Rental Housing and Bonus Incentives 
The Rental Housing and Bonus Incentives section will contain the City’s inclusionary 
rental housing and community benefit bonus provisions. No changes are proposed to 
existing community benefit bonus provisions, but changes are proposed to the 
inclusionary rental requirements to incorporate provisions for rental replacement units. 
These changes were first introduced at Planning and Development Committee (PDC) in 
the report titled “Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and 
Associated Bylaw Updates,” dated March 31, 2026. 
The following requirements, previously part of Stream 1 of the Rental Use Zoning Policy 
(RUZP), are proposed to be carried forward without change: 
• returning tenants shall be offered a replacement rental unit with the same 
number of bedrooms as the unit they occupied on the eligibility date; and 
• returning tenants will pay the same rent they paid on the eligibility date, plus any 
annual increases permitted under the Residential Tenancy Act (RTA). 
However, changes are proposed in relation to second tenancy, where a TPB-eligible 
tenant does not return or later moves out of their replacement rental unit.  
Under the RUZP (Stream 1), once a replacement unit became vacant, it reverts to an 
inclusionary unit with rent set at 20% below CMHC median rents. Under the proposed 
update to inclusionary rental requirements in Zoning Bylaw 2026, once a replacement 
rental unit is no longer rented to a returning tenant, it will revert to the requirements of 
the inclusionary rental provisions in Part 5 of Zoning Bylaw 2026 instead of the RUZP. 
The proposed inclusionary rental provisions outline:  
1. Where the number of replacement rental units exceeds the required inclusionary 
obligation: 
• At second tenancy, replacement rental units shall be designated as 
inclusionary units until the required inclusionary threshold is met.  
• Any remaining replacement units beyond the required inclusionary threshold 
shall, at second tenancy, be designated as market rental units.  
2. Where the number of replacement rental units is less than the inclusionary rental 
unit obligation:  
• Additional units shall be designated as inclusionary units to achieve the 
required inclusionary rental unit threshold (as described in Option 2B in the 
financial impact evaluation included as Attachment 6).  
3. Where no inclusionary housing requirement applies to the development:  
• At second tenancy, all replacement rental units shall be designated as market 
rental units. 
Attachment 7 provides an example illustrating how inclusionary and rental replacement 
requirements would be applied at both initial occupancy and second tenancy. The 
example demonstrates how units are designated as inclusionary, replacement, or

applicable inclusionary requirement.

Temporary Use Permits, or that apply conditions to particular uses across multiple 
zoning districts, such as home occupations, child care facilities, and secondary suites. 
Part 7 Parking, Loading, and Mobility 
The Parking, Loading, and Mobility section contains and reorganizes regulations 
previously under the Off-Street Vehicle Parking, Off-Street Loading, and Off-Street 
Bicycle Parking & End of Trip Facilities schedules in Zoning Bylaw 1965. Key changes 
were discussed at Committee of the Whole on February 23, 2026, and related revisions 
are further outlined in section 3.1.2 below. 
Schedules: Development Permit Area Guidelines 
Three schedules will be appended to Zoning Bylaw 2026 containing the new DPA 
Guidelines, as further described in section 3.2 of this report. 
3.1.2  Proposed Changes in Response to Committee of the Whole Feedback 
The following summarizes key changes resulting from Committee of the Whole 
feedback on February 23, 2026. 
Building Separations 
Within Zoning Bylaw 1965, Section 6.3 contains provisions for minimum separation 
between buildings on the same lot (not including the R1 District). These provisions 
establish a wide range of separation requirements—from 1.8 m to over 30 m—
depending on factors such as building height, whether walls contain windows, and 
whether the building is residential or non-residential. For a typical windowed multi-family 
residential building, the Zoning Bylaw 1965 applies three different sets of requirements 
depending on building height and zoning district. Buildings up to three storeys require a 
minimum separation of 7.5 m, while buildings taller than three storeys are subject to a 
formula that increases separation with height. In the RM4 and RM5 Districts, buildings 
above the sixth storey must provide a fixed face-to-face tower separation of 30 m.

In practice, these provisions are complex to interpret and do not align well with 
contemporary development practices. Because the requirements vary depending on 
several factors and require calculations to determine compliance, they are frequently 
modified through Comprehensive Development (CD) rezonings to achieve appropriate 
built form outcomes. 
Zoning Bylaw 2026 introduces simplified building separations that establish a consistent 
set of baseline distances applicable across multiple zoning districts. Rather than relying 
on formulas, the new requirements apply discrete separation distances based on storey 
ranges that correspond with common building components such as podiums, towers, 
and transitional floors between these forms. These distances are based on a review of 
existing and recently approved development projects in Burnaby and other Canadian 
cities, such as Vancouver and Toronto, and are generally consistent with separation 
standards achieved through recent CD rezonings. The new provisions will apply to 
buildings located both on the same lot and on adjacent lots. 
In response to feedback from Committee of the Whole, building separations have been 
differentiated between residential and commercial buildings and are proposed to be 
increased by 3 m to 10 m for most storey ranges, with the greatest setbacks for 
residential buildings.

The updated framework maintains the objective of supporting privacy and access to 
light while allowing greater flexibility in building design. For example, podium storeys (up 
to the first six storeys) may be permitted to share a party wall with no separation where 
appropriate, reflecting conditions commonly seen in low-rise buildings and the podiums 
of taller buildings along commercial corridors. 
While Zoning Bylaw 2026 establishes clear baseline separation distances, the Form and 
Character DPA Guidelines provide additional design guidance and allow site-specific 
refinement through the development permit process. For example, proposed guidelines 
will specifically address privacy considerations through design strategies such as 
window placement, glazing treatments, screening elements, and landscape buffers. 
Minimum Permeable Lot Area 
To help advance the City’s adopted Urban Forest Strategy and OCP, new requirements 
for landscaping were proposed and discussed at Committee of the Whole. 
This included reframing existing maximum impervious surface area requirements, which 
currently only apply to the R1 District, to minimum permeable area requirements, and 
introducing new minimum permeable area requirements in all other zoning districts. 
In response to feedback from Committee of the Whole, the proposed minimum 
permeable area, as a percentage of total lot area, increased from 5% to 10% for the 
Industrial (I1) District:

For all permeability requirements, at least half the permeable area must be provided as 
vegetated area (cannot be permeable pavers or other porous hardscape). Where there 
is an overlay Zoning District, the lower requirement between the Zoning Districts will 
apply. Vegetated areas (i.e. areas planted with trees and shrubs) contribute additional 
environmental benefits including evapotranspiration, air filtration, habitat, and reduced 
energy consumption through shade; and are therefore proposed to be credited at a 
higher rating than permeable surfaces on their own. 
R1 District Tree Requirements 
Proposed minimum tree requirements for R1 District lots were previously discussed with 
Committee of the Whole. In response to Committee feedback, staff will continue to 
review these requirements alongside updates to the Tree Bylaw and other Urban Forest 
Strategy implementation measures, to be brought forward to Council at a later date. 
In the interim, an incentive-based approach is proposed for the R1 District to support 
tree retention. Areas identified as tree protection zones within an approved tree 
management plan may be credited toward the required minimum permeable lot area at 
a rate of 1.6 times the protected area. Additional details are provided in Part 4, Section 
4.3 of Attachment 1. 
Parking 
Recent parking studies (e.g. from NAIOP, Bunt & Associates, and UDI) and 
consideration of changes made to parking rates in other Metro Vancouver municipalities 
generally suggest that current minimum required parking and loading rates in Burnaby, 
particularly for non-residential uses such as commercial, employment, and industrial 
properties, are resulting in parking space oversupply. 
At the February 23, 2026, Committee of the Whole meeting, staff presented proposed 
reduced parking rates for various uses including commercial uses and office/healthcare 
facility uses for sites located within a Transit-Oriented Area (TOA) or Frequent Transit 
Network Area (FTNA).  
37

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 17 
 
Separate rates for these uses are proposed given that office and healthcare facilities will 
be permitted widely outside of commercial zones (e.g. in the E1 and P2 Districts) as 
principal uses, including outside TOAs and FTNAs. 
For Employment and Industrial uses, the Committee discussed whether reduced 
parking rates for larger sites (i.e. sites exceeding 9,290 m2) should be limited to certain 
areas, citing concerns about potential parking spill-over effects into adjacent residential 
neighbourhoods. Specifically, employment and industrial areas in the Big Bend and 
Lake City (north of Lougheed Highway) community plan areas were identified as areas 
where further reduced parking rates for larger sites could be considered given they are 
located further or otherwise more isolated from residential areas, thus generating less 
concern about potential parking spill-over effects. 
Required parking rates for civic uses were also discussed at Committee of the Whole, 
with concerns raised about not requiring minimum parking for such uses. Based on this 
discussion, staff have proposed a minimum rate of 1 space per 33.3 m2 for civic uses.

3.1.3  Other Supporting Changes 
The following summarizes supporting changes proposed to all sections of Zoning Bylaw 
2026 relative to the equivalent sections of Zoning Bylaw 1965 in support of the City’s 
new development framework and are detailed in Attachment 1. 
Part 1 Administration 
Proposed Part 1, Administration, consolidates introductory provisions formerly located in 
Sections 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 of Zoning Bylaw 1965. 
The following key changes are proposed: 
• Add transitional provisions for in-progress PPA applications to provide a 1-year 
period following the effective date of the Zoning Bylaw 2026 (proposed to be July 
1, 2026) for applications to receive final approval. 
• Remove former Zoning Bylaw 1965 purpose statement. 
• Remove provisions that will be addressed in the Burnaby Development 
Procedures Bylaw relating to administration of PPAs (being replaced by new 
Development Permit process), the Zoning Bylaw amendment process, and fees. 
• Insert the Official Zoning Map reflecting assignment of new zoning districts as 
described in section 3.3 of this report. 
• Add clarifying interpretation provisions on how numeric requirements should be 
rounded and how certain common bylaw language should be interpreted (e.g. 
when “including” is used in a provision). 
Part 2 Definitions 
The Definitions section of Zoning Bylaw 2026 has been streamlined and modernized, 
reducing the total number of defined terms while improving clarity and flexibility. A total 
of 30 new definitions have been introduced to support emerging policy areas, including 
environmental protection, tenant protection, and the City’s updated development 
framework. 
At the same time, 64 legacy definitions—primarily highly specific or outdated land use 
categories—have been removed or consolidated into broader use classifications. Many 
retained definitions have also been refined to improve clarity, consistency, and 
alignment with contemporary planning practices.

3.2  
Proposed Development Permit Area Guidelines 
Three sets of DPA Guidelines, as enabled by the Burnaby 2050 OCP, are proposed to 
form schedules of Zoning Bylaw 2026: 
• Schedule I: Form and Character Development Permit Area Guidelines 
(Attachment 2); 
• Schedule II: Streamside Protection and Enhancement Development Permit Area 
Guidelines (Attachment 3); 
• Schedule III: Tenant Protection Development Permit Area Guidelines 
(Attachment 4). 
DPA Guidelines for Tenant Protection were developed in parallel with a separate, 
dedicated Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw, which will be introduced to Council in a 
separate report. 
 
 
46

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 26 
 
3.2.1  Form and Character Development Permit Area Guidelines 
The Form and Character DPA Guidelines (Attachment 2) provide comprehensive 
design guidance across a range of topics, including building siting and orientation, 
architectural form and character, landscape and site design, site access, and the 
interface with the public realm. 
Unlike the mandatory regulations contained in the Zoning Bylaw, the Guidelines are 
discretionary and allow for multiple design approaches to achieve their intended 
outcomes. They establish a framework to support consistent, high-quality urban design 
while recognizing the diversity of development contexts and allowing flexibility to 
respond to site-specific conditions. 
During the development review process, applicants will be provided with the applicable 
guidelines, and staff will assess proposals based on how well they meet the objectives 
and intent of those guidelines. 
Overall, the Guidelines are intended to promote a consistent standard of urban design 
across Burnaby, build on established best practices, and work in conjunction with the 
Zoning Bylaw and other development policies to support the creation of well-designed 
buildings and public spaces that contribute to the Burnaby 2050 OCP vision. 
3.2.2  Streamside Protection and Enhancement Development Permit Area 
Guidelines 
The Streamside Protection and Enhancement (SPEA) DPA Guidelines (Attachment 3) 
are designed to protect and enhance Burnaby’s aquatic habitats and riparian 
areas where land is developed. The Guidelines build on the successful outcomes of 
Burnaby’s existing approach for SPEA, in place since the late 1990s, and which 
has provided a standard of riparian protection comparable to or exceeding the 
Provincial Riparian Areas Protection Regulation.    
Development of the SPEA Guidelines provided an opportunity to enhance Burnaby’s 
approach for streamside protection in response to contemporary community 
development. Specific guidelines are proposed to address streamside protection on 
constrained lots in the R1 District and for parcels of land within Transit Oriented Areas, 
to ensure tree protection and preservation, and to provide a standard of riparian 
protection for constructed watercourses with significant habitat value. Overall, the 
guidelines help determine the size and extent of the SPEA for a specific parcel, how the 
SPEA and the stream are protected during construction, and how the SPEA is 
enhanced as a condition of development, for example by removing invasive plants and 
by planting native trees and shrubs.    
Where development is proposed on lands adjacent to streams and riparian areas, a 
Streamside Protection and Enhancement Development Permit (Streamside DP) is 
required for subdivision, construction of buildings and structures, and site alteration 
activities such as vegetation clearing, grading, and landscaping changes. As part of the 
application process, applicants are required to retain qualified environmental 
professionals to undertake site assessments and prepare supporting documentation 
47

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 27 
 
demonstrating compliance with the streamside protection requirements set out in Part 4 
(General Regulations) of the Zoning Bylaw and the SPEA DPA Guidelines.    
3.2.3  Tenant Protection Development Permit Area Guidelines 
The Tenant Protection DPA Guidelines (Attachment 4) establish how the City manages 
and evaluates Tenant Protection Development Permit (TPDP) applications. The TPDP, 
together with proposed amendments to the Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw (to 
be brought forward in a separate report), sets out requirements for owners of applicable 
rental buildings—primarily purpose-built rental housing—before, during, and after 
redevelopment to ensure tenants receive the information, support, and benefits required 
under the proposed Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw (TPB). Exemptions from these 
requirements are defined in the TPB. 
The Guidelines identify when a TPDP is required, when exemptions may apply, and the 
submission requirements for obtaining a permit. Within the designated area, activities 
such as demolition, subdivision, or significant building alterations cannot proceed until a 
TPDP has been issued. 
Following permit issuance, property owners must continue to meet all obligations under 
the TPB, including providing required benefits to eligible households and caretakers, 
and maintaining ongoing communication with both the City and affected tenants 
throughout the redevelopment process. Where an owner fails to comply with TPDP 
conditions, the City may revoke the permit and issue a stop work order under the 
Burnaby Building Bylaw until compliance is achieved.
