SUBJECT: 
DVP #26-01 – 8012 11TH AVENUE – RETAINING WALL HEIGHT 
VARIANCES  
 
PURPOSE: 
To seek Council approval for Development Variance Permit (DVP) 
#26-01. 
 
REFERENCES 
 
Address:  
 
8012 11th Avenue  
The Common Property, Strata Plan EPS11316 
Applicant:  
 
Bhogal Design Services  
 
 
 
7270 15th Avenue 
 
 
 
Burnaby, BC V3N 4Z7 
 
 
 
Attention: Sunny (Varinder Singh) Bhogal, on behalf of 
 
 
 
HBS Custom Homes Ltd. (Registered Owner) 
Current Zoning:  
R1 – Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing  
 
RECOMMENDATION 
 
THAT the issuance of Development Variance Permit #26-01, included as 
Attachment 2 in the report titled “DVP #26-01 – 8012 11th Avenue – Retaining 
Wall Height Variances” dated April 28, 2026, be approved; and 
 
THAT the City Solicitor be directed to register notice of DVP #26-01 with the 
Land Title Office. 
 
1.0  POLICY  
 
The subject Development Variance Permit (DVP) aligns with the following policies and 
plans adopted by Council:  
 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022),  
• Regional Context Statement (2013),  
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025),  
• Economic Development Strategy (2007),  
• Social Sustainability Strategy (2011), and 
• Environmental Sustainability Strategy (2016).  
 
324

Subject: DVP #26-01 – 8012 11th Avenue – Retaining Walls Height Variances 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
Under Section 498 of the Local Government Act (LGA), a local government may by 
Council resolution, issue a DVP to vary specific provisions of the Zoning Bylaw. 
Pursuant to Section 498.1 of the LGA and Section 4A.2 of the Burnaby Development 
Procedures Bylaw, Council delegated to the General Manager Planning and 
Development the authority to issue DVPs in respect to minor variances that meet the 
criteria set out in Section 4A.1 of the Development Procedures Bylaw. However, as the 
requested variance in the subject DVP does not meet the criteria for a minor DVP, 
Council approval is required. The public notification process for the subject DVP 
application, as outlined in Section 4.0, has been completed. 
 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
 
2.1 
The subject development site is located within the East Burnaby Community Plan 
Area along 11th Avenue, between 4th Street and 2nd Street (see Attachment 1). 
The site is zoned R1 – Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing (SSMUH) and designated 
SSMUH within the Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (OCP). The subject site 
is improved with two primary dwelling units which have been stratified to create 
two strata lots. All adjacent land uses are also zoned and designated for SSMUH.  
 
2.2  The subject site is associated with Building Permit #23-01057, to permit two 
primary dwelling units on the lot. The Building Permit was issued in March 2024, 
prior to adoption of the R1 Zoning District in June 2024, and subsequent 
amendments to the R1 Zoning District in October 2025. 
  
2.3 
The applicant is requesting a DVP to vary the height of the retaining walls 
constructed on the east and west sides of the property. 
 
During an inspection by the Building Department, it was noted that the retaining 
walls are over-height on both the east and west sides of the subject site and that 
they encroach onto the adjacent properties. A site survey (see Attachment 2) 
shows the extent of the encroachments. If Council approves this DVP, the 
owner/strata corporation of the subject site will be required to secure easement 
agreements with the owners of the adjacent properties to the east and west of the 
subject site to permit these encroachments.  
 
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
 
3.1 
The applicant is seeking to vary Section 6.14.1 of the Zoning Bylaw, which permits 
a maximum retaining wall height of 1.2 m. The eastern retaining wall is 2.43 m in 
height at its tallest point, representing a variance of 102.5%, and is 36.12 m in 
length. The western retaining wall is 2.06 m in height at its tallest point, 
representing a variance of 71.7%, and is 33.05 m in length. Table 1 identifies the 
requested variances to the applicable sections of the Zoning Bylaw. 
 
 
 
325

Subject: DVP #26-01 – 8012 11th Avenue – Retaining Walls Height Variances 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
Table 1: Requested Variances  
Bylaw Section 
Bylaw Requirement Requested Variance 
6.14.1 Retaining Wall Height Maximum height of  
1.2 m 
Maximum height of up  
to 2.43 m on the eastern  
side of the site and up to  
2.06 m on the western  
side of the site  
 
3.2 
The intent of the Zoning Bylaw requirements for limiting retaining wall height is to 
mitigate the visual impact of the building and retaining walls on the neighbouring 
properties. The supportive rationale for these variances are as follows: 
 
• Given that the site is situated at a lower elevation than the adjacent 
properties, the visual impact of the proposed retaining walls on neighbouring 
sites is minimal.  
• The retaining walls enhance slope stability and help mitigate erosion, 
contributing to the long-term safety and sustainability of the site.  
 
3.3 
As a condition of the issuance of the DVP, the applicant will be required to enter 
into an easement and Section 219 Covenant agreement, with each of the 
adjacent property owners to permit the construction, maintenance, repair and 
replacement of the portion of the retaining wall which encroaches onto each of the 
adjacent properties. The applicant has confirmed that both neighbouring 
properties have verbally agreed to the easement and covenants in principle.  
 
The applicant has also submitted a signed and sealed letter from a registered 
Professional Engineer confirming the structural stability of the retaining walls on 
both sides of the property, specifying the capacity of the full height of the walls as 
constructed.  
 
3.4 
For the reasons outlined above, staff support the variance requests to permit the 
over-height retaining walls. The required permit for the requested variances is 
attached as Attachment 2 to this report. If Council approves the issuance of this 
DVP, notice of the DVP will be deposited in the Land Title Office and registered on 
the subject property’s title. 
 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
 
In accordance with the requirements set out in the Local Government Act (LGA) and the 
Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw, public notice of the subject DVP application 
was delivered to adjacent property owners and occupants (within 30 m of the subject 
property) at least ten (10) days before Council considers the application for approval. A 
sign was also posted along 11th Avenue.

FROM:

SUBJECT: 
2026 INSURANCE PROGRAM – CONTRACT RENEWALS 
 
PURPOSE: 
To request approval to award insurance contracts for the annual property 
and liability insurance programs. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the total amount of $4,013,855 payable to Aon Reed Stenhouse Inc. for 
insurance premiums for all twelve insurance contracts, as outlined in the report 
titled “2026 Insurance Program – Contract Renewals” dated for April 28, 2026, be 
approved. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
Aon Reed Stenhouse, an international brokerage firm, was awarded a two-year contract 
with three one-year contract extensions through a Request for Proposals for insurance 
brokerage services in 2025. The City is currently in the first year of a two-year contract 
that was signed in October 2025. Working closely with Aon, the Risk Management 
Division of the Community Safety Department manages the process of marketing, 
selecting, and arranging insurance coverage for the City. In consideration of an annual 
fee, Aon is providing a wide range of professional services that include advice and 
negotiation of coverage and policy wording; marketing of the City’s risk in the global 
insurance market; binding coverage and paying premium on behalf of the City; and, as 
necessary, acting as a claims advocate with insurers. City insurance programs renew 
annually on May 1. This year’s insurance renewal shows a 4% (approximately $180,000) 
decrease in overall premium compared to the prior term. 
 
The cost of maintaining insurance programs is a necessary operating expense and is 
included in the City’s 2026 – 2030 Financial Plan. Insurance charges are exempt from 
PST and GST. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
Not applicable. 
 
 
 
 
 
337

Subject: 2026 Insurance Program – Contract Renewals 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
2.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
2.1 Insurance Policies 
• Property Insurance 
The City’s property assets are appraised annually by Universal Appraisal Services. 
The total insured value is currently reported at $2.36 billion.  Changes in the total 
insured value from year to year are attributable to new facilities that have been 
constructed or acquired during the past year; the ever-rising cost of replacement 
construction due to inflation; and the accumulating building code and bylaw update 
requirements for replacement construction. QBE Insurance Group Limited (QBE) 
and Allianz SE (Allianz) lead a group of subscribed insurers and were selected as 
their rates were the most competitive. Both QBE and Allianz remain very strong 
insurers with an “A” or better credit rating and have sufficient capacity to underwrite 
this program. The final renewal terms include a premium decrease of 15%. The 
premium for the Property Policy (Contract #1) is $3,100,000. 
• Main Equipment Breakdown 
Main Equipment Breakdown insurance coverage for major property assets is 
purchased from Intact. The final renewal terms include a premium increase of 24% 
which is due to the increase to the total insured value and limit increase. Premium 
for the Main Equipment Breakdown (Contract #2) is $51,849. 
• Parametric 
Parametric insurance coverage for all property assets, inclusive of buildings, road 
and sidewalk infrastructure and underground infrastructure is purchased from 
Swiss Re Ltd. and is providing $5 million limit for natural catastrophes such as 
earthquakes, floods and extreme temperatures. This coverage will provide the City 
with an instant funding source in responding to the needs of the City during a 
catastrophic event as the claims process is settled fast with coverage confirmed in 
days and funds arrive within weeks. The coverage is also flexible in that it covers 
City owned property that is not covered through traditional property coverage. This 
includes roads, sidewalks, and water and sanitation infrastructure. With the 
uncertainty of climate change and its effects on the weather, this coverage 
provides the City with an efficient and flexible risk transfer solution. The renewal 
terms include a 14% discount due to rate decreases and locking in on a 3-year 
term. Premium for the Parametric Policy (Contract #3) is $175,000. 
• Fine Arts 
Fine Arts insurance coverage for all art assets is purchased from Lloyd’s of London 
and is providing $15.2 million limit. This coverage includes all fine art, inclusive of 
the Carousel, Wurlitzer Band Organ, Antique cars, Old Curly Locomotive and the 
Tram at Burnaby Village Museum. This coverage traditionally fell within the main 
property policy. By purchasing a standalone policy, the City recognizes modest 
savings and is provided specialized coverage with no deductible. The final renewal 
338

Subject: 2026 Insurance Program – Contract Renewals 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
terms include a premium increase of 26% which is solely due to the increase to 
the total insured value. Premium for the Fine Arts Policy (Contract #4) is $11,932. 
• Commercial General and Excess Liability 
This Commercial General and Excess liability coverages are provided by Lloyd’s 
of London for all three liability policies. Lloyd’s of London is providing $10 million 
limit for Primary General Liability (Contract #5), $45 million limit for 1st 
Excess/Umbrella Liability (Contract #6) and $15 million limit for 2nd 
Excess/Umbrella Liability (Contract #7). The renewal terms for the Commercial 
General are 12% higher than expiring due to population increase. Excess Liability 
are provided on the same basis as per the expiring policy. The premium total for 
these three contracts is $420,232. 
• Network Security and Privacy Liability  
The Network Security and Privacy Liability coverage for data assets is purchased 
from American International Group Inc. (AIG) and includes a maximum limit 
amount of $7,000,000. The average data breach loss in Canada was $6.7 million 
in 2025. The renewal terms are provided on the same basis as per the expiring 
policy.  The premium for the Network Security and Privacy Liability (Contract #8).  
is $210,800. 
• Other Programs 
Aon also marketed several other smaller insurance programs, including the Crime 
and Fidelity policy with The Travelers Indemnity Company (Travelers) (Contract 
#9); a minor Equipment Breakdown policy that applies to commercial properties at 
Deer Lake I and II, the Hart House Restaurant, and Brentwood Community 
Resource Centre with Intact (Contract #10);  a minor General Liability (commercial) 
policy specific to the Deer Lake I and II operations placed with Aviva Canada Inc. 
(Contract #11); and an Accidental Death & Dismemberment policy which covers 
Mayor, Council and Volunteers and is placed by Industrial Alliance Financial Group 
(Contract #12). There is a premium increase of 8.5% inclusive of all 4 coverages, 
due to the increase in property value and volunteer numbers.  The total premium 
for these programs is $44,042.

SUBJECT: 
2026 HERITAGE BC CONFERENCE 
 
PURPOSE: 
To seek Council approval for members of the Community Heritage 
Commission to attend the 2026 Annual Heritage BC Conference in 
Squamish, BC from May 4-6, 2026. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT an expenditure of up to $4,000 for up to two (2) Community Heritage 
Commission members to attend the 2026 Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, 
BC from May 4-6, 2026, be approved. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
This report for funding expenditure approval, to support Community Heritage 
Commission members attendance at the 2026 Annual Heritage BC Conference, aligns 
with the Corporate Strategic Plan (2022).  
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The 2026 Annual Heritage BC Conference explores how collective action, industrial 
histories, and cultural teachings shape what heritage means today. By highlighting the 
power of people and the continuity of place, the conference frames heritage as an 
ongoing responsibility: to honour stories, nurture environments, and act together for a 
future rooted in care and connection.  
Historically, staff and Community Heritage Commission members have taken part in the 
annual conference to connect, learn and discuss the future of heritage.  
Community Heritage Commission members were provided with information on the 
Annual Heritage Conference. Two (2) members have expressed an interest in taking 
part in the conference.  
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
This report is appearing before Council to authorize an expenditure of up to $4,000 for 
up to two (2) Community Heritage Commission members to attend the 2026 Heritage 
BC Conference.  
Following the conference, the participating Commissioners will prepare and present a 
report to the Community Heritage Commission outlining key learnings, outcomes, and

the City.

This recommendation is to approve a contract award in the amount of $1,962,600

The City’s intent is to award this work to one Contractor to support cost efficiencies

This contract award is one of over twenty-five future awards anticipated for the fit-out of

fixtures, and equipment will be procured separately in accordance with the City’s contract

TO: 
MAYOR & COUNCILLORS 
 
FROM: 
DEPUTY GENERAL MANAGER FINANCE  
RESPONSIBLE 
DEPARTMENT 
FINANCE  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
CC - EXTERNAL AUDIT SERVICES 
 
PURPOSE: 
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) for External Audit 
Services. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT a contract change to KPMG LLP for an estimated cost of $47,000 including taxes, as 
outlined in the report titled “CC - External Audit Services” dated April 28, 2026, be approved; 
and 
THAT final payment will be based on actual quantity of services delivered and hourly rates as 
submitted. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This recommendation is to approve a contract change in the amount of $47,000 including tax. Further 
details are outlined in Attachment 1. 
This contract change is required to continue supporting External Audit Services for the City and is 
primarily a result of numerous changes to public sector accounting standards over the course of 
the original five year contract term. 
KPMG LLP provides comprehensive audit and professional assurance services that ensure the City 
maintains rigorous compliance with Public Sector Accounting Standards (PSAS) while fostering long-
term fiscal resilience. By prioritizing engagement continuity, KPMG minimizes operational disruption 
and leverages deep institutional knowledge to provide proactive guidance on emerging provincial 
regulations, evolving reporting mandates, and the shifting landscape of municipal governance in 
British Columbia. 
The City’s internal financial resources can address some of the related requirements; however, they 
cannot eliminate the need to rely on external audit service providers. This is because external audit 
standards, as governed by applicable provincial and national auditing and accounting regulations, 
do not permit organizations to conduct independent audits of their own financial statements or 
internal controls. As a result, the City must continue to engage qualified external auditors to ensure 
compliance, objectivity, and adherence to mandated audit and assurance standards. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The City’s Change Order Policy requires Council approval for change orders, which individually or 
when accumulated with prior change orders, increases the total value of the contract by $500,000. 
 
 
349

Subject: CC - External Audit Services 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ............................................ Page 2 
 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Following the Request for Proposal (RFP) 116-04-21 in April 2021, the City awarded a 5-year 
contract to KPMG LLP, for an estimated cost of $442,995 including tax for the period of September 
14, 2021, to September 13, 2026, with no options to renew.  
Since the award of this contract, there have been three contract changes totaling $51,529 (including 
taxes). Additional details can be found in Attachment 1 – Contract History. The previous contract 
changes, as well as the current requested contract change, are primarily related to changes in 
accounting standard and the need to maintain the City’s compliance with mandatory external audit 
standards and financial reporting requirements. The additional work performed by KPMG arising 
from these accounting standard changes (e.g. PS3280 Asset Retirement Obligations, PS3400 
Revenue, CAS315 Identifying & Assessing the Risks of Material Misstatement) was not included in 
the original contract, as it is difficult to predict when new accounting standards would be officially 
introduced and come into effect along with associated audit field work requirements. The scope of 
work supported by these contract changes was essential to maintaining the independence, 
objectivity, and thoroughness required under provincial and national auditing regulations, which 
prohibit organizations from performing their own external audit functions.  
The City has been working with KPMG LLP., for the supply of these services for more than 10 years. 
As the existing contract is set to expire in September 2026, staff have begun the procurement 
process to award a new contract for external audit services. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
The estimated total contract value is $541,524 including tax. 
City staff have reviewed the change and believe it appropriately addresses the City’s needs at a fair 
cost. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
RFP #116-04-21 External Audit Services was publicly advertised. Upon closing, the City received a 
total of two submissions. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Funding for this operating work is included in the 2026-2030 Financial Plan under the Finance

SUBJECT: 
CC – BURNABY LAKE RECREATION COMPLEX 
GEOTECHNICAL CONSULTING SERVICES 
 
PURPOSE: 
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) for 
geotechnical consultant services for the Burnaby Lake Recreation 
Complex project. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT a contract change to WSP Canada Inc. for an estimated cost of $384,893 
including taxes, as outlined in the report titled “CC – Burnaby Lake Recreation 
Complex Geotechnical Consulting Services” dated April 28, 2026, be approved; 
THAT a contingency for an estimated amount of $73,500 including tax be 
approved; and 
THAT final payment will be based on the actual quantity of goods and services 
delivered and hourly rates submitted. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This recommendation is to approve a contract change in the amount of $384,893 
including tax and contingency in the amount of $73,500. Further details are outlined in 
Attachment 1. 
WSP Canada Inc. has been providing Geotechnical Consulting service on the Burnaby 
Lake Recreation Centre project since 2019. Their current scope of work includes 
geotechnical design, testing, work plan, and construction stage inspection and 
administration.  
This contract change is required as the project has progressed from onsite to offsite 
excavation, unforeseen poor ground conditions have been encountered, along with 
additional geotechnical design requirements. To address these conditions, additional 
consulting services are required to develop appropriate design solutions. These services 
will ensure the selected approaches respond to the identified ground conditions, comply 
with Engineering Department requirements, and provide best value to the City.   
Contingency is required as the unknown nature for this contract change requires that the 
City be prepared to quickly address any additional unforeseen ground conditions. 
352

Subject: CC – Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex Geotechnical Consulting Services  
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The City’s Change Order Policy requires Council approval for change orders, which 
individually or when accumulated with prior change orders, increases the total value of 
the contract by $500,000. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Following a Request for Proposal (RFP) #193-10-18 Geotechnical Services for Proposed 
Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex, the City received ten proposals by the closing time 
on November 15, 2018. Proposals were evaluated based on technical submission, 
pricing, value-added services, and qualifications.  
Since the award of the contract, there have been six contract changes totaling $645,035 
including tax.  These previous change orders were required due to further hydro 
geological investigation, supplemental geotechnical investigations, ground water and 
infiltration testing, additional seismic testing to facilitate seismic design change, and 
design and construction administration. 
Golder Associates Ltd., now part of WSP Canada Inc. received the highest overall score.  
The City has worked with WSP Canada Inc. for more than 15 years. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
The estimated total contract value is $1,103,428 including taxes. 
City staff have reviewed the change and believe it appropriately addresses the City’s 
needs at a fair cost. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
RFP #193-10-18 Geotechnical Services for Proposed Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex 
was publicly advertised. Upon closing, the City received a total of ten submissions.

5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Funding for this capital work is included in the 2026 – 2030 Financial Plan under Lands 
& Facilities – Civic Projects, Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre with a remaining total 5 
year plan of $161,910,000. This contract award will be accommodated under WBS 
number: BAX.0017 ($436,600 excluding tax). 
This project is funded by the Community Benefit Bonus Reserve, which is established to 
finance community amenities through developer contributions in exchange for increased 
density.

Ratan Grewal, Deputy General Manager Finance

An application was received from Revive Hope Africa Society seeking an operating   
grant to offset costs incurred in 2026. This grant will support professional services, 
office supplies, software and subscriptions as well as repairs and maintenance to 
required equipment. Revive Hope Africa provides housing support, employment 
guidance and cultural orientation to newcomers and is located on Macpherson Avenue 
in Burnaby.  The applicant notes that the key area of impact from the 2025 grant was 
the ability of the organization to expand youth engagement advising that 231 Burnaby 
residents were positively impacted. It is noted that several of their events take place in 
Vancouver but the number of Burnaby residents served is high.

requesting an Operating Grant to offset operating costs in 2026. The grant will support 
staffing costs, rent, insurance, and supplies that allow programs to run effectively. The 
grant would be used from June 2026 – June 2027 to contribute to the operations of the 
Society.

COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE (FMC) 
 
FROM: 
ACTING CHIEF FINANCIAL OFFICER 
 
 
SUBJECT: 
BURNABY CONSOLIDATED FEES AND CHARGES BYLAW - 2026 
MID-YEAR AMENDMENTS 
 
PURPOSE: 
To propose mid-year 2026 fee amendments to certain fee schedules 
within the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the proposed Parks, Recreation and Culture fee updates, as described in 
Sections 4.1 and 4.2, and as outlined in Attachments 1 and 2, of the report titled 
“Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw – 2026 Mid-Year Amendments” 
dated April 21, 2026, be approved;  
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a bylaw to amend the 
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, to amend Schedule F1 as 
described in Section 4.1 of the report, and substantially as set out in Attachment 1 
to the report, effective June 1, 2026; and 
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a bylaw to amend the 
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, to repeal and replace 
Schedule F3, substantially as set out in Attachment 2 to the report, effective 
September 1, 2026. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw (“Fees and Charges Bylaw”) 
provides the means by which most City of Burnaby fees and charges can be held under 
one document and managed in a timely and consistent manner. Fees and charges are 
imposed pursuant to Part 7, Division 2 – Fees, of the Community Charter, and section 462 
of the Local Government Act.\ 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
380

Subject: Burnaby Consolidated Fees And Charges Bylaw - 2026 Mid Year Amendments  
Open Committee Name Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 2 
 
Fees and charges are updated for all departments on an annual basis, however, due to 
the varied nature of municipal services and commencement date of key programs and 
initiatives, amendments are presented to Council at least three times per year. This report 
presents Parks, Recreation and Culture (PRC) fee amendments to Schedule F1 effective 
June 1, 2026, and Schedule F3 effective  September 1, 2026. Further cross departmental 
fee amendments will be presented in September 2026 for other  fees, effective January 
1, 2027, and then again in November 2026 for annual utility rates, also effective January 
1, 2027. Where necessary, additional amendments to the Fees and Charges Bylaw may 
be presented to Council at other time of the year.  
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1 Benchmark Rate Increase 
Staff are using a five-year average BC Headline rate for CPI to establish the 
benchmark general rate increase. The current benchmark general rate increase (as 
of March 13, 2026) for the mid-year update is 3.53%.  
3.2 Rounding 
To better reflect the benchmark rate increase, and to avoid sharp increases in user 
fees, the rounding rules for PRC are being revised. Going forward, all fees are now to 
be rounded up to the nearest 5 cents.  
4.0 KEY UPDATES 
4.1 Schedule F1 - Food Services 
To reflect the expanding nature of hospitality services provided by the City of Burnaby 
the following amendments are proposed for Schedule F1 to the Fees and Charges 
Bylaw, effective June 1, 2026:  
• Schedule F1 which is currently titled "Food Services" is proposed to be renamed 
"Burnaby Hospitality" 
 
• In addition to including fees and charges for Food Services, it is proposed that 
Schedule F1 include fees and charges for Hotel Burnaby, with fees and charges 
for room rates and related charges for Hotel Burnaby to be set as follows:  
Hotel Rates and Services 
Room rates and related charges will be determined in accordance with industry 
practice and be in line with market acceptance.  Room rates may include guest access 
to designated drop-in activities, such as gym and pool access, that would be available 
to the holder of a Be Active Pass. 
Food Services fees and charges will be updated in September for January 1, 2027.  
4.2 Schedule F3 - Recreation and Culture 
It is proposed that Schedule F3 [Recreation and Culture] of the Fees and Charges 
Bylaw be repealed and replaced with a new Schedule F3, substantially in the form in 
381

Subject: Burnaby Consolidated Fees And Charges Bylaw - 2026 Mid Year Amendments  
Open Committee Name Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 3 
 
Attachment 2, effective September 1, 2026.  The key changes to the fees and charges 
in Schedule F3 are summarized at the beginning of Attachment 2.  
5.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Where necessary, impacted departments will inform their customers of the rate changes 
as outlined in this report. An updated consolidated version of the Fees and Charges Bylaw 
will be made available on the city website once adopted. 
 
6.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Finance will support Parks, Recreation and Culture in accounting for the proposed fee 
amendments as outlined in this report. While changes are not significant, effort is required 
in order to update the city's recreation system and related processes.  
 
Respectfully submitted, 
Richard Rowley, Director Finance - Revenue Services

Hotel Rates and Services 
Room rates and related charges will be determined in accordance with industry 
practice and be in line with market acceptance.  Room rates may include guest 
access to designated drop-in activities, such as gym and pool access, that would be 
available to the holder of a Be Active Pass.

SUMMARY OF KEY CHANGES 
 
CHANGES FOR SCHEDULE F3 – RECREATION AND CULTURE 
Key changes are summarized below in the order by which they appear in the bylaw. Wording changes 
are highlighted in red within the schedules: 
 
ADMISSIONS & PASSES 
• 
Summer Fun Pass 
Valid June 29, 2027 – September 7, 2027 - the pass is being held at $20 to encourage continued 
participation. 
 
• 
Indoor/Outdoor Pool & Arena Admissions 
Given the popularity of both the Loonie swim and Toonie skate per person programs, rates are  being 
held at their current values. 
 
• 
Miscellaneous Fees 
In order to ensure that any barriers to entry are minimized, the following rates are being held at their 
current value: 
• 
Shower fee 
 
 
 
 
• 
Racquets and table tennis paddles  
 
OUTDOOR FACILITY RESERVATIONS 
• 
Sports Development Camps 
Currently, community fees do not apply to sports development camps. New fees are now proposed to 
account for Burnaby-based youth sport. 
 
• 
Picnic Reservations  
Removal of Commercial, Private, and Community Fees for Picnic Reservations: as these were 
inadvertently added to the bylaw in error last year. 
 
SWANGARD STADIUM 
• 
Miscellaneous Fees 
Removal of Commercial, Private, and Community Fees for Swangard Miscellaneous Fees, as the 
fees are the same across the different groups. 
 
OUTDOOR SPECIAL EVENTS 
• 
Reservations 
Fees for Wallyball are now included under a combined rate for Racquetball / Wallyball / Squash 
Court.

RESERVATIONS 
Commercial: Any individual, company, group or organization engaged in the pursuit of business for 
profit. 
Private: Any individual, company, group or organization that does not meet the commercial, general or 
community definition. 
General: Registered societies, not-for-profit community groups or informal community groups whose 
participants/members are 60% or less Burnaby residents. 
Community: Registered societies, not-for-profit community groups or informal community groups 
whose participants/members are more than 60% Burnaby residents. 
 
Adult: 19 years and older 
Youth: 18 years and younger

Meeting March 31, 2026

COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT COMMITTEE (PDC) 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT 
SUBJECT: 
PROPOSED TENANT PROTECTION FRAMEWORK AND TENANT 
PROTECTION BYLAW AND ASSOCIATED BYLAW AMENDMENTS 
PURPOSE: 
To seek Council endorsement of the proposed tenant protection 
framework and Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw and associated bylaw 
amendments. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the proposed framework for tenant protection described in the report titled 
“Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and 
Associated Bylaw Amendments,” dated March 31, 2026, be endorsed;  
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward Burnaby Tenant Protection 
Bylaw 2026, in the form substantially set out in Attachment 1 to the report; 
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward amendments to the Burnaby 
Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, substantially as set out in Attachment 3 to 
the report; and 
THAT the application of the Tenant Assistance Policy and Rental Use Zoning Policy 
following the effective date of the Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw, as set out in 
section 5.0 of the report, be approved. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
The Tenant Assistance Policy (TAP) supports tenants who are displaced by development 
projects. The proposed Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 (TPB) is intended to replace 
TAP and also establish an enforcement framework and expand tenant protection 
requirements to developments that do not require rezoning. This report describes the 
proposed new tenant protection framework, introduces the Burnaby TPB and outlines the 
key differences between the TAP and the TPB. It also explains how the TPB will work with 
Tenant Protection Development Permits, the forthcoming new Burnaby Development 
Procedures Bylaw 2026, as well as the proposed changes to the inclusionary rental 
requirements in the forthcoming Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 to include rental replacement 
unit regulations, which together establish the framework to support tenants displaced by 
redevelopment. Finally, this report outlines the proposed accompanying and consequential 
amendments to Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009.

Subject: Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and Associated Bylaw 
Amendments 
PDC Meeting March 31, 2026 ………………………Page 2 
 
1.0   POLICY SECTION 
The following report aligns with provincial and municipal laws, bylaws and policies including: 
• 
Community Charter (2003); 
• 
Local Government Act (2015); 
• 
Mayor's Task Force on Community Housing Final Report (2019);  
• 
HOME: Burnaby’s Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021); 
• 
Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025); and 
• 
Draft Burnaby Zoning Bylaw (2026). 
Pursuant to Sections 8(3), 63, and 63.1 to 63.3 of the Community Charter, a council may 
enact a tenant protection bylaw to require the owner of a proposed redevelopment to 
provide tenants who are displaced by their redevelopment with certain types of benefits and 
protections.  
Pursuant to Sections 488(1)(k) and 491(11) of the Local Government Act, a council may 
designate a Development Permit Area (DPA) to mitigate the effects of displacement on 
tenants impacted by redevelopment, which is defined to mean demolition of a building that 
contains a rental unit or partial demolition such that one or more rental units are completely 
and irreversibly destroyed.  
2.0  BACKGROUND 
Burnaby’s Tenant Assistance Policy (TAP) was introduced in 2015 to support tenants who 
are displaced by redevelopment projects. The TAP has been revised several times between 
2015 and 2025 to respond to challenges faced by tenants. A timeline of the policy and its 
evolution is given in Table 1. 
Table 1: Timeline of the TAP  
Date

the current TAP framework, which provides: 
• 
help finding a new place to rent;  
• 
financial compensation;  
• 
moving compensation; and 
• 
the Right of First Refusal (ROFR) to a replacement rental unit. 
At the same meeting, Council adopts the Rental Use Zoning Policy 
(RUZP). This includes a Rental Replacement Stream (Stream 1) to 
renew the stock of rental housing and provide an opportunity for 
displaced tenants to move back into the redeveloped building at the

2.1  TAP Status 
There have been 34 development projects subject to TAP since the current framework was

number of replacement units provided, as of December 2025.

The TAP provides short-term benefits to tenants displaced by redevelopment, while the 
TAP and RUZP work together to provide long-term rental replacement unit within the new 
development for displaced tenants. Burnaby is now seeing tangible results from these 
policies. On average, redevelopments subject to the TAP and the RUZP take approximately 
7 to 10 years to complete. In 2024 and 2025, four projects completed construction and 
tenants moved into their replacement units. It is anticipated that an additional three projects

3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
The proposed Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 (TPB) addresses two gaps in the 
current TAP:   
3.1  TAP Only Applies to Redevelopments that Require Rezoning  
Currently, any redevelopment of purpose-built rental units that proceeds without a rezoning 
is not subject to TAP. In these cases, the TAP would not apply and any tenant supports 
would be voluntary actions by the owner.  
3.2  Limited Enforcement Capability Under TAP  
The City currently monitors and enforces TAP compliance at several milestones throughout 
the development approval process, including at the various readings of the rezoning and at 
building permit and occupancy permit issuance to restrict occupancy until all TAP 
requirements are met. However, there are other milestones where staff are unable to 
monitor TAP in a timely manner. Key challenges include: 
• 
Timely communication with tenants about development status; 
• 
Timely payment of moving compensation prior to the issuance of the notice to end 
tenancy; and 
• 
Timely submission of TAP materials to the City.  
3.3  Jurisdictional Scan  
The Province enacted Bill 16 Housing Statutes Amendment Act on April 25, 2025, which 
introduced amendments to the Community Charter and Local Government Act. These 
amendments provide local governments with additional and more robust tools to support 
tenants facing redevelopment that best fit the needs of their community. Prior to this 
legislative change, local governments did not have the authority to develop tenant 
protection bylaws or require development permits for tenant protection.  
417

Subject: Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and Associated Bylaw 
Amendments 
PDC Meeting March 31, 2026 ………………………Page 5 
 
Since February 2026, four local governments in BC have adopted tenant protection bylaws, 
as shown in Table 4. Each of these local governments have implemented bylaws that reflect 
their particular community’s needs. All four provide tenant protection, including written 
notices and some form of financial compensation. However, the compensation varies 
between local governments based on different housing needs and development conditions 
in their respective communities.

Burnaby has been a leader in tenant assistance since the enhanced TAP was introduced in 
2020. By transitioning from the TAP to a bylaw, the City would be able to address the issues 
described in Sections 3.1 and 3.2 of this report, and build on key tenant assistance 
measures and replacement rental unit requirements to continue being a leader in tenant 
assistance.

Subject: Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and Associated Bylaw 
Amendments 
PDC Meeting March 31, 2026 ………………………Page 6 
 
4.0  PROPOSED TENANT PROTECTION FRAMEWORK 
Since 2020, TAP and Stream One [Rental Replacement] of the City’s RUZP have worked 
together to support tenants and to secure the replacement of rental units that may otherwise 
be lost through redevelopment. TAP provides the tenant‑support framework, while RUZP 
secures the delivery of replacement rental units. The proposed tenant protection framework 
will transition and strengthen the policy-based requirements in TAP and Stream One of 
RUZP into bylaw through the introduction of: 
• A new Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026; 
• Amendments to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009;  
• The Tenant Protection Development Permit; 
• A new Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw; and 
• Amendments to the inclusionary rental requirements under the Zoning Bylaw. 
If Council endorses the proposed tenant protection framework set out in this report, then as 
part of this report it is recommended that Council authorize the City Solicitor to bring forward 
the Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 (Attachment 1) and amendments to the Burnaby 
Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw (Attachment 3), and that Council also approve 
amendments to the applicability of TAP upon the proposed Burnaby TPB coming into effect.  
The other bylaws required to support the proposed tenant protection framework would be 
brought forward for Council’s consideration as part of a future report that will focus on 
Phase 4 of the Zoning Bylaw Rewrite project (repeal and replace Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 
1965 and supporting regulations). 
4.1  Proposed Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 
The proposed Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 is in Attachment 1. It is proposed that 
the Burnaby TPB come into force and effect on July 1, 2026, to coincide with the proposed 
effective date of the new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026. Below is a summary of the key 
components of the proposed TPB and the differences between the proposed Burnaby TPB 
and the current TAP, which differences are described in greater detail in Attachment 2.   
4.1.1 Applicability of Burnaby’s TPB 
The TPB is proposed to apply to all redevelopments (whether or not a rezoning is 
required) that involve purpose-built rental buildings on a redevelopment site where at 
least one building contains five or more purpose-built rental units. This includes 
separate buildings and/or secondary rental units being consolidated into the 
redevelopment.  
4.1.2 Exemptions  
It is proposed that the following be exempt from the Burnaby TPB:  
o Purpose-built rental housing rented at non-market rental rates that are 
redeveloped by those government bodies and non-profit entities more specifically 
set out in Attachment 2; 
o Redevelopment that is subject to TAP, which would include projects with an in-
process rezoning application, projects that have received final adoption of 
419

Subject: Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and Associated Bylaw 
Amendments 
PDC Meeting March 31, 2026 ………………………Page 7 
 
rezoning, as well as projects (either with an in-process rezoning application or an 
adopted rezoning subject to TAP) that submit a new rezoning application within 
five years following adoption of the TPB. The proposed amendment to the 
application of TAP following the effective date of the TPB is discussed further in 
section 5.0 of this report;  
o Redevelopment of co-operative housing by a non-profit housing co-operative1; 
and  
o Redevelopment of rental units that were created by the wind-up of a strata plan. 
These are strata buildings purchased by a developer with the intention of 
redeveloping them in the short term. Tenancies in these buildings are typically 
short-term, and tenants move in with the understanding that redevelopment is 
planned. 
 
4.1.3 Tenant Eligibility:  
In line with the Community Charter, and as outlined in the proposed Burnaby TPB, a 
tenant is eligible for TPB benefits if they live at the redevelopment site under a tenancy 
agreement on the date the Four Month Notice to End Tenancy is issued.  This is 
different from TAP, which determines tenant eligibility by reference to the date the 
developer submits a rezoning application. Even with this change, the proposed Burnaby 
TPB still requires early notification to tenants about the planned redevelopment, just as 
the TAP currently requires.  
Under the proposed TPB, eligible caretakers will continue to be eligible for the same 
benefits as under TAP.    
Notably, staff have applied a broader application of tenancy agreement to include a 
written agreement (as outlined in the Community Charter), as well as proof of payment 
of rent and proof of residency as documentation of tenancy in the case of no written 
agreement. This expanded definition allows tenants that do not have a written tenancy 
agreement (often these are tenants with longstanding tenancies) to be eligible for the 
Burnaby TPB. 
4.1.4 Other Requirements Under Burnaby’s TPB  
The substance of the following requirements under TAP will continue under the 
proposed TPB: 
• 
Requirement for a tenant relocation coordinator to manage, facilitate and administer 
the tenant relocation process, tenant benefits, and communications with tenants; 
• 
Requirement for developers to provide regular communications and updates to the 
City and tenants; and  
 
1 Following adoption of the Burnaby TPB, staff will start working with the non-profit housing sector to 
create an alternative “tenant assistance” policy for non-profit and co-operative housing developments 
in alignment with the phase three HOME Strategy Action 9.3 to be completed between 2027 and 
2031.  
 
420

Subject: Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and Associated Bylaw 
Amendments 
PDC Meeting March 31, 2026 ………………………Page 8 
 
• 
Eligible tenants will continue to be eligible for the following benefits: 
▪ 
the option of selecting rent top-ups in developer-secured or tenant-secured 
interim housing, or a lump sum payment;  
▪ 
Moving compensation; and 
▪ 
Right of First Refusal to a replacement rental unit (with the same number of 
bedrooms as the eligible tenant’s prior unit) in the new development at the same 
rental rate as they paid for their prior unit, plus permitted RTA rent increases.  
4.2  Compliance and Enforcement  
Under the current TAP, compliance checks are required prior to the following development 
approval stages:  
• 
Rezoning report advancement;  
• 
Final Adoption;  
• 
Building Permit issuance; and  
• 
Occupancy Permit issuance. 
The proposed tenant protection framework will introduce new enforcement tools through the 
TPB (and associated amendments to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 
as further described in section 5.2 of this report) and Tenant Protection DP. 
4.2.1 Amendments to Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 
The TPB, together with the proposed amendments to the Bylaw Notice Enforcement 
Bylaw 2009, will enable the City to issue bylaw notices, impose fines, and prosecute 
developers for violations of the Burnaby TPB. It is recommended that Council authorize 
the City Solicitor to bring forward amendments to the Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 
2009 set out in Attachment 3 to enable staff to issue fines for violations of the TPB.   
As with all other bylaw violation cases, staff will work cooperatively with developers to 
resolve any areas of concern before resorting to issuing violation notices. However, in 
instances where there are serious or repeated violations, violation notices would be 
issued. The fine amounts for violations of the various sections of the TPB are proposed 
to be set at the maximum amount permitted by the province ($500). For example, in the 
case of non-payment of rent top-ups from the developer to eligible tenants, staff would 
issue fines to the developer for failing to pay required top‑ups. In this situation, staff 
would also use the security provided by the developer to make payment to tenants to 
ensure their rent top‑ups are received immediately. Information about the security 
collected as part of the Tenant Protection DP is expanded upon in the section below, 
and will be used in conjunction with bylaw enforcement measures, when needed, to 
ensure delivery of required tenant assistance measures. 
4.2.2 Tenant Protection DP 
The Tenant Protection DP will include a requirement that the owner comply with the 
provisions of the TPB. If a developer fails to comply with the conditions under the 
Tenant Protection DP, then the City may revoke the Tenant Protection DP and issue a 
stop work order under the Building Bylaw to stop the construction of the development 
until the developer brings itself into compliance. 
421

Subject: Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and Associated Bylaw 
Amendments 
PDC Meeting March 31, 2026 ………………………Page 9 
 
4.3  Centralized Application and Permit Process  
The current TAP process does not include a centralized application or permit process for 
applicants, tenant relocation coordinators, or City staff. The new proposed Burnaby 
Development Procedures Bylaw 2026 would, among other things, establish application and 
security requirements for Tenant Protection DPs to ensure that applicants have completed 
certain preliminary requirements (including initial tenant communications, draft tenant 
eligibility list, tenant meeting information, and security calculations) and to streamline 
application review and DP issuance.  
The method for calculating the security amount would remain the same as under the current 
TAP, and payment of the security would be required prior to and as a condition for issuance 
of the Tenant Protection DP. The conditions related to the use and release of the security 
are proposed to be included as conditions of the Tenant Protection DP. 
The proposed new Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw 2026 is anticipated to be 
brought forward for Council’s consideration as part of a future report that supports the 
proposed new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026.  
4.4  Inclusionary Rental Requirements under new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 
As noted in section 4.1.4, the requirement under TAP for developers to provide eligible 
tenants with a Right of First Refusal (ROFR) to a replacement rental unit in the new 
development will be carried forward under the proposed TPB, including:  
• 
the same number of bedrooms as the eligible tenant’s prior unit; and  
• 
at the same rental rate as they paid for their prior unit (plus permitted RTA rent 
increases).  
Under the proposed new tenant protection framework, the requirement to provide and 
secure the rental tenure and rental rates of replacement rental units at second tenancy, 
once either an eligible household decides not to exercise their ROFR for a replacement 
rental unit or the returning tenant vacates the replacement rental unit, is proposed to be 
addressed through updates to the previously adopted Inclusionary Rental Requirements 
schedule as part of the proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026. The Inclusionary Rental 
Requirements schedule was adopted by Council in October 2025 and added to Burnaby 
Zoning Bylaw 1965, and works alongside the RUZP in so far as the Zoning Bylaw schedule 
applies to new R District applications, and the RUZP generally applies to RM based 
development applications. Key differences between the Inclusionary Rental Requirements 
schedule and the RUZP include the percentage of inclusionary units required within new 
developments, and the rental rates of required inclusionary units. These amended 
requirements contained within the schedule were informed based on a detailed financial 
analysis of the R District regulations. The Inclusionary Rental Requirements schedule did 
not, however, supersede or amend Stream 1 of the RUZP. Stream 1 of the RUZP continued 
to apply to both R District and RM District applications while the Burnaby TPB was being 
advanced; however, consistent with the March 11, 2025 Council Report on Inclusionary 
Rental Requirements, R District based applications are currently not required to provide 
additional inclusionary rental units beyond the rental replacement units, and no offset 
density is provided. 
422

Subject: Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and Associated Bylaw 
Amendments 
PDC Meeting March 31, 2026 ………………………Page 10 
 
As noted above, rental replacement is currently implemented through Stream 1 [Rental 
Replacement] of the RUZP and applies to both RM and R District based redevelopments 
subject to rezoning. Through the rezoning process, the delivery of replacement rental units 
are secured through a Housing Agreement and Section 219 Housing Covenant. These 
agreements and covenants stipulate that in the event that either an eligible tenant declines 
to exercise the ROFR to a replacement rental unit, or a returning tenant vacates the 
replacement rental unit, the replacement rental unit is to convert to an inclusionary unit with 
rents set at 20% below CMHC market median rents in line with Stream 2 of the RUZP. The 
rents required under Stream 2 of the RUZP are notably lower than those required under the 
Inclusionary Rental Requirements schedule, which requires rents at Burnaby-wide CMHC 
market median rates.  
Going forward, it is proposed that the regulations in the forthcoming update to the 
inclusionary rental requirements Schedule of the new Zoning Bylaw 2026 (which will apply 
to all new R District based developments, including those that do not require a rezoning) 
generally align with the following: 
• 
All replacement rental units are proposed to be restricted to rental tenure; 
• 
Replacement rental units are proposed to include the same mix of units (by bedroom 
type) as the rental units that were demolished for redevelopment;  
• 
Where an eligible tenant under the Burnaby TPB either declines to exercise their 
ROFR to a replacement rental unit or subsequently vacates the replacement rental 
unit: 
o those replacement rental units that would otherwise be required to be 
inclusionary units, be restricted to rent rates that do not exceed the 
inclusionary rental rates as set out in the inclusionary rental requirements 
Schedule of the new Zoning Bylaw 2026, rather than the RUZP inclusionary 
rental rates;  
o those “extra” replacement rental units beyond the number of required 
inclusionary units as set out in the inclusionary rental requirements Schedule 
of the new Zoning Bylaw 2026, be unrestricted as to rental rates (i.e., 
permitted to be market rental units);  
• 
In instances where total replacement rental units do not meet the minimum required 
inclusionary percentage as set out in the Inclusionary Rental Requirements 
schedule, it is proposed that the amount of inclusionary units be “topped up” such 
that the greater of the inclusionary requirement and rental replacement requirement 
is provided; and.  
• 
In instances of development that are 100% purpose-built market rental projects, all 
replacement rental units will be unrestricted as to rental rates at second tenancy (i.e. 
permitted to be market rental units). 
 
For clarity, and in keeping with the general intent to apply the RUZP to RM District based 
applications, and the Inclusionary Rental Requirements schedule and Burnaby TPB to R 
District based applications, the proposed amendments as outlined above would only apply 
to new applications (R District based), with existing instream applications (mostly RM 
District based) continuing to be processed under the RUZP.  
 
423

Subject: Proposed Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and Associated Bylaw 
Amendments 
PDC Meeting March 31, 2026 ………………………Page 11 
 
This proposed approach to inclusionary and replacement rental requirements, together with 
a summary and consideration of the financial feasibility analysis , will be discussed in detail 
in a future report that will focus on Phase 4 of the Zoning Bylaw Rewrite project, which will 
include a proposed revised Inclusionary Rental Requirements schedule. It should be noted 
that several R District-based applications are advancing through approvals prior to the 
proposed amendments potentially coming into effect on July 1, 2026, subject to subsequent 
Council approval. In an effort to process R District based applications consistently and fairly, 
and to avoid potential delays in applicants completing their applications and advancing the 
associated covenants and Housing Agreements while awaiting the effective date of the 
amendments, staff may seek Council approval, where warranted, to consider early 
implementation of the proposed amended measures as noted above.  
5.0  IMPLEMENTATION OF NEW TENANT PROTECTION FRAMEWORK, TAP AND 
RUZP 
It is proposed that any new redevelopment proposal submitted to the City after the effective 
date (anticipated to be July 1, 2026) of the Burnaby TPB would be subject to the TPB, 
Tenant Protection DP and the inclusionary rental requirements in the forthcoming Burnaby 
Zoning Bylaw 2026. TAP and RUZP would not apply to these new redevelopments. If the 
Burnaby TPB is approved by Council, the existing TAP would be amended to reflect the 
current inclusionary regulation in the Zoning Bylaw and to outline that new rezoning projects 
are not subject to TAP.  
Currently, there are 30 TAP projects underway (including projects that have yet to achieve 
final adoption of rezoning and projects that have been rezoned) that have not yet achieved 
occupancy as outlined in Attachment 4. All of these in-progress development applications 
will continue to be subject to TAP and RUZP. If a new rezoning application is submitted for 
any of these in-progress TAP projects within five years after the effective date of the 
Burnaby TPB, then it is proposed that TAP and RUZP (Stream 1) continue to apply to the 
project. For clarity, where an R District rezoning application is otherwise subject to the 
Zoning Bylaw’s Inclusionary Rental Requirements (Schedule XI), only Stream 1 (Rental 
Replacement) requirements will apply to the proposed development. The goal of this 
approach is to reduce the impacts on existing TAP tenants as the City shifts from the TAP 
to the new Burnaby TPB. By exempting the projects listed in Attachment 4 for five years 
following the effective date of the Burnaby TPB, tenants who were originally eligible for TAP 
benefits would be able to keep those benefits. These projects would continue to use the 
original rezoning application date, not the notice to end tenancy move-out date, to 
determine who is eligible for tenant supports. This ensures that the tenants who were 
eligible under TAP remain eligible during the transition.  
After all of these in-progress TAP projects have been completed, staff will bring forward a 
report to summarize the outcomes of the TAP and RUZP programs and formally archive all 
TAP program materials.

6.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Staff have completed the following communication and community engagement actions 
since Council authorized the initiation of this work:  
• 
A project webpage has been developed to provide information about the Burnaby 
TPB and Tenant Protection DP.  
• 
Public consultation on the draft Tenant Protection DPA was completed as part of 
Phase 4 of the OCP engagement held between April 9 and May 11, 2025.  
If the TPB is adopted by Council, the following communications would be undertaken:  
Communication on the Burnaby TPB:  
• 
A new webpage for the TPB; 
• 
A new brochure for Tenant Protection;  
• 
A new tenant guide and owner guide; and  
• 
Presentations to affected parties, including the development community and Tenant 
Relocation Coordinators who work in Burnaby.  
Communication for in-progress TAP projects:  
• 
Outreach through letters to current owners and applicants with in-progress rezoning 
applications confirming that:  
▪ 
In-progress projects are not subject to the TPB (unless a resubmission is made 
after 5 years of the effective date of the TPB).  
• 
Maintenance of the existing TAP webpage and TAP materials for all 30 in-progress 
TAP projects until those projects are completed.  
7.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The Burnaby TPB enforcement framework will have operating impacts, which are intended 
to be offset by both development permit fees and revenues received through fines. Given 
the highly variable nature of the costs of administering a program such as that proposed, 
even though RUZP and the enhanced TAP have existed for nearly 7 years, future costs are 
very hard to predict, as they relate primarily to the level of applicant compliance. Thus the 
degree and extent of those operating impacts are not fully known at this time. As such, after 
two years have passed from the effective date of the TPB, this process will be reviewed and 
funds generated through TPB administration and enforcement will be reassessed. It is 
anticipated that the proposed framework can be administered with existing staffing levels, 
and that additional resources are not required at this time. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
E.W Kozak, General Manager Planning and Development

of this bylaw have the meanings commonly assigned to them in the context in which they 
are used in this bylaw. 
2.3 
All references to the statutes, regulations, bylaws, orders, policies or guidelines of a federal, 
provincial, municipal or other governmental authority is a reference to such statute, 
regulation, bylaw, order, policy or guideline as amended or replaced from time to time. 
2.4 
The schedules to this bylaw are attached to and form an integral part of this bylaw. 
 
PART 3: APPLICATION 
3.1 
Subject to section 3.2, this bylaw applies to a redevelopment involving: 
(a) five (5) or more purpose-built rental housing dwelling units on a single parcel; and 
(b) any parcels with purpose-built rental housing dwelling units or secondary rental 
units, which are consolidated or subdivided with a parcel that meets the criteria set 
out in subsection 3.1(a). 
3.2 
This bylaw does not apply to: 
(a) purpose-built rental housing dwelling units rented at non-market rental rates, 
which are redeveloped by any of the following entities: 
431

 
 
Bylaw No. XXXXX 
Page 6 
(i) 
the government of British Columbia or an agent of the government of British 
Columbia; 
(ii) 
the government of Canada or an agent of the government of Canada;  
(iii) 
a municipality;  
(iv) 
a regional district;  
(v) 
a corporation incorporated by, or in which shares have been acquired by, a 
municipality or regional district for a purpose that includes providing affordable 
housing;  
(vi) 
a First Nation;  
(vii) 
a society as defined in section 1 of the Societies Act (British Columbia); 
(viii) a registered charity as defined in section 248(1) of the Income Tax Act (Canada); 
or 
(ix) 
a corporation as defined in section 2(1) of the Canada Not-for-profit 
Corporations Act (Canada); 
(b) redevelopment that is subject to the Rental Use Zoning Policy and Tenant 
Assistance Policy; 
(c) redevelopment of a co-operative housing development by a non-profit housing co-
operative; 
(d) redevelopment of purpose-built rental housing that was created by the cancellation 
of a strata plan. 
PART 4: TENANT RELOCATION COORDINATOR 
4.1 
Commencing no later than the date the owner submits a development application for a 
redevelopment until the owner has submitted a final tenant relocation report, to the 
satisfaction of the City, in accordance with section 7.4 of this bylaw, the owner shall retain 
a tenant relocation coordinator, and ensure that the tenant relocation coordinator is the 
primary contact for, and maintains regular communication by phone and email at consistent 
hours, with prior site households, eligible households, and eligible caretakers regarding:  
(a) notices and information with respect to the redevelopment;   
(b) information about financial compensation in relation to termination of tenancy 
agreements;  
(c) information about other assistance to find and relocate to interim housing; 
(d) information about the opportunity to exercise the right of first refusal for a 
replacement rental unit; and 
(e) such other information that the City may require for the purposes of this bylaw. 
4.2 
The owner shall not designate the owner, the owner’s authorized agent for the 
development application, or the caretaker, property manager, superintendent or building 
manager of the prior site as the tenant relocation coordinator. 
432

 
 
Bylaw No. XXXXX 
Page 7 
 
PART 5: COMMUNICATIONS WITH TENANTS 
5.1 
The owner shall, following the move-out date set out in the notice to end tenancy, provide 
eligible households and eligible caretakers with status updates on the redevelopment at 
least every six (6) months, which status update shall include the then-current anticipated 
date for issuance of an occupancy certificate for the replacement rental units.  
5.2 
The owner shall, six (6) months prior to the anticipated issuance of an occupancy 
certificate for the replacement rental units, provide to each eligible household and 
eligible caretaker the information and documentation reviewed and accepted by the City 
in accordance with section 1 of Schedule C to this bylaw. 
5.3 
The owner shall, three (3) months prior to the anticipated issuance of an occupancy 
certificate for the replacement rental units, provide to each eligible household and 
eligible caretaker the information and documentation reviewed and accepted by the City 
in accordance with section 2 of Schedule C to this bylaw. 
5.4 
No earlier than the date an occupancy certificate is issued for the replacement rental 
units, and at least 45 days before the move-in date for a replacement rental unit, the 
owner shall provide to each eligible household and eligible caretaker: 
(a) offer of a right of first refusal for a replacement rental unit at the replacement 
rental rate by delivering to each eligible household and eligible caretaker the 
notice reviewed and accepted by the City in accordance with section 3 of 
Schedule C to this bylaw; and 
(b) the opportunity to schedule a viewing of the replacement rental unit at least 35 
days prior to the move-in date.  
 
PART 6: HOUSEHOLD AND CARETAKER BENEFITS 
6.1 
Subject to section 6.4, the owner shall provide each eligible household: 
(a) one of the three following forms of financial compensation, as selected by the eligible 
household on their tenant assistance form: 
(i) 
Option 1: Rent top-ups in owner-secured interim housing, as more particularly 
described and in accordance with section 1 of Schedule A to this bylaw; 
(ii) Option 2: Rent top-ups in tenant-secured interim housing, as more particularly 
described and in accordance with section 2 of Schedule A to this bylaw; or 
(iii) Option 3: Lump sum payment, as more particularly described and in accordance 
with section 3 of Schedule A to this bylaw; 
(b) moving compensation, calculated in accordance with Schedule B to this bylaw; 
(c) access to the services of a tenant relocation coordinator; and 
(d) right of first refusal for a replacement rental unit at the replacement rental rate, 
and if an eligible household exercises their right of first refusal, the owner shall 
433

 
 
Bylaw No. XXXXX 
Page 8 
enter into a tenancy agreement for a replacement rental unit at the replacement 
rental rate with that eligible household . 
6.2 
Subject to section 6.5, the owner shall provide each eligible caretaker: 
(a) 
moving compensation, calculated in accordance with Schedule B to this bylaw; 
(b) 
access to the services of a tenant relocation coordinator; and 
(c) 
right of first refusal for a replacement rental unit at the replacement rental unit 
rate, and to enter into a tenancy agreement for a replacement rental unit at the 
replacement rental rate with each eligible caretaker that exercises their right of 
first refusal. 
6.3 
If an eligible household or eligible caretaker requests additional support on their tenant 
assistance form, then the owner shall provide such additional support as follows: 
(a) 
early communication and notifications;  
(b) 
provision of transportation to view interim housing options; and/or 
(c) 
arranging packing assistance.  
6.4 
If an eligible household has and their tenancy in their interim housing terminated for 
cause, then that eligible household ceases to be eligible for further household benefits 
under section 6.1. 
6.5 
If an eligible caretaker has their tenancy in their interim housing is terminated for cause, 
then that eligible caretaker ceases to be eligible for benefits outlined in section 6.2, and 
the owner ceases to be obligated to provide benefits to that eligible caretaker. 
6.6 
The owner shall provide security, calculated and provided in accordance with the Burnaby 
Development Procedures Bylaw, to the City prior to the issuance of a Tenant Protection 
Development Permit under the Zoning Bylaw and the City may use such security for 
fulfilling the owner’s requirements in respect to household benefits and caretaker 
benefits under this bylaw, on the terms and conditions set out in the Tenant Protection 
Development Permit for the redevelopment. 
 
PART 7: SUBMISSIONS TO THE CITY 
7.1 
Within 35 days following the move-out date set out in the notice to end tenancy, the 
owner shall submit a tenant relocation report to the City.  
7.2 
The owner shall, following the move-out date set out in the notice to end tenancy, provide 
the City with status updates on the redevelopment at least every six (6) months, which 
status update shall include the then-current anticipated date for issuance of an occupancy 
certificate for the replacement rental units. 
434

 
 
Bylaw No. XXXXX 
Page 9 
7.3 
The owner shall, commencing seven months prior to the anticipated issuance of an 
occupancy certificate for the replacement rental units, provide to the City for review 
and acceptance, the information and documentation as described in and in accordance with 
the timelines set out in Schedule C to this bylaw. 
7.4 
Within 35 days following the date the last returning tenant moves into their replacement 
rental unit, the owner shall submit to the City a final tenant relocation report.  
 
PART 8: OFFENCES AND PENALTIES 
8.1 
Every person who violates any of the provisions of this bylaw, including a specific 
regulation set out in the Schedules, or who suffers or permits any act or thing to be done in 
contravention of any of the provisions of this bylaw, or who neglects to do or refrains from 
doing anything required to be done by any of the provisions of this bylaw, or who does 
any act, or who violates any of the provisions of this bylaw, is guilty of an offence and is 
liable, on summary conviction, to a fine of not less than five thousand dollars ($5,000.00) 
and not more than fifty thousand dollars ($50,000.00). 
8.2 
If an offence continues for more than one day, a separate offence occurs on each day or 
part of a day, and separate fines may be issued for each day or part of a day in respect of 
which the offence occurs or continues. 
8.3 
Any person who contravenes any provision of this bylaw is liable to the City for and must 
indemnify the City from all costs, expenses, damages and injuries resulting from the 
contravention. This does not in any way limit any other provision or any other remedy the 
City may have under this bylaw or otherwise at law. 
8.4 
A violation of any of the provisions identified in this bylaw shall result in liability for 
penalties and late payment amounts established in Schedule A of Burnaby Bylaw Notice 
Enforcement Bylaw, 2009, and be subject to the procedures, restrictions, limits, obligations 
and rights established in Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw, 2009 and the Local 
Government Bylaw Notice Enforcement Act. 
PART 9: SEVERABILITY 
9.1 
If any portion of this bylaw is held invalid by a Court of competent jurisdiction, the invalid 
portion shall be severed and the remainder of this bylaw is deemed to have been adopted 
without the severed section, subsection, paragraph, subparagraph, clause or phrase.

Schedule A  
Household Financial Compensation 
1. Option 1: Rent top-ups in owner-secured interim housing 
If an eligible household selects rent top-ups in owner-secured interim housing, then the owner 
shall: 
(a) between the issuance of and move-out date set out in the notice to end tenancy, identify 
and present to the eligible household at least three interim housing options that: 
(i) 
are located in Burnaby, and within or proximate to the same area as the prior site, 
or, if agreed to by the eligible household, located outside Burnaby but within the 
Metro Vancouver area; 
(ii) 
meet the eligible household’s needs as identified in their tenant assistance form; 
(iii) 
unless otherwise agreed to by the eligible household, has the same number of 
bedrooms as their prior unit; 
(iv) 
permit the same number and type(s) of pet(s) as the eligible household kept and was 
permitted to keep in their prior unit, and that do not, unless required by law, impose 
any restrictions on pets or the keeping of pets that were not also imposed on the 
eligible household under their tenancy agreement for their prior unit;  
(b) pay to the eligible household an amount equal to the difference, if any, between the amount 
of the damage security and, if applicable, pet damage deposit, for an eligible household’s 
interim housing unit and the amount of such deposits paid by the eligible household under 
the tenancy agreement for their prior unit; 
(c) commencing on the date that the eligible household moves into the owner-secured 
interim housing unit and ending on the last day of the month after the determined move-
in date for a replacement rental unit (regardless of whether the eligible household 
exercises their right of first refusal to a replacement rental unit), pay to the eligible 
household prior to rent under the tenancy agreement for an interim housing unit is due, 
the sum of the following amounts: 
(i) 
an amount equal to the difference between the rent payable for the interim housing 
selected by the eligible household and the eligible household’s rent for their prior 
unit as of the date the notice to end tenancy was issued; 
(ii) 
if the rent paid by the eligible household for their prior unit included heat but the 
rent payable for the interim housing does not, an amount equal to the “electricity” 
amount set out in the Monthly Heat Allowance Rates published by BC Housing from 
time to time; and 
(iii) 
if the rent paid by the eligible household for their prior unit included hot water but 
the rent payable for the interim housing does not, an amount equal to the “gas 
supplement” amount set out in the Monthly Heat Allowance Rates published by BC 
Housing from time to time; 
(d) if the rent for an eligible household’s interim housing is increased, as permitted by the 
Residential Tenancy Act, pay to the eligible household the owner’s share of such increase, 
437

 
 
Bylaw No. XXXXX 
Page 12 
which share shall be equal in proportion to the amount under paragraph 1(c)(i) in 
proportion to the rent payable for the interim housing on the date the eligible household 
moved into the interim housing; and 
(e) if during the interim period, an eligible household’s tenancy in an owner-secured interim 
housing unit is terminated other than for cause, secure another interim housing unit for 
that eligible household that meet the criteria set out in section 1(a) of this Schedule A.   
 
2. Option 2: Rent top-up in tenant-secured interim housing 
If an eligible household selects rent top-ups in tenant-secured interim housing, then the eligible 
household will be responsible to pay for the rent, any deposits and rent increases for the tenant-
secured interim housing, and the owner shall, commencing on the date that the eligible 
household moves into the tenant-secured interim housing unit and ending on the last day of the 
month after the determined move-in date for a replacement rental unit (regardless of whether the 
eligible household exercises their right of first refusal to a replacement rental unit), pay to the 
eligible household prior to rent under the tenancy agreement for an interim housing unit is due, 
the lesser of: 
(a) 
A – B, where;  
i. A = the amount of rent payable for the interim housing selected by the 
eligible household 
ii. B = eligible household’s rent for their prior unit as of the move-out date 
set out in the notice to end tenancy; and  
(b) the greater of: 
i. (A x 1.30) – B, where:  
• A= the most recent CMHC market median rent for the same unit 
type as the eligible household’s prior unit as of the move-out date 
set out in the notice to end tenancy 
• B = the eligible household’s monthly rent for their prior unit; and 
ii. A x 0.15, where:  
• A = the eligible household’s monthly rent for the prior unit 
If the rent for an eligible household’s interim housing is increased, as permitted by the 
Residential Tenancy Act, pay to the eligible household the owner’s share of such increase, 
which share shall be equal in proportion to the lesser of the amounts calculated in 2(a) and 2(b), 
in proportion to the rent payable for the interim housing on the date the eligible household 
moved into the interim housing 
3. Option 3: Lump Sum Payment  
If an eligible household selects a lump sum payment, then the owner shall pay to the eligible 
household on the move-out date set out in the notice to end tenancy, an amount equal to the 
greater of:  
(a) 
((A x 1.30) – B) x 36, where: 
438

 
 
Bylaw No. XXXXX 
Page 13 
• A = the most recent CMHC market median rent for the same unit type as the 
eligible household’s prior unit as of the date the notice to end tenancy is 
issued; and  
• B = the eligible household’s monthly rent for their prior unit.  
 
(b) 
(A x 0.15) x 36, where:  
• A = the eligible household’s rent for their prior unit as of the date the notice 
to end tenancy is issued
