Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report      11
Recycle BC continues to expand the range of materials 
accepted in the provincial Blue Box program. In 2025, 
Recycle BC launched a pilot curbside collection program 
for flexible plastics (known as the Pink Box program) in 
Maple Ridge and West Vancouver. In 2026, this program 
will expand to the City of Vancouver (multifamily only) and 
the Regional District of Central Okanagan.
Accepted flexible plastics include plastic bags, overwraps, 
outer bags, flexible packaging with plastic seals, woven plastic 
bags, zipper‑lock pouches, crinkly wrappers and protective 
cushion packaging. These materials are also accepted 
at depot locations, including Burnaby’s Eco‑Centre. As 
technologies and facilities improve, the range of recyclable 
items is expected to continue expanding.
Regional Waste Management System
Burnaby is one of 21 member municipalities collaborating 
with Metro Vancouver to develop and implement regional 
solid waste strategies. Metro Vancouver’s regional 
Integrated Solid Waste and Resource Management 
Plan (ISWRMP), approved by the provincial Ministry of 
Environment in 2011, provides long-term guidance for waste 
reduction, recycling and residual waste management.
Metro Vancouver is currently updating the ISWRMP 
to reflect modern needs and priorities. The update is 
expected to emphasize accelerated waste diversion, 
reduced GHG emissions, and a transition toward a 
circular economy—keeping materials circulating through 
repair, reuse and recycling for as long as possible. 
The plan is currently in its initial draft stage, and Metro 
Vancouver is seeking feedback from municipal partners.
Burnaby participates in two regional committees:
	» the REAC Solid Waste Subcommittee
	» the Regional Waste Reduction Coordinators 
Committee (RWRCC)
These groups support knowledge-sharing, coordination 
on regional initiatives, and communication between Metro 
Vancouver, local governments and the province. The 
Vancouver Landfill has also received a lifetime extension 
from 2030 to 2050, providing the region with additional 
capacity and time to plan for future disposal needs. 
In November 2023, the Province mandated zoning 
bylaw amendments to increase small-scale multi-unit 
(SSMUH) across British Columbia. As of July 1, 2024, 
Burnaby updated its Zoning Bylaw to allow additional 
density in R1 residential areas, enabling duplexes, 
multiplexes, rowhouses, laneway homes and up to six-
unit configurations. These changes will significantly affect 
future waste generation and collection practices.
With increased density comes the need for updated 
waste management strategies and bylaw adjustments. 
On December 11, 2023, Burnaby amended its Solid Waste 
and Recycling Bylaw No. 12875 to align the definition of 
“primary dwelling” with the new Zoning Bylaw No. 14630. 
The amendment also requires households to use the 
largest available receptacles before requesting additional 
containers, helping manage limited storage and staging 
space. Recycling bins continue to be provided at no cost 
to encourage waste diversion.
To prepare for increased small scale multi unit housing 
development, the City created Waste and Recycling 
Design Guidelines to support builders in planning 
storage and collection areas on densified properties. The 
guidelines recommend shared garbage and green waste 
bins, with individual recycling bins provided for each 
unit, ensuring adequate space and efficient curbside 
collection.
Development activity increased substantially in 2025, 
with 93 SSMUH development reviews completed and 24 
more under review, compared to only five in the final third 
of 2024. As new developments are completed, SW&R 
collection teams are actively adapting operations to 
support changing densities while maintaining high-quality 
service delivery.

3.0 Program and Services
3.1	 Curbside Collection Program
Burnaby’s in-house collection services have been a core component of the City’s SW&R 
program for more than 40 years. These services are delivered by approximately 146 staff and 
supported by a fleet of 86 vehicles. Our office team and field crews work collaboratively to 
provide efficient and reliable service, and to meet the evolving needs of the community.
Burnaby’s curbside collection program provides 
collection services for three key sectors:
	» Single-family residential
	» Multifamily residential
	» Industrial, commercial, institutional (ICI)
The City operates a multi-stream recycling system that 
aligns with Recycle BC’s residential printed paper and 
packaging program. 
Materials are collected using a colour coded system 
designed to maintain high-quality recyclables:
	» Blue Box: mixed containers
	» Yellow Bag/Box: mixed paper
	» Grey Box: glass jars and bottles
Organic materials are collected through the City’s Green 
Bin program, which accepts food scraps, yard trimmings 
and leaf debris. These materials are processed into 
compost at a contracted regional facility.

3.2 General Services Program
The General Services Program is responsible for several key operational services that support cleanliness, waste 
diversion, and the management of large and abandoned materials throughout the city. These services include the Large 
Item Pickup program, Litter and Public Space Collection, and Abandoned Waste Collection. The program is supported 
by dedicated equipment, including three rear loader trucks for large items and abandoned material collection, four 2 
tonne flat-deck trucks for curbside mattress and appliance collection, five split body trucks for public space recycling 
and waste collection, and a fleet of three mid-size and three full-size pickup trucks for litter collection activities.
Large Item Pickup
In addition to weekly curbside recycling and waste 
collection, the City offers residents a Large Item Pickup 
service for oversized or hard-to-dispose-of materials 
such as mattresses, appliances, and furniture. This 
program remains one of the most popular services 
provided by the City, valued for being offered at no 
cost and accommodating up to three items per pickup. 
Residents can request this service via phone or through 
the City’s online service request portal.
In 2025, the City received 11,723 Large Item Pickup 
requests, making the online form for this service the 
most frequently used form on the City’s website. To 
comply with the regional disposal ban on mattresses, all 
collected mattresses are transported to and processed 
at a City-contracted mattress recycling facility, ensuring 
proper diversion of these materials from the waste 
stream. 
Streetscape recycling and waste 
collection
Littering is a persistent challenge in dense, urban 
environments such as Burnaby, where it can detract 
from the appearance of city streets and the natural 
environment. To help reduce litter and promote proper 
disposal of waste in public areas, the City maintains a 
network of 630 waste receptacles located along major 
arterial roads, at bus stops, and in areas with high 
pedestrian traffic. The litter collection fleet services 
these receptacles on a regular schedule and performs 
ongoing litter pickup along streets and boulevards.
To further support recycling in public spaces, the City 
entered into an agreement with Creative Outdoor 
Advertising (COA) in 2010 to provide three stream

receptacles. In exchange, COA receives advertising rights 
on the front and back panels of the bins, with a portion 
of the revenue shared with the City. A percentage of 
units is also reserved for City-related messaging; several 
Silverbox receptacles currently display City branded 
posters promoting the Climate Action Framework and 
encouraging residents to “Reduce, Reuse, Recycle.”
As of 2025, there were 391 Silverbox receptacles 
installed throughout Burnaby.
In addition to the Silverbox units, the City continues to 
assess other receptacle models to address specific 
location needs, such as limited space or higher waste 
generation volumes, to improve streetscape cleanliness 
and collection efficiency.
In 2025, the streetscape diversion rate experienced a 
slight decrease to 29%, with 243 tonnes of garbage and 
99 tonnes of recycling collected from public receptacles.

Abandoned Material Service
Abandoned large items—such as mattresses, furniture 
and appliances—continue to be a common issue 
throughout Burnaby, often appearing along roadsides, 
boulevards and lanes. The City’s abandoned materials 
collection crews respond to these items as they are 
identified by staff or reported by residents through 
service requests. In 2025, the City received 9,913 
service requests related to abandoned items, general 
litter and illegal dumping— representing a 30% increase 
compared to 2024.
Option for streetscape receptacles
Waste receptacle at a trail entrance
To help address illegal dumping in areas with persistent 
activity, the City continued its targeted education and 
outreach efforts. Residents in neighbourhoods with 
a history of frequent dumping received informational 
letters explaining the City’s Large Item Pickup program 
and encouraging the reporting of abandoned materials. 
In 2025, the City distributed 1,157 information letters 
to residents in these areas. This outreach may have 
contributed to both improved reporting and increased 
Large Item Pickup requests.
To strengthen enforcement and deter illegal dumping, 
since 2021 the City has been deploying a mobile 
camera unit at known dumping hotspots across 
Burnaby. Prior to each deployment, notification letters are 
sent to residential properties within 50 metres of the unit to 
inform them of its purpose and provide contact information 
for questions or concerns. In 2025, 345 notification letters 
were issued. The unit is equipped with lighting, flashing 
indicators, and signage stating, “This area is under 24 
hour video surveillance.” While deployed, the camera 
has proven to be an effective deterrent at problem 
locations and has supported investigations by helping 
identify individuals responsible for illegal dumping, as 
many do not initially recognize the unit’s function.
Unusual items removed from city streets this period 
included 20 abandoned newspaper boxes belonging 
to various newspaper companies. Many of the boxes 
were in poor condition, no longer serving their intended 
purpose, and had become receptacles for garbage. 
Each company was notified about the condition of 
its boxes; however, after numerous complaints from 
residents and business owners, the unclaimed units 
were removed from the streets and transported to the 
Eco-Centre for temporary storage.

As Burnaby continues to grow, the Environmental 
Services team has expanded its role in the development 
review process to ensure new buildings are designed 
to support effective long term waste management. At 
the early design stage, the team reviews proposed 
developments to confirm that adequate space is 
provided for SW&R collection, bulky item storage, and a 
staging area for materials. This proactive planning helps 
prevent future limitations related to space, access and 
collection efficiency, supporting the City’s broader waste 
diversion goals.
To facilitate coordinated, well-designed developments, 
Environmental Services participates as one of several 
technical reviewers for a range of applications, 
including Preliminary Plan Approvals (PPAs), Rezoning 
applications (REZ), Building Permits (BP) and small-
scale multi-unit housing residential building permits 
(R BP). Reviews may involve creating, modifying or 
relocating SW&R rooms, enclosures, loading zones 
and collection access routes for both new and existing 
developments.
Through this work, the team ensures that new 
construction aligns with City standards and is equipped 
with the infrastructure needed to support efficient waste 
collection and high waste diversion performance for 
years to come.
3.4 Environmental Services
Development Reviews
The Solid Waste and Recycling Bylaw (Bylaw No. 12875) 
requires that all residents and businesses in Burnaby 
manage their waste responsibly by properly separating 
recyclables, green waste and non-hazardous garbage. 
Despite this, contamination remains a recurring 
challenge, often due to lack of awareness or disregard 
for proper disposal practices.
When contamination is identified by City staff, the 
first step is education. A contamination sticker is 
placed on the affected bin the first two instances, and 
the property owner receives an educational letter 
explaining the issue and outlining proper diversion 
options. If a third contamination event occurs, a 
service report is generated, prompting Environmental 
Services staff to conduct an on site inspection of the 
bin or bins in question. Staff then work with occupants, 
property owners or property managers to ensure they 
understand the requirements and corrective actions 
needed.
For locations where contamination persists despite 
multiple educational interventions, the issue escalates 
to enforcement. A Bylaw Violation Notice— commonly 
referred to as a ticket—may be issued in accordance 
with the Solid Waste and Recycling Bylaw.
Illegal dumping cases, when traceable to a specific 
individual or property, also result in enforcement actions. 
Depending on the nature of the items and whether 
immediate collection is possible, staff may issue a letter, 
a ticket, or both.
In 2025, the City issued:
	» 25 tickets related to illegal dumping
	» 33 tickets for ongoing contamination of recycling or 
green waste receptacles
These efforts support waste diversion goals by 
reinforcing proper disposal practices and encouraging 
long-term behavior change and compliance.

Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report      21
In 2025, Burnaby continued to see positive progress in 
waste diversion, with the volume of garbage collected 
decreasing year over year and corresponding increases 
in both recycling and green waste tonnage. This 
trend reflects continued improvement in community 
participation and diversion behaviour across the city.
The City collected approximately 74,489 tonnes of total 
material—including garbage, recyclables and green 
waste—from residential and commercial sources. This 
represents a 1% increase compared to the previous year. 
The largest gain occurred in recycling, which increased 
by 447 tonnes, followed by a modest rise in green waste. 
Garbage volumes declined further, reinforcing the long 
term shift toward higher diversion rates.
Despite improvements in material separation, overall 
waste generated per household increased slightly. In 
2025, the average household produced 808 kg of total 
waste, an increase of 10 kg per household from the 
previous year. While this is a minor year over year rise, 
per household waste generation remains below levels 
observed during the COVID-19 period, when household 
waste peaked due to changes in work and living patterns.
These results demonstrate that, although total waste 
generation fluctuates with population growth and 
behavioural changes, the City continues to make steady 
gains in recycling performance and diversion outcomes.
4.0 Performance
The diagram below highlights Burnaby’s disposal and 
diversion performance for 2025. The City’s overall 
diversion rate remained stable at 61%, reflecting 
continued progress in separating recyclable and 
compostable materials from the garbage stream.
The Burnaby Eco-Centre played a significant role in 
maintaining this diversion rate. In 2025, the facility 
recorded increased volumes across several key material 
categories compared to 2024. Notable growth occurred 
in the collection of scrap metal, used oil, donated 
clothing, polystyrene (Styrofoam), electronics, small 
appliances, paint and mattresses. These increases 
demonstrate strong resident participation and the 
ongoing effectiveness of the City’s recycling and 
stewardship partnerships.

4.4 Construction and Demolition Waste 
Diversion Bylaw 
In June 2022, the City adopted a Construction and 
Demolition (C&D) Waste Diversion Bylaw that requires all 
buildings being demolished to divert C&D materials from 
the landfill through reuse and recycling. Construction 
and demolition waste accounts for a third of all waste 
generated in the Metro Vancouver region. Increased 
reuse and recycling building materials reduces energy 
consumption and production of GHG emissions, 
conserves landfill space and supports a circular 
economy. 
The C&D Bylaw applies to all buildings being 
demolished, requires submission of a Waste Diversion 
Plan, payment of a Waste Diversion Deposit and 
submission of a Compliance Report post-demolition. 
To receive a full deposit refund, a minimum of 70% of 
materials must be diverted through reuse and recycling.
Table 8 summarizes demolition permits completed 
between 2023 and 2025 by building type. Single- and 
two-family dwellings accounted for most demolitions 
each year, increasing from 19 in 2023 to 156 in 2025. 
Demolition of accessory buildings also increased over 
this period, while non-residential demolitions showed a 
slight increase in 2025 compared to 2024.  
In 2025, 26,972 tonnes of C&D materials were diverted 
from the landfill through reuse and recycling. This brings 
the cumulative total materials diverted from landfill since 
the bylaw’s implementation to 44,592 tonnes.
Figure 8 illustrates the percentage of materials diverted 
and landfilled, as well as the diversion rate achieved 
for each building type. In 2025, all building types met 
or exceeded the 70% diversion target, with an average 
diversion rate of 91% across all demolitions.

What do I do if I see a bear?
If you see a bear in a residential area, please report 
your sighting to a BC Conservation Oﬃ  cer by calling 
1-877-952-7277. The reports can help the City better 
understand where bear activity may need to be
monitored more closely.
Do the rules for setting out and
removing bins apply year round?
Yes. While most bears go into a deep sleep from
November to April, some bears may not hibernate
and could still be present within the Bear Area.
What is a bear-resistant enclosure?
A bear-resistant enclosure is a fully enclosed
structure that is strong enough to prevent access
by bears. This may include a garage, shed, fenced 
structure or a structure approved by Burnaby’s
Engineering Department.
Are there ﬁ nes for failing to comply
with the Bear Area requirements?
Our goal is to minimize human-bear interactions,
and we need your cooperation. If a bear is spotted
in your neighbourhood, we may issue a notiﬁ cation
letter. Failure to comply may result in a penalty of 
either $200 or $500.
BURNABY
BEAR AREA
We share the environment with black bears.
They move through local forests, mountains and 
wilderness areas in search of food. Unfortunately, 
when bears associate food with human activities, 
they lose their fear of people.
To keep wildlife and the community safe, the
City of Burnaby has established a Bear Area.
This includes places where black bears have been 
spotted, such as areas around Burnaby Mountain, 
Burnaby Lake, Deer Lake, Robert Burnaby Park 
and other parklands.
BEAR AREA
REQUIREMENTS
By following some simple rules in the Bear Area, we
can reduce the potential for conﬂ ict. Properly store
your waste bins (garbage, recycling and green bins)
to minimize bear attractants.
Single or two-family properties must: 
» Set out waste bins between 5:30am and 7am on
 collection day and remove waste bins by 8pm.
» Securely store waste bins within your property on  
 non-collection days.
Multi-family properties must:
» Set out waste bins between 5:30am and 7am on
 collection day and remove waste bins the same day.
» Store garbage, yard waste, food scraps and
 recycling in bear-resistant bins or store all bins
 in a bear-resistant enclosure on the property.
Industrial, commercial and institutional
properties must:
» Store all garbage, yard waste, food scraps and
 recycling in bear-resistant bins or store all bins
 in a bear-resistant enclosure on the property.

Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report      31
In 2019, City Council approved the establishment of 
a designated Bear Area within the SW&R Bylaw to 
reduce human wildlife conflicts and improve waste 
management practices in areas with higher bear activity. 
Within this zone, single- and two-family properties must 
follow specific morning set out times for collection, while 
multifamily and ICI properties are required to secure 
their receptacles or store them in a bear resistant 
enclosure.
The boundaries of the Bear Area are updated annually 
based on reported wildlife activity. Previously, these 
reports relied on data from the BC Conservation Officer 
Service but that data is no longer available in a format 
accessible to external organizations. Instead, Burnaby’s 
SW&R division collaborated with the City’s Geographic 
Information Systems (GIS) team to develop the Burnaby 
Wildlife Tracking App. This new tool enables staff across 
multiple departments to enter and access wildlife 
sightings, calls and complaints directly on a shared GIS 
map. The system supports the tracking of bear, coyote 
and other wildlife activity, improving the City’s ability to 
allocate resources effectively and reduce human wildlife 
interactions.
In 2024, increased bear activity in several areas of 
northeast Burnaby prompted an expansion of the Bear 
Area. Figure 9 highlights the updated boundaries, with 
the affected zones outlined in red.
As of 2025, Burnaby’s Bear Area regulations have been 
in effect for five full years, supporting the City’s efforts 
to reduce human/bear conflicts by limiting access to 
attractants such as household garbage and food scraps. 
To prepare residents for the annual “bear season” (May 
to November), the City may distribute bear awareness 
information packages to all households within the Bear 
Area, providing reminders on set out times, proper 
storage practices and strategies for securing attractants.
In 2025, Environmental Services staff undertook 
targeted monitoring in neighbourhoods experiencing 
higher levels of bear activity. This work involved 
collaboration with BC Conservation Officers, direct 
5.0 Bear Area
Figure 9 - Bear Area - 2024
communication with property owners regarding non 
compliance, and proactive education on best practices. 
Over the course of the year, the City issued 1,418 bear 
awareness letters to notify residents of increased 
activity in their area and to reinforce expectations 
around proper set out times and attractant management.
Some areas— most notably Burnaby Mountain— 
continue to experience elevated bear activity and 
require enhanced measures. Metal bear-resistant 
toppers installed on steel garbage bins have proven 
effective, and additional units have been ordered to 
strengthen attractant control in these zones. The City 
also trialed new bear-resistant food scrap bins in 2024, 
which successfully deterred bears. These containers, 
however, present some operational challenges that staff 
are actively working to resolve.
Despite these improvements, the most effective long 
term solution remains clear—multifamily customers 
must secure their receptacles or store them in a bear 
resistant enclosure. Ensuring compliance with these 
requirements is essential to minimizing bear attractants 
and reducing the risk of human wildlife conflict across 
the Bear Area.

6.0 Public Outreach and Education Programs
Spring Cleanup Event
The City hosted its second Spring Cleanup Event on 
June 14, 2025, providing residents with a one-stop 
opportunity to drop off household garbage, recyclables 
and reusable items at the Still Creek Works Yard. With 
strong support from City staff and local partners, the 
event welcomed 1,693 vehicles—an increase of 560 
from 2024—and collected 73,060 kg of garbage, 
representing 12,435 kg more waste than the previous 
year. The event also diverted 229 mattresses, with all 
recyclable materials transferred to the Burnaby Eco-Centre.
Reusable items were collected by Big Brothers of 
Greater Vancouver, totaling 6,951 kg, more than double 
the 2024 total. This included:
	» 3,429 kg of clothing, shoes, accessories, bedding 
and towels
	» 1,555 kg of small household items (kitchenware, toys, 
decorations, small equipment)
	» 533 kg of books
	» 1,434 kg of larger reusable items such as furniture 
and décor
Community litter cleanups
From June to September 2025, the City partnered with 
Green Teams of Canada to host four community litter 
cleanup events near Burnaby’s four major town centres: 
Edmonds, Lougheed, Brentwood and Metrotown. These 
events engaged 340 volunteers and removed 874 kg of 
litter, an increase of 199 kg from 2024.
The August 16 event at Edmonds Community Centre 
achieved the highest litter collection of any City cleanup 
event to date, gathering 313 kg with only 80 volunteers. 
These events also drew participation from residents 
in neighbouring municipalities, broadening regional 
awareness of civic environmental initiatives.

Adopt-a-Street Program
Launched in 2022, the Adopt-a-Street Program 
continued to grow and now includes more than 155 
active volunteers. To better support participants, the 
City launched an improved volunteer database in 2024 
along with an updated webpage, application process 
and interactive street map. These enhancements have 
strengthened volunteer coordination and expanded 
cleanup activities across City boulevards, streets and 
laneways. Coordination with the Parks, Recreation 
and Culture Department has also improved, allowing 
volunteers to contribute to litter cleanups in parks and 
City facilities.
Seasonal waste diversion campaigns
Throughout the year, the City promoted seasonal 
waste reduction messages, with a notable focus on the 
Holiday Waste Reduction Campaign during November 
and December. A dedicated webpage provided tips 
for reducing holiday packaging waste, managing food 
scraps and properly recycling Christmas trees. This 
information was also included on the curbside collection 
calendar, the City’s mobile app, and through holiday 
push notifications to ensure broad public reach.
Repair Café partnership
In 2025, the City strengthened its collaboration with 
Burnaby Neighbourhood House, a community non profit 
organization, to support repair cafés—free events where 
residents can bring damaged household items to be 
repaired by skilled volunteers. These cafés promote 
reuse and waste reduction by extending the lifespan of 
household goods. Commonly repaired items include:
	» small appliances (coffee makers, rice cookers, 
kettles, air fryers)
	» electronics (phones, laptops, audio devices)
	» bicycles
	» clothing and textiles
Burnaby Neighbourhood House launched the initiative 
in March 2025 and held six repair cafés throughout the 
year. The City funded and promoted two of these events 
and plans to support additional cafés moving forward.

36	 Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report
Waste collection app
Burnaby residents living in single family homes can 
stay informed and avoid missed collection days by 
using the City’s free Recycle Coach waste collection 
app. The app provides convenient access to the most 
current information on garbage, recycling and green 
bin services, and allows residents to set personalized 
reminders for their collection schedules.
Key app features include:
	» Collection day reminders, including alerts for holiday 
schedule changes and weather related disruptions.
	» Program details outlining accepted materials and 
preparation requirements for garbage, recycling and 
green bin collection.
	» Notifications about changes to recycling programs and 
accepted materials, including updates from Recycle BC.
	» A “What Goes Where?” searchable database 
offering drop off depot information and proper 
disposal instructions.
	» A “Report a Problem” tool for missed collections, 
illegal dumping and other service concerns.
Recycle Coach is available for both Apple and Android 
devices and can also be accessed through the 
Recycling & Garbage pages on the City’s website.
In 2025, the app had 21,221 total users and recorded 
4,362,150 interactions, equivalent to 142,554 interactions 
per 1,000 households. Usage increased across both the 
mobile and web platforms, reflecting strong engagement 
with the City’s waste services information.

In addition to providing collection details, the app 
enables the City to send direct messages to users 
about service disruptions, statutory holiday schedule 
changes and special events, such as the annual 
Christmas tree collection program. Table 10 outlines the 
types of users and usage patterns observed in 2025.
The Recycle Coach app provides several tools that 
allow residents to report issues, request assistance and 
communicate directly with the City regarding waste 
collection services. These features support quicker 
response times and help staff identify service challenges 
or patterns of concern across neighbourhoods.
In 2025, app based reporting trends showed an 
overall decrease in service related issues, indicating 
improved compliance, fewer missed collections and 
better understanding of set out requirements. At the 
same time, reports related to illegal dumping increased, 
reflecting greater public engagement in identifying and 
reporting problem areas.

Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report      37
Social media, web and print campaigns
Recycling and garbage services continue to be among the most frequently accessed topics 
on the City’s website. As part of the City’s web renewal project, the Solid Waste & Recycling 
category was prioritized as the first listing in the Services & Payments menu when the site was 
updated in August 2021—a placement that continued through 2025 and contributed to strong 
online engagement with waste related content.
In 2025, the City expanded its digital and print communications efforts, strengthening public 
awareness through consistent social media outreach and updated visual messaging on 
collection vehicles. Key highlights from the year include:
2025 Curbside Collection Calendar
	» The calendar’s format was updated and simplified 
to a double-sided, single-sheet layout that included 
both the full collection schedule and a recycling 
guide.
	» Distribution took place in late December 2024 
(adjusted due to a Canada Post strike).
	» Copies were delivered to all single family homes, 
duplexes and rowhouses, with additional copies 
available at City Hall, the Burnaby Eco-Centre, 
community centres and libraries.
	» The calendar was translated into nine languages, all 
available online: 
•	 Chinese (Simplified and Traditional)
•	 Farsi
•	 French
•	 Italian
•	 Korean
•	 Punjabi
•	 Spanish
•	 Tagalog
“Oops” Sticker Program
The “Oops” contamination stickers continued to be an 
effective educational tool. When contamination was 
observed on collection day, staff placed a sticker on 
the bin to identify the issue. A follow up contamination 
letter was then mailed to the residents, providing 
further guidance, examples of proper sorting and 
contact information for additional support. This two step 
approach remained a valuable method in reducing 
contamination and increasing waste diversion.

Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report      39
7.0 Looking Ahead to 2026
In 2026, the City plans to:
	» Host the third annual Spring Cleanup Event, continuing to 
provide residents with expanded opportunities to properly 
dispose of garbage, recyclables and reusable items.
	» Adapt and optimize waste and recycling operations to support 
new small-scale multi-unit housing developments and ensure 
the highest level of service consistency across all housing 
types.
	» Monitor the flexible plastics collection pilot in partnership with 
Recycle BC to determine its long term viability for Burnaby and 
evaluate the potential for broader implementation.
	» Continue supporting repair cafés and local reuse initiatives 
in collaboration with community partners to promote waste 
reduction through repair and material reuse.
	» Expand waste diversion and recycling outreach to additional 
schools, neighbourhoods and community groups to 
strengthen public awareness and participation.
	» Launch the “Recycling Rock Star” recognition initiative, 
offering positive reinforcement to residents who consistently 
demonstrate excellent recycling practices.
	» Continue research and testing of bear-resistant equipment 
compatible with the City’s collection vehicles to further reduce 
wildlife attractants and improve community safety.
	» Expand the three-stream streetscape bin program at transit 
stops and explore enclosure designs that provide flexibility 
in limited spaces while remaining compatible with automated 
collection trucks.
	» Grow the Adopt-a-Street program, encouraging more 
community volunteers to help keep Burnaby’s boulevards, 
lanes and public spaces clean.
	» Continue conducting waste composition audits, using the 
findings to refine education priorities and target contamination 
and diversion performance.
Staff at Spring Clean Up event
	» Plan for mattress and large item drop-off options at the Eco-
Centre to improve efficiency and increase diversion of bulky 
materials.
	» Implement emission reduction strategies for waste collection 
vehicles to support the City’s broader climate and carbon 
reduction goals.
	» Undertake industry engagement on proposed amendments 
to the Construction and Demolition Waste Diversion Bylaw, 
including:
•	 increasing the diversion target from 70% to 90%
•	 introducing a lumber salvage requirement for single  and 
two family homes built prior to 1950
These initiatives will help Burnaby continue making meaningful 
progress toward its waste reduction and sustainability goals while 
responding to the needs of a growing and changing community.
The City of Burnaby has consistently met emerging environmental challenges through innovation, 
strategic planning and a strong commitment to environmental protection. Looking ahead to 2026, 
the City will continue to strengthen its waste reduction and diversion programs through a series of 
planned initiatives and service enhancements. These efforts will build on the successes of recent 
years while supporting the City’s long term sustainability objectives.

COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
ENVIRONMENT COMMITTEE (EC) 
 
FROM: 
DEPUTY GENERAL MANAGER PARKS, RECREATION AND 
CULTURE  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
SU:ENHANCING FOOD SUSTAINABILITY IN BURNABY 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide an operational update on food sustainability measures 
within the City of Burnaby.  
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “SU: Enhancing Food Sustainability in Burnaby” dated April 
13, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
This report aligns with the Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) and Social Sustainability 
Strategy (2011). 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
On March 31, 2025, Environment Committee meeting a motion was adopted requesting 
staff to explore the feasibility of more plant-forward food options in city facilities and 
report back on new ways of informing, promoting and incentivizing patrons to choose 
more sustainable food options. The city operates several cafes and restaurants within 
civic facilities and parks, and over previous years have already introduced a range of 
practical measures to improve the availability of plant-based, vegetarian, vegan, and 
dairy-free options. 
At the same time, food-service operations must remain customer-focused, operationally 
sound, and financially responsible. For that reason, menu development continues to 
reflect customer demand while providing a variety of dietary options. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
Staff have adopted a balanced and operationally practical approach to food 
sustainability within City food-service operations. This approach focuses on maintaining 
customer choice, responding to demonstrable demand, and introducing or refining 
plant-based and vegetarian offerings where it is operationally feasible. 
675

Subject: SU: Enhancing Food Sustainability in Burnaby  
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 2 
 
3.1.1 Current Status 
Plant-based and vegetarian options have already been introduced at Riverway, Mintara 
a top Burnaby Mountain (“Mintara”) and other service locations. These offerings are 
included within regular menu planning so that customers have meaningful choice 
alongside traditional menu items. 
Additional vegetarian, vegan and dairy-free selections are also available through café 
and concession operations, including the Passion Coffee program. Where customers 
request dairy-free, vegetarian, or vegan accommodation that is not specifically listed on 
a menu, staff make reasonable efforts to address those requests when service 
conditions permit. 
Staff also continue to pursue local sourcing opportunities where feasible and to make 
operational choices that support sustainability objectives, including the use of an electric 
roasting system within the coffee program. 
3.1.2 Operating Approach 
Staff’s approach is deliberate and measured. The role of the food service operation is to 
provide customer choice and respond to demonstrated demand, while ensuring services 
remain operationally sound and financially responsible. 
Staff will continue to introduce, retain, and refine plant-based and vegetarian offerings 
where there is clear customer uptake, operational fit and acceptable menu performance. 
Staff will also continue to monitor demand and item performance by location, adjust 
offerings based on sales and feedback, accommodate reasonable dietary requests 
where feasible, and add further options incrementally where demand supports 
continued expansion. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
No specific communication or engagement is required as part of this report. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The city’s Food Services operations provide food and beverage options at civic facilities 
in a financially responsible manner while meeting customer demand. Staff have 
introduced plant-based food options across facilities at level aligned with current 
demand and continue to monitor interest in these offerings. As with any menu 
adjustments, expanding or promoting additional plant-forward options require careful 
consideration of potential impacts on revenue and costs. Food Services reviews menu 
performance and customer preferences to ensure an appropriate balance between 
financial sustainability and evolving dietary trends and will continue to evaluate 
opportunities accordingly.

COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
TRANSPORTATION COMMITTEE (TC) 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER ENGINEERING  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
CENTRAL BOULEVARD AND PARKADE INTERSECTION SAFETY 
IMPROVEMENTS  
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide Council with an overview of the proposed safety 
improvements at the intersection of Central Boulevard and Metropolis 
at Metrotown underground parkade access. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Central Boulevard and Parkade Intersection Safety 
Improvements” dated April 8, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The proposed safety improvements at the intersection of Central Boulevard and 
Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade access support the City of Burnaby’s 
Corporate Strategic Plan and goals for a safe, connected, healthy, and dynamic 
community. The project aligns with Council-adopted goals, policies, and targets 
identified in the Burnaby Transportation Plan including Vision Zero, and the City’s 
Climate Action Framework that supports the development of a sustainable, safe, and 
accessible transportation network. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Metrotown is one of Burnaby’s Town Centres and a key destination in the region. The 
growing residential population and sustained commercial activities have contributed to 
high pedestrian activities in the area. The signalized intersection of Central Boulevard 
and the Metropolis underground parkade access (the “project location”) provides a key 
connection between the SkyTrain station, bus loop and shopping centre for pedestrians 
and transit riders. The high pedestrian and vehicle traffic volumes have resulted in traffic 
safety concerns raised by the public and Metropolis. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
At the project location, the main pedestrian safety concerns pertain to conflicts between 
crossing pedestrians and turning vehicles. To mitigate these concerns, this project 
includes a new exclusive pedestrian signal phase (pedestrian scramble) as well as 
other intersection enhancements. Together, these improvements will enhance the safety 
environment at this location such that the measures are intuitive and inclusive for all 
users, as detailed below. 
679

Subject: Central Boulevard and Parkade Intersection Safety Improvements  
TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 2 
 
 
3.1 Pedestrian Scramble Signal Phase 
A pedestrian scramble phase is a type of traffic signal operation where all vehicular 
movements are stopped. During this signal phase, pedestrians can cross the 
intersection in all directions – including diagonally – while all vehicles are stopped. 
Vehicles can then proceed after the phase without any potential conflicts with 
crossing pedestrians. This time separation allows pedestrians to cross without 
conflicting vehicle movements and simplifies driver decision-making. 
3.2 Intersection Improvements 
Geometric enhancements are proposed to improve safety and accessibility for all 
road users. Curb ramp accessibility and pedestrian waiting areas will be improved 
together with more direct crossing alignments. The right-turn island on the northeast 
corner will be removed, which reduces the number of crossings needed for 
pedestrians, minimizing the exposure to traffic. Accessible pedestrian signals (APS) 
will also feature updated audible messaging to clearly indicate that pedestrians may 
cross in all directions during the pedestrian scramble signal phase. 
3.3 Project Timeline 
Detailed design is expected to be completed in April 2026. Construction is scheduled 
to commence shortly after with the completion targeted for Summer 2026. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
The project team will be providing stakeholders, including Metropolis at Metrotown, with 
notification ahead of construction. The project team will continue to address queries and 
concerns as they arise. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The funding for this project has been committed in the Engineering 5-year capital plan, 
2026 – 2030. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
May Phang, P.Eng., General Manager Engineering 
 
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS 
This report was prepared by Martin To, P.Eng., Transportation Engineer, and Keera 
Wang, P.Eng., PMP, Transportation Engineer, and reviewed by Eric Tam, P.Eng., PTOE, 
Senior Manager Transportation, and Amy Choh, P.Eng., PMP, Director Engineering 
Transportation.

at Section 2(2), by adding the following definitions in alphabetical order: 
 
““Cycle” means a device having any number of wheels that is propelled by human 
power and on which a person may ride and includes a motor assisted cycle, but does 
not include any device that is gas powered, a skateboard, roller skates or inline 
roller blades. 
 
“Electric Kick Scooter” has the meaning set out in the Electric Kick Scooter Pilot 
Project Regulation. 
 
“Crossride Pavement Markings” means a series of white rectangular or square 
pavement markings that delineates a crossing area where cycling is permitted. 
 
“Major Arterial” means a highway that provides primary mobility for people and 
goods through the City, connecting town centres and major destinations, and 
accommodates regional, inter-municipal, and intra-municipal trips, as generally 
shown in Burnaby’s Transportation Plan.

“Major Road Network” has the meaning set out in the South Coast British 
Columbia Transportation Authority Act. 
 
“Minor Arterial” means a highway that provides primary mobility for people and 
goods between major activity centres within the City, and accommodates both 
inter-municipal and intra-municipal trips, as generally shown in Burnaby’s 
Transportation Plan. 
 
“Motor Assisted Cycle” has the meaning set out in the Motor Vehicle Act. 
 
“Multi-Use Pathway” means a street, or portion of a street, designed for use by 
pedestrians, cycles, electric kick scooters, motor assisted cycles, and other non-
motorized rolling conveyances which is physically separated from a roadway used 
for vehicles. 
 
“Protected Cycle Lane” means a portion of a street, designed and designated 
exclusively for the use of cycles, electric kick scooters and motor assisted cycles 
which is separated from vehicle traffic by physical elements, signing, striping, 
and/or crossride pavement markings. 
 
“Shared Electric-Bike” means a motor assisted cycle that is publicly accessible and 
owned by a shared micromobility operator. 
 
“Shared Electric Kick Scooter” means an electric kick scooter that is publicly 
accessible and owned by a shared micromobility operator. 
 
“Shared Micromobility Device” means a shared electric-bike or shared electric kick 
scooter. 
 
“Shared Micromobility Parking Zone” means the area or space on a roadway 
designated by a traffic control device and established by the Engineer in accordance 
with Section 18A(1).

(b) 
which has a valid agreement with the City to authorize the shared   
 
 
micromobility operator to allow its members to park shared  
 
 
 
micromobility devices on a street.” 
(b) 
by adding the following after Section 18(2): 
 
“SHARED MICROMOBILITY DEVICES 
 
18A. (1) 
The Engineer may: 
(a) 
establish shared micromobility parking zones for the use of 
a shared micromobility operator and the parking of shared 
micromobility devices within the City;  
(b) 
designate such micromobility parking zones and the use of 
such micromobility parking zones by way of a traffic 
control device; and  
(c) 
enter into agreements with micromobility operators to 
regulate and control the operation of shared micromobility 
operators. 
(2) 
Shared micromobility parking zones shall be used exclusively for 
the parking of shared micromobility devices. 
(3) 
No person shall park a shared micromobility device, except within 
a shared micromobility parking zone. 
 
 
 
692

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 4 of 5 
 
 
CYCLES, MOTOR ASSISTED CYCLES AND ELECTRIC KICK SCOOTERS  
 
 
 
18B. 
No person shall ride a cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick scooter: 
 
(1) 
upon any sidewalk except where permitted by a traffic control 
device; 
 
(2) 
upon a street while wearing headphones, or any other  
manufactured device capable of transmitting sound, over or in close 
proximity to both ears, except that this prohibition shall not apply to 
the wearing of a device designed and worn for the purpose of 
improving the person’s ability to hear sounds emanating from 
outside of the device; 
 
(3) 
upon a street unless the cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick  
scooter is equipped with a bell capable of being used as a warning;  
or 
 
(4) 
in a marked crosswalk, unless it is also marked by crossride  
pavement markings on one or both sides of the crosswalk, or where 
cycles are permitted by a traffic control device. 
 
 
 
18C. 
(1) 
Subject to subsection (2), a person may ride a cycle, motor assisted  
 
 
cycle or electric kick scooter in an unmarked crosswalk. 
 
 
 (2) 
A person riding a cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick scooter 
through or out of a marked or unmarked crosswalk, must yield the 
right of way to pedestrians who are entering into, walking in or 
walking out of the crosswalk and for the purposes of this subsection, 
a marked crosswalk includes the area of the crosswalk delineated by 
crossride pavement markings. 
 
 
 
693

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 5 of 5 
 
 
18D. 
A person must not operate an electric kick scooter: 
 
(1) 
while on a street without due care and attention, or without 
reasonable consideration for other persons using the street; 
 
(2) 
in contravention of the “Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Project 
Regulation”; or 
(3) 
on a major road network or arterial road, except along a 
protected cycle lane, or multi-use pathway that is located within 
or adjacent to a major road network, major arterial or minor 
arterial.”

designated for such use.”

A BYLAW to establish tax rates upon all taxable 
land and improvements pursuant to 
sections 197 and 216 of the Community Charter 
  
WHEREAS Council must before the 15th day of May, subject to the Community 
Charter, adopt a bylaw to impose property value taxes for the year by establishing tax rates on 
all taxable land and improvements according to their assessed value to provide the money 
required for the purposes specified in section 197 of the Community Charter; and 
 
WHEREAS Council may impose a local service tax in relation to a business 
improvement area on land or improvements, or both, in accordance with section 216 of the 
Community Charter   
 
  
NOW THEREFORE the Council of the City of Burnaby ENACTS as follows: 
  
1. 
This Bylaw may be cited as BURNABY RATES BYLAW 2026. 
  
2. 
Property value taxes at the following tax rates are hereby imposed and levied for the year 
2026: 
 
(a) 
to provide for the municipal purposes set out in Schedule 1 of this 
Bylaw based on the assessed value of land and improvements 
taxable for municipal purposes, in accordance with the rates set out 
in Schedule 1 of this Bylaw;  
 
(b) 
to provide for regional district purposes based on the assessed value 
of land and improvements taxable for such purposes, in accordance 
with the rates set out in Schedule 2 of this Bylaw; and

to provide for business improvement area local area service tax

DELEGATIONS AND INVITED PRESENTATIONS 
6.1 
DELEGATION: New Westminster & District Labour Council - Day of 
Mourning 
Speaker: Sarah Bjorknas 
 
Sarah Bjorknas, representing the New Westminster & District Labour Council, 
appeared before Council to raise awareness of the National Day of Mourning.  
The speaker advised that each year, the Labour Council seeks support and 
recognition of April 28th as a National Day of Mourning for workers killed and 
injured on the job. 
The speaker further noted that in 2024, 136 people in British Columbia died as a 
result of their work. There were 6,593 claims reported for serious injuries. The 
speaker stressed that each of these numbers represents a member, a life 
changed forever or lost to be mourned by family and friends. Impacts from work 
place injuries go far beyond time spent at work. 
The speaker highlighted recent advances in tower crane safety noting the  
mandatory crane licensing and permitting program through WorkSafeBC. 
The speaker emphasized that Unions in BC have fought hard for gains in 
ensuring appropriate WorksafeBC coverage but know that prevention is the most 
important action to be taken, noting that licensing, permitting regulations, and 
strong enforcement are proven tools for reducing risk and further noted that 
strong unions make safer places. 
S. Bjorknas stated that workers across Canada are reminding communities that 
not all hazards are visible, and psychological health and safety is occupational 
health and safety.  
*Councilor Santiago arrived at 5:08 pm 
In conclusion, the speaker expressed the importance of collective action to  
improve workplace safety, and invited City Council and members of the public to 
the National Day of Mourning event being held on Tuesday, April 28, 2026 at     
11:00 am at Westminster Pier Park in New Westminster.

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ............................................. Page 2 
 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This report presents the proposed Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 (“Zoning Bylaw 2026”) 
for Council’s consideration and advancement to First and Second Readings. If adopted, 
Zoning Bylaw 2026 will replace the City’s current Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 (“Zoning 
Bylaw 1965”). The proposed bylaw (Attachment 1) is the culmination of a multiyear, 
multi-phase process and represents the City’s first comprehensive zoning review in 60 
years, serving as a key implementation tool for the Burnaby 2050 Official Community 
Plan (OCP). New Development Permit Area Guidelines (Attachment 2, Attachment 3, 
and Attachment 4), as enabled through the Burnaby 2050 OCP, are also presented as 
schedules to the proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026. Adoption of Zoning Bylaw 2026 will 
repeal Zoning Bylaw 1965, requiring many properties citywide to be assigned to the new 
zoning districts under Zoning Bylaw 2026. 
1.0   POLICY SECTION 
The proposed amendments outlined in this report align with the following provincial and 
municipal laws, bylaws, and policies, including: 
 
• Local Government Act (2015) 
• Metro Vancouver Regional Growth Strategy (2023) 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) 
• Climate Action Framework (2020)  
• Mayor’s Task Force on Community Housing Final Report (2019) 
• Burnaby Housing Needs Report (2021; interim update 2024) 
• Economic Development Strategy (2007) 
• HOME: Burnaby’s Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021) 
• Burnaby Transportation Plan (2021) 
• Urban Forest Strategy (2025) 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025) 
2.0  
BACKGROUND 
2.1  
Zoning Bylaw Rewrite 
In late 2023, the City initiated the Zoning Bylaw Rewrite project, the first comprehensive 
review and update of the City’s Zoning Bylaw in 60 years. The objectives of the Rewrite 
included:  
• Provide more flexible and modern regulations; 
• Reduce complexity to improve ease of understanding; 
• Enhance user-friendliness for residents, applicants, and City staff; and 
• Implement and align with City plans and policies, and Provincial legislation. 
 
 
23

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ............................................. Page 3 
 
Project Phasing 
The Zoning Bylaw Rewrite was carried out as a 4-phase project structured around a 
series of major amendments to implement the new Burnaby 2050 OCP land use 
framework and address new legislation on small-scale multi-unit housing, transit-
oriented areas, and other new development requirements. 
Phase 1 of the project introduced the R1 Small-Scale Multi-Unit Housing District, 
adopted by Council on June 10, 2024.  
Phase 2 introduced the next major amendment, adopted by Council on June 10, 2025, 
which introduced a new height-based development framework, including new interim 
multi-family residential and commercial districts. Related to this phase of work was the 
introduction of new Community Benefit Bonus and Inclusionary Rental requirements, 
adopted by Council on October 14, 2025. 
Phase 3 introduced five additional height-based zoning districts for employment, 
industrial, park, institutional, and agricultural land uses, which represented the 
remaining new zoning districts required to implement the Burnaby 2050 OCP land use 
framework. This major amendment was adopted by Council on March 10, 2026. 
The first three phases represented an interim state that allowed the City to continue its 
transition to the height-based framework and support early implementation of the new 
Burnaby 2050 OCP land use framework while the full Zoning Bylaw 2026 was still being 
finalized. 
Phase 4 Repeal and Replacement 
Phase 4 of the Zoning Bylaw Rewrite is intended to complete the transition to a new 
Zoning Bylaw 2026, including establishment of Development Permit Area (DPA) 
Guidelines to implement the Form and Character, Streamside Protection and 
Enhancement, and Tenant Protection DPAs designated in the Burnaby 2050 OCP. 
Phase 4, the culmination of the project as outlined in this report, introduces: 
• a new Zoning Bylaw 2026 document, with improved formatting, organization, and 
visual aids; 
• updates to the supplementary regulations of Zoning Bylaw 1965, including 
administrative and parking-related sections; 
• finalized versions of the zoning districts introduced since Phase 1; 
• new Development Permit Area (DPA) Guidelines that will form schedules to 
Zoning Bylaw 2026; and 
• a strategy for assigning new zoning districts to properties citywide that are 
currently zoned with districts that will be repealed with Zoning Bylaw 1965. 
The above-noted items would all come into effect through adoption of Zoning Bylaw 
2026, which would contain the new DPA Guidelines and an updated Official Zoning Map 
of Burnaby. The proposed effective date of the new Zoning Bylaw 2026 is July 1, 2026.

2.2  
Implementation Approach 
On June 10, 2025, Council adopted a height-based approach to development for the 
City of Burnaby, as outlined in the Council report titled “Proposed Bylaw Amendments – 
Height-Based Development Framework,” dated April 22, 2025. This direction introduced 
new height-based zoning districts aligned with the Burnaby 2050 OCP land use 
framework. At that time, Council also directed staff to facilitate height-based 
development applications through Comprehensive Development (CD) rezonings until 
the City’s new OCP and Zoning Bylaw 2026 (which includes the DPA Guidelines) were 
adopted. 
The height-based development framework shifts the City’s regulatory approach from 
density-based controls toward built form, with a greater emphasis on building height, 
massing, and the relationship of development to the public realm. While this framework 
prioritizes physical form, land use designations within the Burnaby 2050 OCP and 
corresponding zoning districts in Zoning Bylaw 2026 will continue to establish permitted 
uses. Working together, the OCP and Zoning Bylaw 2026 (which includes the DPA 
Guidelines) provide the primary tools for implementing this framework. 
Consistent with the Development Approval Process (DAP) project, the framework is 
intended to streamline development regulations, improve transparency, and better align 
development outcomes with the Burnaby 2050 OCP land use framework. The approach 
is designed to achieve the following objectives: 
• Increase transparency and clarity regarding permitted building height and form; 
• Simplify development regulations and the approvals process; 
• Support greater flexibility for mixed-use development; 
• Enhance urban design standards and the relationship between buildings and the 
public realm; 
• Align with the Burnaby 2050 OCP and new provincial legislation related to small-
scale multi-unit housing, development financing tools, and development in 
Transit-Oriented Areas; and 
• Maintain opportunities to support affordable housing. 
 
 
25

Subject: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw – Proposed Repeal and Replacement 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ............................................. Page 5 
 
To support these objectives, the framework introduces several key changes to the City’s 
development regulations, including: 
• Establishing permitted building heights within OCP land use designations and 
corresponding zoning districts; 
• Removing Floor Area Ratio (FAR) as a primary development control mechanism; 
• Introducing Form and Character DPA Guidelines as a key regulatory tool; 
• Aligning major planning policies with the new framework; 
• Reducing the number of zoning districts to improve clarity and efficiency; and 
• Enhancing opportunities for mixed-use development. 
With adoption of Zoning Bylaw 2026 (which includes the Form and Character DPA 
Guidelines) and repeal of Zoning Bylaw 1965 (which includes the former FAR-based 
zoning districts), the new land use framework will be fully implemented.

approach following adoption of Zoning Bylaw 2026.

2.2.1  Transition for In-Progress Applications 
Preliminary Plan Approvals 
To support the completion of recently approved and active applications made under 
Zoning Bylaw 1965, and also to provide clear direction on how much time an applicant 
has to act on an approved application, and when an application must be approved 
before it will be cancelled and a new application must be submitted under Zoning Bylaw 
2026, a one year transition period is proposed for issued and valid Preliminary Plan 
Approval (PPA) as well as active PPA applications. The following transition approach is 
proposed for these situations: 
• If, prior to the Zoning Bylaw 2026 effective date (July 1, 2026), a complete PPA 
application is received, or if a PPA was issued and remains valid on July 1, 2026, 
then Zoning Bylaw 2026 will not apply to the land that is the subject of the PPA 
application or issued PPA for 12 months (until July 1, 2027) and instead Zoning 
Bylaw 1965 will apply.  
o The related building permit (BP) must be applied for within 12 months of 
the effective date of Zoning Bylaw 2026 (on or before July 1, 2027), after 
which the PPA under Zoning Bylaw 1965 will cease to be valid and the 
submission of a new application under Zoning Bylaw 2026 and the new 
DPA Guidelines will be required. 
Rezoning Applications 
The approach for the management of rezoning applications made before the adoption of 
Burnaby 2050 OCP is outlined in section 4.3 of the Council report titled “Burnaby 2050 
Official Community Plan – Final Version”, dated September 9, 2025, and is as follows: 
• If a rezoning application was made prior to the adoption of Burnaby 2050 OCP 
(Dec. 9, 2025) and it does not align with new OCP, then it would need to be 
adopted by Dec. 9, 2026, after which it would be cancelled and a new rezoning 
application will be required, which will be subject to Burnaby 2050 OCP (per 
September 2025 OCP report), and Zoning Bylaw 2026 and new DPA Guidelines.

Rezoning Bylaws for In-Progress Rezoning Applications 
As of July 1, 2026, being the effective date of Zoning Bylaw 2026 and repeal of Zoning 
Bylaw 1965, a number of amendment bylaws of Zoning Bylaw 1965 for in-progress 
rezoning applications (“in-progress rezoning bylaws”) will have received at least one 
reading but not been adopted.  Provided there are no changes to the land use 
regulations applicable to the proposed development on the lands under an in-progress 
rezoning bylaw, it is proposed that minor alterations be made to the in-progress 
rezoning bylaw as may be required to allow the bylaw to be adopted under Zoning 
Bylaw 2026 as follows: 
• For those in-progress rezoning bylaws that have received first or second reading 
as of July 1, 2026, it is proposed that minor alterations be made to the text of the 
in-progress rezoning bylaw either between first and second reading, or between 
second and third reading, as applicable, of the in-progress rezoning bylaw   
• For those in-progress rezoning bylaws that have received third reading as of 
July 1, 2026, it is proposed that Council rescind third reading of the in-progress 
rezoning bylaw and give third reading to the in-progress rezoning bylaw, as 
amended.

Forthcoming Development Procedures Bylaw 
A subsequent report will be brought forward to Council to introduce a new Burnaby 
Development Procedures Bylaw to repeal and replace the current Development 
Procedures Bylaw 2022 (Bylaw No. 14427). Under section 460 of the Local Government 
Act, a local government that adopts an OCP or Zoning Bylaw must define procedures 
for applications to amend the OCP or Zoning Bylaw or permits under Part 14 of the 
Local Government Act (such as development permits, development variance permits, 
temporary use permits, etc.). Currently these procedures are separately set out in the 
Burnaby OCP, Zoning Bylaw 1965, and Development Procedures Bylaw 2022. It is 
proposed that these and other types of development applications be set out in a 
consolidated Development Procedures Bylaw. This consolidated approach will support 
implementation of Zoning Bylaw 2026 and the City’s updated development framework.

3.1  
Proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026 
3.1.1  Zoning Bylaw 2026 Document 
The proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026 will replace the City’s current Zoning Bylaw 1965 and 
earlier Zoning Bylaw Rewrite amendments with a fully consolidated and modernized 
regulatory document that introduces a clearer and more intuitive document structure. 
Improvements include enhanced formatting, a clearer hierarchy of information, and the 
increased use of visual aids such as tables and diagrams. Large blocks of text present 
in Zoning Bylaw 1965 have been reorganized into shorter sections with clearer 
headings and sub-sections to improve readability and interpretation for applicants, staff, 
and the public. 
Document Structure 
The proposed bylaw is organized into the following major Parts: 
• Part 1 Administration 
• Part 2 Definitions 
• Part 3 Zoning Districts 
• Part 4 General Regulations 
• Part 5 Rental Housing and Bonus Incentives 
• Part 6 Use-Specific Regulations 
• Part 7 Parking, Loading, and Mobility 
• Schedules: Development Permit Area Guidelines 
Part 1 Administration 
The Administration section consolidates introductory provisions formerly located 
throughout Sections 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 of Zoning Bylaw 1965, to establish the 
administrative framework for Zoning Bylaw 2026, and includes enforcement provisions, 
and interpretation rules. Part 1 also contains the new Official Zoning Map of Burnaby 
that reflects proposed zoning district reassignment as further described in section 3.3 of 
this report. 
Part 2 Definitions 
The Definitions section will be updated primarily to remove outdated terms no longer in 
use, improve clarity and consistency of existing definitions, and introduce new 
definitions required to support the updated regulatory framework and DPA Guidelines. 
Part 3 Zoning Districts 
Part 3 will contain the City’s new zoning districts, which Council previously adopted as 
new interim zoning districts in Zoning Bylaw 1965. These districts will be reformatted 
within the new Zoning Bylaw 2026 and include minor adjustments to align with the 
proposed DPA Guidelines and improve consistency with other regulatory updates.

Part 5 Rental Housing and Bonus Incentives 
The Rental Housing and Bonus Incentives section will contain the City’s inclusionary 
rental housing and community benefit bonus provisions. No changes are proposed to 
existing community benefit bonus provisions, but changes are proposed to the 
inclusionary rental requirements to incorporate provisions for rental replacement units. 
These changes were first introduced at Planning and Development Committee (PDC) in 
the report titled “Tenant Protection Framework and Tenant Protection Bylaw and 
Associated Bylaw Updates,” dated March 31, 2026. 
The following requirements, previously part of Stream 1 of the Rental Use Zoning Policy 
(RUZP), are proposed to be carried forward without change: 
• returning tenants shall be offered a replacement rental unit with the same 
number of bedrooms as the unit they occupied on the eligibility date; and 
• returning tenants will pay the same rent they paid on the eligibility date, plus any 
annual increases permitted under the Residential Tenancy Act (RTA). 
However, changes are proposed in relation to second tenancy, where a TPB-eligible 
tenant does not return or later moves out of their replacement rental unit.  
Under the RUZP (Stream 1), once a replacement unit became vacant, it reverts to an 
inclusionary unit with rent set at 20% below CMHC median rents. Under the proposed 
update to inclusionary rental requirements in Zoning Bylaw 2026, once a replacement 
rental unit is no longer rented to a returning tenant, it will revert to the requirements of 
the inclusionary rental provisions in Part 5 of Zoning Bylaw 2026 instead of the RUZP. 
The proposed inclusionary rental provisions outline:  
1. Where the number of replacement rental units exceeds the required inclusionary 
obligation: 
• At second tenancy, replacement rental units shall be designated as 
inclusionary units until the required inclusionary threshold is met.  
• Any remaining replacement units beyond the required inclusionary threshold 
shall, at second tenancy, be designated as market rental units.  
2. Where the number of replacement rental units is less than the inclusionary rental 
unit obligation:  
• Additional units shall be designated as inclusionary units to achieve the 
required inclusionary rental unit threshold (as described in Option 2B in the 
financial impact evaluation included as Attachment 6).  
3. Where no inclusionary housing requirement applies to the development:  
• At second tenancy, all replacement rental units shall be designated as market 
rental units. 
Attachment 7 provides an example illustrating how inclusionary and rental replacement 
requirements would be applied at both initial occupancy and second tenancy. The 
example demonstrates how units are designated as inclusionary, replacement, or

are further outlined in section 3.1.2 below. 
Schedules: Development Permit Area Guidelines

Guidelines, as further described in section 3.2 of this report. 
3.1.2  Proposed Changes in Response to Committee of the Whole Feedback 
The following summarizes key changes resulting from Committee of the Whole 
feedback on February 23, 2026. 
Building Separations 
Within Zoning Bylaw 1965, Section 6.3 contains provisions for minimum separation 
between buildings on the same lot (not including the R1 District). These provisions 
establish a wide range of separation requirements—from 1.8 m to over 30 m—
depending on factors such as building height, whether walls contain windows, and 
whether the building is residential or non-residential. For a typical windowed multi-family 
residential building, the Zoning Bylaw 1965 applies three different sets of requirements 
depending on building height and zoning district. Buildings up to three storeys require a 
minimum separation of 7.5 m, while buildings taller than three storeys are subject to a 
formula that increases separation with height. In the RM4 and RM5 Districts, buildings 
above the sixth storey must provide a fixed face-to-face tower separation of 30 m.

In practice, these provisions are complex to interpret and do not align well with 
contemporary development practices. Because the requirements vary depending on 
several factors and require calculations to determine compliance, they are frequently 
modified through Comprehensive Development (CD) rezonings to achieve appropriate 
built form outcomes. 
Zoning Bylaw 2026 introduces simplified building separations that establish a consistent 
set of baseline distances applicable across multiple zoning districts. Rather than relying 
on formulas, the new requirements apply discrete separation distances based on storey 
ranges that correspond with common building components such as podiums, towers, 
and transitional floors between these forms. These distances are based on a review of 
existing and recently approved development projects in Burnaby and other Canadian 
cities, such as Vancouver and Toronto, and are generally consistent with separation 
standards achieved through recent CD rezonings. The new provisions will apply to 
buildings located both on the same lot and on adjacent lots. 
In response to feedback from Committee of the Whole, building separations have been 
differentiated between residential and commercial buildings and are proposed to be 
increased by 3 m to 10 m for most storey ranges, with the greatest setbacks for 
residential buildings.

The updated framework maintains the objective of supporting privacy and access to 
light while allowing greater flexibility in building design. For example, podium storeys (up 
to the first six storeys) may be permitted to share a party wall with no separation where 
appropriate, reflecting conditions commonly seen in low-rise buildings and the podiums 
of taller buildings along commercial corridors. 
While Zoning Bylaw 2026 establishes clear baseline separation distances, the Form and 
Character DPA Guidelines provide additional design guidance and allow site-specific 
refinement through the development permit process. For example, proposed guidelines 
will specifically address privacy considerations through design strategies such as 
window placement, glazing treatments, screening elements, and landscape buffers. 
Minimum Permeable Lot Area 
To help advance the City’s adopted Urban Forest Strategy and OCP, new requirements 
for landscaping were proposed and discussed at Committee of the Whole. 
This included reframing existing maximum impervious surface area requirements, which 
currently only apply to the R1 District, to minimum permeable area requirements, and 
introducing new minimum permeable area requirements in all other zoning districts. 
In response to feedback from Committee of the Whole, the proposed minimum 
permeable area, as a percentage of total lot area, increased from 5% to 10% for the 
Industrial (I1) District:

For all permeability requirements, at least half the permeable area must be provided as 
vegetated area (cannot be permeable pavers or other porous hardscape). Where there 
is an overlay Zoning District, the lower requirement between the Zoning Districts will 
apply. Vegetated areas (i.e. areas planted with trees and shrubs) contribute additional 
environmental benefits including evapotranspiration, air filtration, habitat, and reduced 
energy consumption through shade; and are therefore proposed to be credited at a 
higher rating than permeable surfaces on their own. 
R1 District Tree Requirements 
Proposed minimum tree requirements for R1 District lots were previously discussed with 
Committee of the Whole. In response to Committee feedback, staff will continue to 
review these requirements alongside updates to the Tree Bylaw and other Urban Forest 
Strategy implementation measures, to be brought forward to Council at a later date. 
In the interim, an incentive-based approach is proposed for the R1 District to support 
tree retention. Areas identified as tree protection zones within an approved tree 
management plan may be credited toward the required minimum permeable lot area at 
a rate of 1.6 times the protected area. Additional details are provided in Part 4, Section 
4.3 of Attachment 1. 
Parking 
Recent parking studies (e.g. from NAIOP, Bunt & Associates, and UDI) and 
consideration of changes made to parking rates in other Metro Vancouver municipalities 
generally suggest that current minimum required parking and loading rates in Burnaby, 
particularly for non-residential uses such as commercial, employment, and industrial 
properties, are resulting in parking space oversupply. 
At the February 23, 2026, Committee of the Whole meeting, staff presented proposed 
reduced parking rates for various uses including commercial uses and office/healthcare 
facility uses for sites located within a Transit-Oriented Area (TOA) or Frequent Transit 
Network Area (FTNA).  
37

Separate rates for these uses are proposed given that office and healthcare facilities will 
be permitted widely outside of commercial zones (e.g. in the E1 and P2 Districts) as 
principal uses, including outside TOAs and FTNAs. 
For Employment and Industrial uses, the Committee discussed whether reduced 
parking rates for larger sites (i.e. sites exceeding 9,290 m2) should be limited to certain 
areas, citing concerns about potential parking spill-over effects into adjacent residential 
neighbourhoods. Specifically, employment and industrial areas in the Big Bend and 
Lake City (north of Lougheed Highway) community plan areas were identified as areas 
where further reduced parking rates for larger sites could be considered given they are 
located further or otherwise more isolated from residential areas, thus generating less 
concern about potential parking spill-over effects. 
Required parking rates for civic uses were also discussed at Committee of the Whole, 
with concerns raised about not requiring minimum parking for such uses. Based on this 
discussion, staff have proposed a minimum rate of 1 space per 33.3 m2 for civic uses.

3.1.3  Other Supporting Changes 
The following summarizes supporting changes proposed to all sections of Zoning Bylaw 
2026 relative to the equivalent sections of Zoning Bylaw 1965 in support of the City’s 
new development framework and are detailed in Attachment 1. 
Part 1 Administration 
Proposed Part 1, Administration, consolidates introductory provisions formerly located in 
Sections 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 of Zoning Bylaw 1965. 
The following key changes are proposed: 
• Add transitional provisions for in-progress PPA applications to provide a 1-year 
period following the effective date of the Zoning Bylaw 2026 (proposed to be July 
1, 2026) for applications to receive final approval. 
• Remove former Zoning Bylaw 1965 purpose statement. 
• Remove provisions that will be addressed in the Burnaby Development 
Procedures Bylaw relating to administration of PPAs (being replaced by new 
Development Permit process), the Zoning Bylaw amendment process, and fees. 
• Insert the Official Zoning Map reflecting assignment of new zoning districts as 
described in section 3.3 of this report. 
• Add clarifying interpretation provisions on how numeric requirements should be 
rounded and how certain common bylaw language should be interpreted (e.g. 
when “including” is used in a provision). 
Part 2 Definitions 
The Definitions section of Zoning Bylaw 2026 has been streamlined and modernized, 
reducing the total number of defined terms while improving clarity and flexibility. A total 
of 30 new definitions have been introduced to support emerging policy areas, including 
environmental protection, tenant protection, and the City’s updated development 
framework. 
At the same time, 64 legacy definitions—primarily highly specific or outdated land use 
categories—have been removed or consolidated into broader use classifications. Many 
retained definitions have also been refined to improve clarity, consistency, and 
alignment with contemporary planning practices.
