 SUBJECT:  PROPOSED AMENDMENT TO THE BURNABY TOWN CENTRE 
DESIGN STANDARDS TO INCORPORATE PROTECTED 
INTERSECTION GEOMETRY 
RECOMMENDATION: 
THAT the proposed amendments to the Burnaby Town Centre Design 
Standards, as outlined in the Discussion section of the proposed motion titled 
“Proposed Amendment to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards to 
Incorporate Protected Intersection Geometry”, dated April 8, 2026, of the 
Transportation Committee meeting, be endorsed. 
REPORT 
The Transportation Committee, at its meeting held on April 8, 2026, received and 
adopted the attached proposed motion from Resident Representative Zanotto seeking 
endorsement to amend the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards to incorporate 
protected intersection geometry, improve road safety for pedestrians and cyclists and 
support all-ages-and-abilities mobility. 
On behalf of the Transportation 
Committee, 
Councillor R. T. Lee 
Chair 
Councillor D. Tetrault 
Vice Chair 
458

 
Meeting April 8, 2026  
PROPOSED MOTION 
 
TO: 
TRANSPORTATION COMMITTEE 
 
FROM: 
MORENO ZANOTTO, RESIDENT REPRESENTATIVE 
 
SUBJECT: 
PROPOSED AMENDMENT TO THE BURNABY TOWN CENTRE 
DESIGN STANDARDS TO INCORPORATE PROTECTED 
INTERSECTION GEOMETRY 
 
PURPOSE: 
To seek endorsement to amend the Burnaby Town Centre Design 
Standards to incorporate protected intersection geometry, improving 
road safety for pedestrians and cyclists and supporting all-ages-and-
abilities mobility. 
 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the proposed amendments to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards, as 
outlined in the Discussion section of the proposed motion titled “Proposed Amendment 
to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards to Incorporate Protected Intersection 
Geometry”, dated April 8, 2026, be endorsed. 
 
BACKGROUND 
Alignment with Committee Mandate 
This Proposed Motion is situated firmly within the Transportation Committee’s Terms of 
Reference, which empower the Committee to advise Council on long-range road and 
active transportation network development, transportation-supportive policies, active 
transportation improvements, and road-safety upgrades. The Motion requests Council’s 
endorsement for a policy-level update to City design standards, which is consistent with 
that advisory role.  
Policy Context 
 
In 2020, Council adopted the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards. Implementation 
of the street typologies would occur over time during property redevelopments adjacent 
to Town Centre streets within Burnaby’s four Town Centres: Brentwood, Metrotown, 
Lougheed, and Edmonds. 
 
These Town Centre Design Standards included protected cycle tracks. A cycle track is a 
type of cycling facility that provides a cycling zone physically separate from motor 
vehicles and separate from people walking on the sidewalk (Image 1). 
 
459

Proposed Motion: Proposed Amendment to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards o Incorporate 
Protected Intersection Geometry 
  TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 2 
 
 
Image 1: Example of a Town Centre street cross-section under the current Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards, 
illustrating the allocation of space for walking, cycling (#4), and motor vehicle travel along a Town Centre corridor. 
Source: City of Burnaby, Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards. 
 
 
Burnaby’s Transportation Plan, adopted in 2021, commits the City of Burnaby to Vision 
Zero and mode-shift targets. Creating supportive active transportation environments is 
an important precursor to a shift from car use to active transport. This Motion directly 
supports the Burnaby Transportation Plan’s goal of safe and secure mobility by 
enhancing the safety and comfort of people walking, rolling, and cycling. It embeds 
proven safety-forward intersection design into the design of Town Centre streets as they 
are built through redevelopment.  
 
 
DISCUSSION 
Safer intersections are essential to creating walkable, welcoming, and people-centred 
streets. Evidence and best practices show that protected intersections reduce conflicts 
and improve safety for people walking and cycling by improving visibility, simplifying 
movements, and lowering vehicle turning speeds. These features create more 
predictable crossings for vulnerable road users and directly support the City’s 
commitment to Vision Zero and safe, accessible mobility for all ages and abilities. 
460

Proposed Motion: Proposed Amendment to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards o Incorporate 
Protected Intersection Geometry 
  TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 3 
 
Canadian cities including Vancouver, Victoria, Calgary, and Ottawa have integrated 
protected intersection geometry into their All-Ages-and-Abilities (AAA) cycling networks, 
reporting reductions in turning-movement conflicts and improved user comfort. 
 
Design Rationale and Application to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards 
 
Current cycle track design truncates physical separation between people cycling and 
motor-vehicle traffic on the approach to intersections, directing cyclists into shared traffic 
lanes at the most conflict-prone locations (Image 2). The gap in physical protection at 
intersections can be addressed by using a protected intersection geometry. 
 
 
Image 2: Existing Town Centre intersection condition showing a cycle track transitioning into a shared conflict zone 
with turning motor-vehicle traffic at the intersection. This condition illustrates the loss of physical protection for 
people cycling at the most conflict-prone location. 
 
 
Protected intersections consist of four key elements: a forward stop bar for cyclists, a 
set-back cycle crossing (about one vehicle length), corner refuge islands, and 
cycle-friendly signal phasing. Together, these geometric elements and signal phases 
create a protected, highly visible waiting position for people cycling and naturally lower 
turning speeds, consistent with an all-ages-and-abilities active-transportation network 
(Image 3). 
 
Protected intersection geometry can be employed at newly constructed intersections on 
four- and six-lane streets within Burnaby’s four Town Centres. 
 
461

Proposed Motion: Proposed Amendment to the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards o Incorporate 
Protected Intersection Geometry 
  TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 4 
 
 
Image 3: Example of a protected intersection in Vancouver, BC, illustrating set-back cycle crossings, corner refuge 
islands, and protected waiting areas that improve visibility, reduce turning conflicts, and enhance safety for 
pedestrians and cyclists. 
 
Summary 
Protected intersections offer a proven, people-centred approach to reducing collisions 
and creating safer, more intuitive crossings. Updating the Burnaby Town Centre Design 
Standards to include protected intersection geometry aligns the city with established 
North American practice and reflects evidence that protected intersections reduce 
conflicts and increase comfort for vulnerable road users. 
 
FINANCIAL IMPACTS 
There are no new costs to the City when implementing these changes as streetscape 
improvements in Town Centres are funded by developers as part of redevelopment. 
Protected intersection geometry primarily consists of revised curb radii, set-back 
distances, and refuge island placement; elements that are geometric adjustments rather 
than added materials. Updating the Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards now will 
also avoid future retrofit costs. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
Moreno Zanotto, Citizen Representative, Transportation Committee

COUNCIL REPORT 
 
TO: 
MAYOR & COUNCILLORS 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER PLANNING AND DEVELOPMENT  
SUBJECT: 
BRENTWOOD PARK SSMUH MOTION – RESTRICTIVE COVENANTS 
AND STATUTORY BUILDING SCHEMES 
 
PURPOSE: 
To respond to Council’s September 9, 2025, motion regarding exempting 
Brentwood Park properties with Restrictive Covenants from Provincial 
SSMUH requirements. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion – Restrictive Covenants 
and Statutory Building Schemes”, dated April 28, 2026, be received for 
information.  
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
On September 9, 2025, Council adopted a resolution to review all bylaw and other 
regulatory amendments to have the Brentwood Park neighbourhood exempted from the 
Province’s SSMUH mandates until Restrictive Covenants have been cleared by the 
owner/developer.   
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are private property interests 
registered on title that the City is not party to.  The City of Burnaby is bound by 
Provincial law and regulations to zone for SSMUH development, and Restrictive 
Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are not included as exemptions from 
SSMUH requirements.   
Under the Community Charter’s authority to regulate buildings and structures, Council 
may direct an amendment to the Burnaby Building Bylaw to require compliance with 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes as part of Building Permit 
approval.  Such a requirement would have to apply City-wide to all properties with 
registered Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes, including residential, 
commercial, industrial, etc.  Implementation of this requirement would result in added 
challenges, including limitations to suites and duplexes, as well as increased application 
cost and process time for the City and applicants and impacts on different types of 
properties and in many parts of the City outside the Brentwood Park residential 
neighbourhood identified in the September 9, 2025, resolution.  For these reasons it is 
not recommended by staff. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
The report provided aligns with the following provincial and municipal laws, bylaws, and 
policies, including: 
463

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
• Local Government Act (2015) 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) 
• Burnaby Housing Needs Report (2021; interim update 2024) 
• HOME: Burnaby’s Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021) 
• Mayor’s Task Force on Community Housing Final Report (2019) 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025) 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
2.1  Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion 
At the September 9, 2025 ,Open Council meeting, Council adopted the following 
motion: 
WHEREAS the Brentwood Park neighbourhood is an established neighbourhood 
with a distinct character that is protected by Restrictive Covenants that do not 
contemplate the kinds of development generated by provincial mandates related 
to Small‑Scale Multi‑Unit Housing (SSMUH);  
THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT staff move forward expeditiously with all 
bylaw and other regulatory amendments to have the Brentwood Park 
neighbourhood exempted from the Province’s SSMUH until Restrictive 
Covenants have been cleared by the owner/developer. 
The motion directs staff to explore mechanisms to exempt Brentwood Park from 
SSMUH mandates on the basis of existing Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building 
Schemes registered on title. This report provides information on Restrictive Covenants 
and Statutory Building Schemes generally, the potential to amend the Building Bylaw to 
require compliance with a Statutory Building Scheme or Restrictive Covenant 
requirement relating to construction as part of Building Permit issuance, and the 
practical implications of including such a requirement in the Building Bylaw.   
2.2  Overview of Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are private property interests 
registered on title under the Land Title Act to allow private parties (as opposed to 
municipalities and other public authorities) to restrict the use and character of a group of 
properties by regulating matters such as minimum lot sizes, building setbacks, 
architectural character, land use restrictions, types of dwellings, etc. Restrictive 
Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes were particularly popular in the 1950s and 
1960s prior to the adoption of most comprehensive zoning bylaws.  The Brentwood 
Park neighbourhood contains a significant number of lots encumbered by Restrictive 
Covenants or Statutory Building Schemes dating from this era. Although they are 
seldom used at this time, Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes remain 
valid under the Land Title Act and continue to apply to properties where these charges 
are registered on title and not subsequently discharged.  
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes exist in many other Burnaby 
neighbourhoods, including but not limited to Capitol Hill, Suncrest, Deer Lake, Garden 
464

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
Village, Government Road, and Lochdale.  The exact location and total number of 
affected properties is currently unknown.  Many of these Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes are outdated, ambiguous, and were intended by the initial 
developer/subdivider to apply to the use and character of early buildings within a 
development in order to maintain the development’s consistency, primarily as a 
marketing feature for future sales within the area.   
2.3  Applicability and Enforceability  
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are private property rights and 
represent obligations imposed on property owners that run with the land, i.e. are 
enforceable against future owners of a property even though they did not enter into the 
agreement with the initial developer/subdivider.  Where the initial party no longer exists 
(which is often the case), Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes can be 
enforced by the owners of adjacent properties with the same charges on title if they 
wish to maintain the requirements under the charges.  The City is not party to the 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes and cannot enforce them 
directly.  Currently, City staff review land titles as part of Planning (i.e. Rezoning, 
Preliminary Plan Approval and Subdivision) and Building Permit applications and 
notifies applicants of existing Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes on 
title and encourages the owners to resolve obligations under these charges prior to 
advancing development and/or construction. As part of the Building Department’s 
current Building Permit application process, the applicant is required to sign a 
declaration acknowledging that the City is not a party to the private charges and that 
issuance of a Building Permit does not absolve the applicant from their legal 
responsibilities under the charge.  To date, such acknowledgements have not precluded 
SSMUH development.  Other municipalities within Metro Vancouver take similar 
approaches and do not enforce requirements under Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes as part of their Building Permit process.  
2.4  Provincial SSMUH Legislation 
The Province’s SSMUH legislation passed in December 2023 requires municipalities to 
permit SSMUH developments—such as triplexes, fourplexes, and small multiplexes — 
on most residential lots formerly zoned for single family and duplex dwellings. The 
legislation is mandatory and sets minimum numbers of dwelling units that must be 
permitted under municipal zoning bylaws based on lot size and other criteria.  The City’s 
zoning bylaw cannot contradict these mandatory requirements.  There are limited 
exemptions under the Provincial SSMUH legislation and regulation, and Restrictive 
Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are not included as permitted exemptions.  
However, the Province has made it clear that Statutory Building Schemes remain valid, 
and it is the responsibility of the property owner to uphold the restrictions in the building 
scheme.  The Provincial Policy Manual & Site Standards for SSMUH (Provincial Policy 
Manual) provides the following guidance in respect to Statutory Building Schemes (at 
pages 25-26):  
Statutory building schemes are another form of restriction registered on a 
parcel’s title that could impact the potential to achieve the residential densities 
465

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 4 
 
prescribed by the SSMUH legislation. Statutory building schemes are generally 
reciprocal, in that the restrictions on each lot are imposed for the benefit of the 
other lots in the development. 
Restrictions imposed by the building scheme run with the land and bind future 
owners/renters in the subdivision. Typical restrictions or requirements deal with 
building sizes, styles, finishes or colors, but can also restrict the use of buildings. 
Local governments are not generally party to, or responsible for the 
administration of the building scheme. 
Provided the building scheme is valid, an existing statutory building scheme 
registered on title that limits the use of a property to one dwelling unit will take 
precedence over the unit densities prescribed through zoning updates made in 
accordance with the SSMUH legislation. This does not prevent a local 
government from zoning land subject to a statutory building scheme for a higher 
density, but the first responsibility of the owner(s) of that land is to uphold the 
terms of the building scheme. 
The Province has been monitoring the situation with regards to Restrictive Covenants 
and Statutory Building Schemes that restrict the full implementation of the Provincial 
SSMUH requirements.  However, changes to legislation or regulation have yet to be made 
overriding such charges.  
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1  Potential Building Bylaw Amendments 
In respect to Brentwood Park, Council’s specific motion at the September 9, 2026, Open 
meeting was as follows:   
THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT staff move forward expeditiously with all 
bylaw and other regulatory amendments to have the Brentwood Park 
neighbourhood exempted from the Province’s SSMUH until Restrictive 
Covenants have been cleared by the owner/developer. 
Due to lack of legislative authority, staff are unable to implement the direction in 
Council’s motion.  Notably, a bylaw amendment relating to Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes cannot address the Brentwood Park neighbourhood only 
and the City cannot compel property owners to discharge a private charge.  
As an alternative, it could be within the Council’s authority to regulate buildings and 
structures (under s. 8 of the Community Charter) to amend the Burnaby Building Bylaw 
to allow a Building Permit to be refused if it does not comply with a Restrictive Covenant 
or Statutory Building Scheme registered on title.  In this case, the recourse for an 
applicant would be that they comply with the conditions of the Restrictive Covenant or 
Statutory Building Scheme, or seek to have the charge removed from title (either 
through a Court process or consent of the original charge holder or all applicable 
adjacent property owners subject to the same Restrictive Covenant or Statutory Building 
Scheme) prior to advancing a Building Permit application.  
466

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 5 
 
Should Council wish to proceed with directing staff to bring forward an amendment to 
the Building Bylaw, the requirement would need to apply throughout the City as a 
condition in the Building Bylaw could not be limited to one geographic area.  As such, if 
the bylaw amendment were implemented, all properties with Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes throughout the City would be required to demonstrate 
compliance with conditions contained in those charges or have the charges removed 
from title as part of advancing a Building Permit application.   
3.1.1  Potential Planning Considerations / Challenges 
With respect to broader planning policy considerations and challenges, the enforcement 
of third-party Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes that restrict the 
development of R1 – SSMUH Development would be inconsistent with the following 
bylaws, laws and regulations which permit and/or require SSMUH development on all 
residential lots.  
• Burnaby Zoning Bylaw R1 SSMUH District 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan SSMUH Designations 
• Local Government Act, Section 481.3 [zoning bylaws and small scale multi-family 
housing] 
• Local Government Zoning Bylaw Regulation  
Notwithstanding, as Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes are not 
registered on all lots, and since they are private charges and owners have the 
opportunity to seek the discharge of these charges, should Council direct staff to make 
amendments to the Building Bylaw as discussed in section 3.1 above, it would not be in 
direct conflict with the above-referenced bylaws, legislation and regulations.   
It is noted, however, that upholding Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building 
Schemes that the City is not party to, may be interpreted by other neighbourhoods 
subject to SSMUH legislation that Council is seeking additional means of restricting 
SSMUH development more broadly outside of the conditions to exempt SSMUH 
development set within the Local Government Act and Local Government Zoning Bylaw 
Regulation.  This may lead to additional requests of other neighbourhoods to further 
restrict SSMUH development in Burnaby, creating greater pressure on the City not to 
align with the intent of the Provincial SSMUH legislation.  Further action in this regard 
would not only frustrate SSMUH development, which broadly is intended to allow for 
greater housing choice in accordance with the findings of the Mayor’s Task Force on 
Community Housing, Home Strategy, and the City’s Housing Choices program, but may 
restrict the ability to develop suites or duplexes which are also limited by such 
covenants. Further, the application of such a compliance requirement in the Building 
Bylaw will affect properties throughout the City beyond lots zoned for SSMUH, i.e. 
commercial and industrial properties with Restrictive Covenants or Statutory Building 
Schemes registered on title. The requirements under those charges may not align with 
the intent of the City’s commercial, employment and industrial zoning districts, 
especially following the implementation of proposed Zoning Bylaw 2026 as part of the 
Zoning Bylaw Rewrite project.  
467

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 6 
 
3.1.2  Potential Building Considerations / Challenges 
There are some significant administrative challenges for the Building Division if 
compliance with Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes registered on 
title is a requirement of Building Permit approval. While the City is aware of multiple 
neighbourhoods and properties with Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building 
Schemes (mostly from previous applications relating to properties with these charges on 
title), we do not have, nor can we simply obtain, a comprehensive list of the potentially 
thousands of affected properties.  Further to this, the Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes may contain conditions and restrictions that are not subject 
to City reviews, including landscape standards, material standards, and use conditions 
that are not checked by the Building Division.  Further some Restrictive Covenants and 
Statutory Building Schemes relate to commercial and industrial properties outside the 
scope of SSMUH controls.  As such, should Council direct staff to bring forward 
amendments to the Burnaby Building Bylaw to require compliance with Statutory 
Building Schemes or Restrictive Covenants as part of Building Permit approval, 
changes to the Building Permit application process will be required.  For SSMUH 
developments, such changes could include amendments to the Residential initial 
application process to require an applicant engage a lawyer to prepare a title summary 
at the applicant’s expense to identify any charges that restrict construction or include 
specific construction requirements, note all conditions contained within the charges, and 
identify those that specifically pertain to zoning and building requirements, such as 
number of dwelling units, bulk, massing, etc.  Furthermore, the applicant would be 
required to either agree to comply with the conditions of the Restrictive Covenants or 
Statutory Building Scheme, or seek the discharge of these encumbrances prior to 
advancing a full Building Permit application.  The results of this additional review for all 
properties with Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes registered on title 
will undoubtedly add time and expense to the development process for the City and 
applicants. 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Not applicable at this time.  Should Council direct staff to prepare amendments to the 
Burnaby Building Bylaw applicable through the City to require compliance with 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes as part of Building Permit 
approval, staff would update the Building Division’s process documents and information 
on the City’s website.  
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Should amendments to the Burnaby Building Bylaw be made to require compliance with 
Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes as part of Building Permit 
approval, additional submission requirements and review of applicable Restrictive 
Covenant and Statutory Building Scheme requirements would likely add time and cost 
to the development application process, both in terms of City staff and applicant 
resources. For these reasons, and others noted in this report, amending the Burnaby 
468

Subject: Brentwood Park SSMUH Motion - Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes 
Open Council Meeting April 28, 2026 ........................................... Page 7 
 
Building Bylaw to uphold private third-party Restrictive Covenants and Statutory 
Building Schemes is not recommended by staff.  
Respectfully submitted, 
Lee-Ann Garnett, Deputy General Manager Planning and Development, on behalf of 
E.W. Kozak, General Manager Planning and Development

1.0  POLICY SECTION 
Documentation of the Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel through an as-found report 
and preparation of a Statement of Significance for the memorial park is consistent with 
the Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025). 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
The Forest Lawn Memorial Park at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue (Attachment 1) is identified 
in the City’s heritage inventory Burnaby’s Heritage: An Inventory of Buildings and 
Structures (2011). The inventory is a list of heritage resources that have significant 
heritage value and are recommended for further consideration for inclusion on the 
Community Heritage Register and for permanent protection. The property is the subject 
of a development application (Rezoning Application #24-07) that includes the demolition 
of the Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel (‘the Chapel’). Submission of a Statement of 
Significance for the memorial park and an as-found report for the Chapel were 
conditions of development approval. This report advances the as-found report and 
Statement of Significance to Council for information. 
473

As-found Report and Statement of Significance for Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue 
CHC Meeting April 9, 2026  .......................................................... Page 2 
 
2.1  Forest Lawn Memorial Park 
The Forest Lawn Memorial Park opened to the public in September 1936.  The 
cemetery’s original design featured a curvilinear road network and manicured lawns 
with flush, bronze grave markers to create a space that felt more like an open park than 
a traditional cemetery. Since that time, the memorial park has continued to evolve, 
including the development of an assortment of public monuments, gardens, 
architectural structures, community-designated burial sections, and other memorials 
and commemorations. 
 
Prior to 1955, funeral services were performed at a Chapel of Peace, located outside 
the gates of the memorial park on the northeast corner of Royal Oak Avenue and Sprott 
Street. The Chapel of Peace building was purchased by an Anglican congregation in 
1955 and moved to 5584 Kincaid Street (where it is protected by Heritage Designation 
Bylaw #13162 and continues to operate as a place of worship).  
 
Development of an on-site chapel at Forest Lawn Memorial Park began soon after, with 
architectural plans for the Chapel completed in 1961. An official opening was held in 
June 1962 for the building, which was originally known as the “Little Chapel of Flowers.” 
The Chapel was designed by architects McCarter & Nairne, who are responsible for 
several significant buildings in the region (including Vancouver’s Marine Building).  
Forest Lawn Cemetery is listed on the City’s Heritage Inventory. The inventory listing 
identifies the Chapel and the landscape design of the Forest Lawn Memorial Park as 
components of its heritage value. 
2.2  Rezoning Application  
Rezoning Application #24-07 received Third Reading at the January 13, 2026 Council 
meeting. It is anticipated that the proposal will be advanced for final adoption later this 
year. 
The rezoning application seeks to rezone a portion of the property from P4 Cemetery 
District to CD Comprehensive Development District to redevelop Forest Lawn Memorial 
Park’s funeral home facilities. The proposed development includes demolition of five 
buildings and construction of a new, two-storey facility to consolidate existing services 
into one building at the northeast corner of the site. The Chapel building is among the 
buildings that is proposed to be demolished. 
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
As the property is not formally protected by a Heritage Designation Bylaw or listed on 
the Community Heritage Register, retention of the chapel is not required. 
However, as the rezoning application provides an opportunity for the owner to consider 
the heritage values of the property, staff requested the following as conditions of 
development approval: 
474

As-found Report and Statement of Significance for Heritage Resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue 
CHC Meeting April 9, 2026  .......................................................... Page 3 
 
• Documentation of the Chapel building through an as-found report that is 
acceptable to the City; and 
• Preparation of a Statement of Significance for the site to identify heritage values 
and character defining elements of the memorial park. 
These documents have subsequently been received and accepted by staff. They are 
attached here for the information of Council. A brief description of these documents is 
provided below. 
3.1  As-found Report 
An as-found report is a record of a heritage resource created to retain information about 
a heritage building, site, or structure as part of the historical record. The as-found report 
for the Chapel was produced by Luxton Heritage Consultants and includes a historical 
overview and photographs of the building, contemporary site photos, the original 
architectural plans, and an as-built floor plan with interior photo references (Attachment 
2). A set of as-built drawings and elevations was also included (Attachment 3). 
Advancing the as-found report as an attachment to a Council report ensures its 
retention as a permanent City record that is accessible to the public. It also provides an 
opportunity for Council and the Community Heritage Commission to be aware of the use 
of as-found reports as a best practice for heritage resource management when a 
resource cannot be retained. 
3.2  Statement of Significance 
A Statement of Significance (SOS) is a heritage resource management tool that 
describes a historic place. 
A SOS describes why a historic resource is important (its heritage value) and identifies 
the specific features of the resource that contribute to its value (the ‘character defining 
elements’). The statements are cornerstones of a “values-based” approach to heritage 
management: they identify which components of a resource should be retained based 
on how those components contribute to the overall value. By identifying character-
defining elements, an SOS helps property owners avoid interventions that could harm 
the overall heritage value of a property.  
A values-based approach can also ensure that features that might not be original to a 
site or may not stand out for their architectural uniqueness can still be recognized as 
important if they have value to the community. In the case of the Forest Lawn Memorial 
Park, special features include commemorative monuments that mark community-
designated burial sections that serve as spaces within the cemetery for cultural and 
religious communities.  
The SOS can be used to guide the owners of the memorial park to avoid impact on 
character-defining elements when altering the cemetery landscape. It can also help the 
City identify when a development permit impacts a character-defining element, reducing 
the time needed to review applications that do not impact heritage character. For future

applications, when a planned development has potential to impact a character-defining 
element, the property owners could contact the City early in their planning to explore 
options to support retention.

5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS

INTRODUCTION
Located within the ancestral territory of the hən̓ q̓ əmin̓ əm̓  
and Sḵwx̱ wú7mesh speaking peoples, the Forest Lawn 
Funeral Home Chapel is situated in the Douglas-Gilpin 
neighbourhood planning area in the city of Burnaby’s 
southwest quadrant. Positioned near the geographical 
centre of the expansive Forest Lawn Memorial Park, the 
multi-faith and multi-cultural Forest Lawn Funeral Home 
Chapel is constructed into a gentle slope and features 
a stacked red-brown concrete brick exterior finish with 
a gray and red-brown recessed concrete block trim. 
Concrete blocks and bricks are also employed within the 
interior, and the enclosed multi-vehicle concourse, which 
also serves as an overflow area for services, retains its 
original terrazzo floor. The extant, rambling form of the 
structure is a result of its original 1962 construction, 
consisting of the hip-roof chapel with its octagonal spire 
and a tall, flat-roof rectangular plan former crematorium 
on its west periphery; and a significant 1985-86 flat-
roof addition which enveloped the west, north, and east 
elevations of the original building. 
While the architectural firm of McCarter, Nairne & 
Partners designed the Forest Lawn Funeral Home 
Chapel in the International style, an architectural 
movement which was typically exemplified by its 
horizontality, balanced, and purely functional form and 
finishes, the Funeral Home Chapel employs a variety of 
traditional, eclectic, and Expressionist elements. 
FOREST LAWN MEMORIAL PARK HISTORY
The majority of Forest Lawn Memorial Park, including 
the Funeral Home Chapel, sits upon a large portion of 
the 146-acre District Lot 81, which was granted by the 
Province to James Ellard on January 4th, 1878. By the

SEPTEMBER 2024
mid-1930s, the vacant property was owned by The 
British Columbia Mills Timber & Trading Company who 
sold it to the General Realty Company in circa 1934. 
In 1935, the Forest Lawn Cemetery Company was 
incorporated by Albert F. Arnold and District Lot 81 was 
acquired from the General Realty Company. 
Albert F. Arnold (1877-1964) was born in England and 
came to Canada as child, arriving in British Columbia 
in the early 1890s where he eventually became a 
printer with local newspapers in Vancouver and later 
Nanaimo. His career transitioned in 1907 when he helped 
establish the Canadian Financiers Trust Company, a 
lender and real estate investment firm, becoming its first 
secretary, a position he held until 1910 when he began 
solely managing its real estate department. In 1919, the 
Canadian Financiers Trust Company established Ocean 
View Burial Park in Burnaby, which Albert is credited 
for producing the landscape design of, employing 
contemporary principles of cemetery planning which 
sought to present cemeteries as places of beauty in 
park-like settings. He also served as a director with the 
Ocean View Burial Park Company, incorporated the 
same year to manage the cemetery.
Albert continued working for the Canadian Financiers 
Trust Company up until the mid-1930s, additionally 
serving as a councillor with the Municipality of Point 
Grey for over a decade prior to its amalgamation with 
the City of Vancouver in 1929. His work with Ocean View 
Burial Park appears to have resulted in an interest to 
invest into, and establish, his own private cemetery. 
Albert was reportedly a good friend of Hubert L. Eaton, 
the latter who acquired the famed Forest Lawn Memorial 
Park in Glendale, California in 1917 and created the 
precedent for which many subsequent cemeteries 
across North America were modeled after. Similar 
to Hubert, Albert’s design for his own Forest Lawn 
Memorial Park in 1935 included curvilinear lanes, flat 
bronze grave markers for single-plot burials, a heavy 
emphasis on Christian symbology and nomenclature, 
Oblique aerial view of Forest Lawn Memorial Park, circa 1950. Simon Fraser University Digitized Collections MSC130-4677-01
Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel
481

3
3789 ROYAL OAK AVENUE, BURNABY
SEPTEMBER 2024
and an integration of a full range of deathcare services 
at the cemetery property itself.
On October 2, 1935, the Provincial Board of Health 
approved the establishment of Forest Lawn Memorial 
Park, and it was opened publicly on September 12, 1936 
with dignitaries from Burnaby and New Westminster 
in attendance. At the time of its opening, Albert had 
invested $200,000.00 into the property, with 11 acres 
developed to accept interments. A staff of four managed 
the Memorial Park, and a church, known as the Chapel 
of Peace, was erected outside the gates of the Memorial 
Park at the northeast corner of Royal Oak Avenue 
and Sprott Street to provide for funeral services and 
weddings (this chapel was purchased by an Anglican 
congregation in 1955 and moved to 5584 Kincaid Street 
where it remains extant).
Albert remained active in managing Forest Lawn 
Memorial Park until just a few years before his passing in 
1964. His funeral services were held in the Forest Lawn 
Funeral Home Chapel, and he was interred in a family 
plot within the cemetery. 
In 1969, Service Corporation International (SCI), 
headquartered in the United States, purchased Forest 
Lawn Memorial Park, and continue to own and operate it 
under their Dignity Memorial brand.
FOREST LAWN FUNERAL HOME CHAPEL
Architectural plans for the Forest Lawn Funeral Home 
Chapel were furnished in 1961 by the Vancouver-based 
architectural firm of McCarter, Nairne & Partners, one 
of British Columbia’s most prominent and successful 
practices which lasted from 1921 to 1982. Their studio 
was responsible for the designs of many landmarks 
throughout the Vancouver area, including the Spencer’s 
Department Store; the demolished Medical-Dental 
Building; the Marine Building; Seaforth Armoury; 
the McLennan, McFeely & Prior Building in New 
Westminster; and the immense, post-Second World War 
Vancouver General Post Office.
The original exterior design of the Forest Lawn Funeral 
Home Chapel, a large portion now obscured by its 1980s 
additions and other alterations, featured its primary 
entrance on the east elevation within a recessed portico 
sheltered by the east end of the hip-roof of the chapel 
building. This portico was supported by protruding 
right-angle wing walls composed of concrete bricks and 
blocks, and double-doors within the portico led directly 
into the extant narthex of the chapel. 
The rectangular-plan hip-roof over the chapel space 
includes an octagonal spire positioned immediately 
above the chancel. Triangular multi-light windows are 
located on the south and north elevations of the steeple, 
and the steeple itself features a copper cap. Both the 
hip-roof and steeple were originally clad in cedar 
shingles.
An original colonnade of concrete blocks situated on the 
south elevation and provides a secondary entrance into 
the building, leading directly into the concourse. Similar 
to the exterior, concrete units are also utilized within the 
interior of the concourse, framing the entire concourse 
space.
Multi-coloured cathedral glass is used extensively in-
conjunction with wood-framed interior screens and 
windows, as well as the triangular exterior windows at 
the base of the octagonal steeple. The interior of the 
chapel itself, including the nave and chancel, is finished 
in wood and wood veneer. An indoor garden, an original 
feature of the building, remains extant on the north side 
of the nave.
Notable differences between the available 1961 plans 
produced by McCarter, Nairne & Partners, and what 
was ultimately constructed, include: an addition of a 
mezzanine within the chapel; a flat-roof extension on 
the northeast corner of the original building; and the 
removal of the planned skylight within the concourse 
which would have been placed adjacent to the chapel 
area. 
Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel
482

4
3789 ROYAL OAK AVENUE, BURNABY
SEPTEMBER 2024
Concrete bricks, and presumably the concrete blocks as 
well, appear to have been sourced from the Denstone 
Company, based in Vancouver, and masonry work in 
1962 was undertaken by Banek & Zirpke Masonry Ltd., 
with San San Installations laying the terrazzo floor within 
the concourse. A partial 850ft2 concrete basement 
was provided for underneath the chapel to house a 
large furnace, and the chapel itself was air originaly 
conditioned. A Baldwin organ (Baldwin Piano & Organ 
Company) was supplied by Gribben Piano & Organ 
Company and included a Chimes Carillon attachment. 
A two-day opening event of the Forest Lawn Funeral 
Home Chapel occurred over the weekend of June 9th 
and 10th, 1962, where the public was invited to view and 
tour the new chapel space. The building was initially 
named The Little Chapel of Flowers (presumably a 
homage to The Little Church of Flowers which was built 
in 1921 at Glendale’s Forest Lawn Memorial Park), and 
during the opening, organist Robert Read performed 
concerts for visitors. Four ministers representing local 
Christian denominations dedicated the chapel and their 
names were inscribed on the granite cornerstone which 
was initially installed on the north wing wall at the former 
main entrance. Similar to the former Chapel of Peace, 
weddings were additionally accommodated at the Forest 
Lawn Funeral Home Chapel.
Two decades after opening, a large addition was 
conceived for the Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel. 
The addition, built between 1985-1986, was a result 
of an immediate need to provide expanded facilities, 
and SCI invested $1.3 million into its construction and 
alterations to the Funeral Home Chapel. At the time of 
completion, Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel became 
the largest full service funeral facility in the province. 
The addition provided for additional visitation rooms, 
arrangement offices, administrative offices, a dedicated 
casket selection room, accessible washrooms, and a 
flower shop. The designer and builder of the addition are 
unknown, and it is unclear if the crematorium services 
which were an original feature of the 1962 building was 
removed at this time, or sometime prior. The northwest 
segment of the addition included an expanded mortuary 
facility which by the early 1990s was supporting over 
1200 preparations per year. 
The 1980s addition resulted in a relocation of the 
main entrance, originally on the east elevation, to the 
south elevation immediately east of the concourse’s 
vehicular exit. A new family reception area, kitchen, and 
administrative offices were provided with the addition 
which was built adjoining the original main entryway. 
On the north side of the building, the former exterior 
concrete blocks and bricks were conserved in-situ and 
provided a backdrop for a new lounge space. 
Another significant investment into the complex 
occurred in 2012, and these alterations were designed by 
North Vancouver architect Ed Berwick. Alterations at this 
time included the provision of a large outdoor patio area 
on the east side of the building, adjacent to the family 
reception area; large glass canopies over both the main 
entrance and pedestrian entrance to the concourse 
along the south elevation; and a square, clerestory 
projection above the reception area of the main entrance. 
Additionally, the indoor garden adjacent to the nave had 
its natural plantings removed and replaced with artificial 
copies. Other alterations, which may have predated the 
2012 work, including the painting of original millwork 
and concrete units in some areas in an off-white colour, 
concealing the original finishes of these features.

STATEMENT OF SIGNIFICANCE

DESCRIPTION OF HISTORIC PLACE 
Situated in west-central Burnaby, Forest Lawn Memorial Park a 118-acre cemetery located on 
the west side of Royal Oak Avenue between Woodsworth and Moscrop Streets. Surrounded by 
residential neighbourhoods, the expansive, sloping property rises to a high point at its 
northeast corner where the primary entrance gates are located, as are the administrative 
offices and maintenance area of the cemetery operator as well. Primarily, and historically, 
comprised of manicured lawns with flush, bronze, grave markers, Forest Lawn Memorial Park 
also contains a variety of water features and monuments, and maintains active facilities that 
continue to provide burial, cremation and other commemoration services.   
 
HERITAGE VALUE OF HISTORIC PLACE 
Established in 1936, Forest Lawn Memorial Park is highly valued as a continually evolving 
cultural landscape. Occupying most of District Lot 81, ownership of this property was first 
obtained by James Ellard, a New Westminster merchant and former Royal Engineer with the 
Columbia Detachment, several days before his passing in January 1878. His estate managed 
the vacant land until the early 1900s, and beginning in the 1910s, interest began growing to 
utilize the District Lot as a cemetery. In 1913, the District of Burnaby sought to purchase land 
to establish a municipal cemetery, with District Lot 81 favoured by councillors and 
administration, though this scheme did not proceed. The property owners also futilely 
approached the District in late 1918 to sell the land for cemetery purposes, and in 1932, 
Burnaby’s council also rejected a proposal by private interests to establish a Memorial Park on 
the land. However, only three years later, the newly incorporated Forest Lawn Cemetery 
Company successfully received permission from provincial and local authorities to proceed 
with the development of their envisioned Forest Lawn Memorial Park. Brush clearing began in 
1935, and the first portion of the Memorial Park, consisting of 11 acres at its northeast corner, 
was opened during a public event on September 12th, 1936. The landscape design was heavily 
influenced by the eponymous Forest Lawn Memorial Park in Glendale, CA, featuring 
geographical zones, a curvilinear road network, exclusive use of flush, bronze, grave markers, 
and a heavy emphasis on Christian symbology. Stones were imported from across Canada and 
Europe for rock gardens, ornamentation, and to armour the water features. The Chapel of 
Peace, a purpose-built, non-denominational church was built in 1936, just outside the entrance 
gates, which hosted both funeral and wedding services, of which were continued in the 1962-
built Forest Lawn Funeral Home Chapel. Over the decades, the early gardenesque vision of the 
Memorial Park transitioned to include upright grave markers, private and public mausoleums, 
garden crypts, estate plots, columbariums, and cremation markers, and now also supports an 
array of multi-faith and multi-cultural funerary practices.  
 
Forest Lawn Memorial Park is also valued for its association with its founder and designer, 
English-born Albert F. Arnold (1877-1865). Migrating to British Columbia in the early 1890s, 
561

STATEMENT OF SIGNIFICANCE 
 
June 6, 2025 
 
 
Page 3 of 70 
 
 
 
Albert began a career as a printer, working in Vancouver and Nanaimo, and also served as a 
councillor with the Municipality of Point Grey in the 1920s. Reportedly a good friend with Hubert 
L. Eaton, who established Forest Lawn Memorial Park in Glendale, Albert also assisted in the 
design of Ocean View Burial Park in the late 1910s. Albert formed the Forest Lawn Cemetery 
Company and personally formulated plans for the Forest Lawn Memorial Park in Burnaby. The 
design embodied his philosophy that the cemetery was meant to celebrate life and beauty, and 
not death. He remained active in the company until his passing, and is buried along with his 
wife and family at the highest point of the Memorial Park. 
 
Forest Lawn Memorial Park is additionally valued for its assortment of public monuments, 
gardens, architectural structures, community-designated burial sections, and other memorials 
and commemoration found throughout the cemetery grounds. Original and extant features of 
the Memorial Park include the imposing, Gothic-style entrance gates; the large linear water 
feature with ornamental plants, trees, and rock gardens; and the Rock Fort and its large stone 
cross. Subsequent and notable additions to the Memorial Park include the 1956-built Cemetery 
Office and 1981-built Garden Mausoleum; marble statues, including Mother Love, sculpted in 
1949 by Italian sculptor Alessandro Piazzi; areas dedicated to veterans, St. Mary’s Ukrainian 
Catholic Church, and St. George's Greek Orthodox Church; and memorials to Estonian and 
Hungarian casualties of Soviet oppression.   
 
CHARACTER-DEFINING ELEMENTS 
The elements that define the heritage character of the Forest Lawn Memorial Park include, 
but are not limited to its: 
• 
location of the west side of Royal Oak Avenue between Woodsworth and Moscrop 
Streets; 
• 
natural, and modified, topography of the property including a high point at the northeast 
corner and a gentle southern aspect slope, with a steeper western aspect slope on the 
western periphery; and a small channel which carries a tributary of Beaver Creek; 
• 
garden landscape which includes a curvilinear and gridiron transportation network 
constructed of concrete roads and curbs, and featuring splitter islands; an organized 
methodology of grave markers, primarily consisting of straight rows orientated in cardinal 
and intercardinal directions; manicured lawns and greenspaces; mature and ornamental 
trees, plants, flowers, and other vegetation; water features and associated rock gardens at 
both the north and northwest sections of the property; the circular Rock Fort; and the 
northeast entrance gate featuring four large square concrete columns finished with 
uncoursed, variegated stone masonry, including concrete octagonal spires and metal 
finials, embedded bronze plaques inscribed with “FOREST LAWN MEMORIAL PARK,” and 
stone-clad curvilinear wing walls with concrete coping; 
562

STATEMENT OF SIGNIFICANCE 
 
June 6, 2025 
 
 
Page 4 of 70 
 
 
 
• 
memorials and other commemorative monuments including: the Carrara marble Mother 
Love sculpture; other historic life-size stone statues depicting individuals and religious 
figures, including one of Jesus Christ; large stone crosses, including those erected in: 
1936 within the Rock Fort, 1954 for the St. Mary’s Ukrainian Catholic Church burial section, 
and 1979 for the Estonian memorial; and the 1986-erected, polished granite Hungarian 
Martyrs monument; 
• 
community-designated burial sections, including those for infants, veterans, St. Mary’s 
Ukrainian Catholic Church, and St. George's Greek Orthodox Church; and 37 burial plots 
and markers recognized and managed by the Commonwealth War Graves Commission;  
and 
• 
buildings, including: the 1956-built West Coast Modern-style Cemetery Office, a wood-
frame, side-gable, two-storey structure which features continuous courses of wood frame 
and sash windows, and stacked red brick and horizontal wood cladding; the 1962-built 
McCarter, Nairne & Partners-designed rear addition to the Cemetery Office; and the 1981-
built Garden Mausoleum.

COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
ENVIRONMENT COMMITTEE (EC) 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER ENGINEERING  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
SOLID WASTE AND RECYCLING (SWR) 2025 ANNUAL REPORT 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide a summary of the City of Burnaby solid waste and recycling 
collection system performance. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Solid Waste and Recycling (SWR) 2025 Annual Report” 
dated April 13, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
Burnaby is one of twenty-one member municipalities that coordinate with Metro 
Vancouver on the plans and initiatives shaping our regional waste management system. 
The regional Integrated Solid Waste and Resource Management Plan (ISWRMP) was 
approved by the BC Ministry of Environment in 2011 and is currently undergoing a 
process of updating to build off current successes while accelerating waste diversion, 
reducing GHG emissions, and promoting a circular economy. 
 
Federal and Provincial environmental initiatives have helped to further shape waste 
management in the region, including the: 
 
• Canada-wide Action Plan on Zero Plastic Waste 
• Single-use Plastic Prohibition Regulations 
• CleanBC Roadmap to 2030 
• CleanBC Plastics Action Plan 
• Single-use and Plastics Waste Prevention Regulation 
 
At the City of Burnaby level, waste management is primarily supported by the following 
City bylaws: 
 
• The Burnaby Solid Waste and Recycling Bylaw 2010 
• The Burnaby Bylaw Enforcement Bylaw 2009 
• The Burnaby Construction and Demolition Waste Diversion Bylaw  
• The Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw 
 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The SWR Annual Report summarizes the City’s current waste management program 
performance as well as planned enhancements for the coming years ahead. It includes 
631

Subject: Solid Waste and Recycling (SWR) 2025 Annual Report 
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 2 
 
data on recycling, green waste, and garbage from homes and businesses through 2025, 
highlighting key trends. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
The City of Burnaby provides comprehensive solid waste and recycling services to its 
customers. The services include curbside collection of various waste streams and large 
items, supplemented by drop-off options for additional recyclables at the Eco-Centre, 
and, more recently, by large scale annual cleanup events. In addition, the program 
devotes significant resources each year to keeping streets and public spaces clean 
through regular litter pick-up, streetscape receptacle servicing, and abandoned waste 
collection. 
3.1 Program Highlights and Performance 
In 2025, the City delivered strong environmental outcomes, expanded public 
services, and advanced key waste-diversion and sustainability initiatives across 
Burnaby. 
The Solid Waste and Recycling Division collected 74,489 tonnes of total material - 
including garbage, recyclables, green waste – from all sectors. This represents a 1% 
increase as compared to the previous year. The largest gain occurred in recyclable 
materials, which increased by 447 tonnes, followed by a modest rise in green waste. 
These results demonstrate that although total waste generation fluctuates with 
population growth and behavioural changes, the City continues to make steady 
gains in recycling performance and diversion outcomes. The overall diversion rate 
was observed at 61%. 
The Burnaby Eco-Centre played a significant role in maintaining this diversion rate. 
The facility recorded increased volumes across several key categories. Notable 
growth in scrap metal, used oil, donated clothing items, polystyrene (Styrofoam), 
electronics, small appliances, paints, and mattresses volumes were recorded. These 
increases demonstrate strong resident participation and the ongoing effectiveness of 
the City’s recycling stewardship partnerships.  
In June of 2022, the City adopted a Construction and Demolition (C&D) Waste 
Diversion Bylaw which requires builders to divert material from the landfill to reuse 
and recycling. Last year alone, 26,972 tonnes of C&D material was diverted. This 
brings the cumulative total material diverted to 44,592 tonnes since program 
inception, with an average diversion rate of 91% per building. 
 
3.2 Looking Ahead to the Future 
The City will continue to strengthen its waste reduction and diversion programs 
through a series of planned initiatives and service enhancements. These efforts will 
build on the successes of recent years while supporting the City’s long-term 
sustainability objectives. 
632

Subject: Solid Waste and Recycling (SWR) 2025 Annual Report 
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 3 
 
In the coming years, the City plans to: 
• Host additional cleanup events to provide residents with expanded 
opportunities to properly dispose of garbage, recyclables and reusable items. 
• Support new SSMUH developments and ensure the highest level of service 
consistency across all housing types. 
• Monitor Curbside Flexible Plastics collection pilots in partnership with Recycle 
BC to determine their long-term viability for Burnaby and evaluate the 
potential for broader implementation. 
• Continue supporting repair cafés and local reuse initiatives in collaboration 
with community partners to promote waste reduction through repair and 
material reuse. 
• Expand waste diversion and recycling outreach to additional schools, 
neighborhoods and community groups to strengthen public awareness and 
participation. 
• Launch the “Recycling Rock Star” recognition initiative, offering positive 
reinforcement to residents who consistently demonstrate excellent recycling 
practices. 
• Continue research and testing of bear-resistant equipment compatible with 
the City’s collection vehicles to further reduce wildlife attractants and improve 
community safety. 
• Expand the three-stream streetscape bin program at transit stops and explore 
enclosure designs that provide flexibility in limited spaces while remaining 
compatible with automated collection trucks. 
• Explore options to raise the minimum C&D diversion threshold levels. 
 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
In keeping with the goal of reducing waste, only a limited number of hard copies of the 
Solid Waste and Recycling 2025 Annual Report will be printed. An electronic copy of this 
report, as in previous years, will be available on the City website for viewing. 
 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
This report is provided for informational purposes only. There are no financial 
considerations at this time. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
May Phang, P.Eng., General Manager Engineering

1.0 Introduction & Highlights
The Solid Waste and Recycling (SW&R) Division of 
Burnaby’s Engineering Department is responsible for 
planning, implementing, and operating a comprehensive 
range of waste management services that support the 
community’s environmental and operational needs. 
The SW&R Division plays a central role in reducing 
waste, promoting responsible disposal practices, and 
maintaining the cleanliness and sustainability of the 
City’s public spaces.
Core responsibilities of the division include:
	» solid waste collection
	» green waste collection
	» recycling collection
	» Eco-Centre operations
	» litter pickup
	» Large Item Pickup program
	» abandoned material removal
	» waste diversion education
	» SW&R bylaw enforcement
	» community outreach services
	» development reviews for SW&R service
	» Adopt A Street volunteer litter picking program
	» community-led and City-supported litter cleanup events
	» special event support (barriers and waste services)
Burnaby’s SW&R Division integrates the principles of 
waste reduction, environmental stewardship, public 
participation and worker safety to serve the community. 
The City delivers services across several sectors, 
including single  and two-family households, small-
scale multi-unit housing (SSMUH) (multifamily buildings, 
and industrial, commercial, institutional and civic (ICI) 
facilities.
In 2025, the division maintained consistent waste 
diversion performance, expanded services at the Eco-
Centre, and continued to pursue opportunities to reduce 
waste, increase proper diversion and improve efficiency.
This annual report provides an overview of the City’s 
waste management system, including details on the 
programs and services planned for implementation 
in 2026 and beyond to support the City’s long term 
waste diversion goals. Waste diversion (recycling and 
organics) and disposal (garbage) volumes for the single 
family, multifamily, and ICI sectors are presented from 
the inception of each program through 2025 to illustrate 
system-wide trends.

2.0 Policy
Federal Initiatives
Canada-wide Action Plan on Zero Plastic Waste (2019)
In 2019, the Government of Canada adopted the 
Canada-wide Action Plan on Zero Plastic Waste. This 
strategy is intended to reduce plastic pollution, support 
the recovery and reuse of plastics in the economy, 
and achieve the goal of zero plastic waste by 2030. 
The plan also contributes to Canada’s national waste 
reduction targets of a 30% reduction in per capita waste 
by 2030 and a 50% reduction by 2040.
Single-use Plastics Prohibition Regulations (2022–2025)
On June 20, 2022, the Government of Canada 
announced the Single-use Plastics Prohibition 
Regulations under the Canadian Environmental 
Protection Act, 1999. These regulations restrict the 
manufacture, import and sale of six categories of single 
use plastics, including checkout bags, cutlery, food 
service containers, stir sticks, ring carriers and straws 
(with limited exemptions).
The regulations were designed with phased 
implementation timelines between December 20, 2022, 
and December 20, 2025, to allow businesses time to 
transition and deplete existing inventories. 
Key milestones included:
	» Prohibitions on the manufacture and import of 
checkout bags, cutlery, stir sticks, straws and food 
service containers were scheduled for completion by 
the end of 2025, while sales prohibitions for these 
items came into force in December 2023.
	» Restrictions on the manufacture, import and sale of ring 
carriers began between June 2023 and June 2024.
	» As of June 2024, the sale of flexible straws 
packaged with beverage containers (such as juice 
boxes and drinking pouches) is prohibited.
Accepting Leader in Sustainability 2025 award
On October 20, 2025, the Ministry of the 
Environment announced “it will no longer pursue 
the implementation of the single-use plastics export 
ban” and made an amendment to the regulation.. 
Canada previously planned by the end of 2025, to 
prohibit the manufacture and import of all six regulated 
plastic categories for the purpose of export. These 
regulations were challenged in federal court resulting 
in a temporary overturning of the ban. However, 
on January 30, 2026, the Federal Court of Appeal 
overturned that decision, confirming that the Single-
use Plastics Prohibition Regulations remain in effect.
These regulations were challenged in federal court 
resulting in a temporary overturning of the ban. 
However, on January 30, 2026, the Federal Court of 
Appeal overturned that decision, confirming that the 
Single-use Plastics Prohibition Regulations remain in 
effect.

Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report      9
CleanBC Roadmap to 2030
The Ministry of Environment and Climate Change 
Strategy continues to advance the CleanBC Roadmap 
to 2030, which aims to reduce GHG emissions by 
diverting recyclable materials, food scraps and organic 
waste from landfills, and by supporting enhanced landfill 
gas capture.
CleanBC Plastics Action Plan (2019)
The CleanBC Plastics Action Plan builds on the 
Roadmap by combining bans on problematic single use 
plastics with expansions to British Columbia’s Extended 
Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework. Under 
EPR programs, producers—such as manufacturers, 
distributors and retailers—are responsible for collecting 
and managing their products at end of life, including 
curbside and depot collection systems.
Single Use and Plastic Waste Prevention 
Regulation (Effective Dec 20, 2023)
British Columbia implemented the Single Use and 
Plastic Waste Prevention Regulation on December 20, 
2023. This regulation introduced restrictions on four 
categories of single use plastic items: shopping bags, 
disposable food service accessories, food service ware 
(takeout containers) and oxo-degradable plastics.
The staged implementation includes:
1.	 December 20, 2023: Restrictions on food  
service accessories.
2.	 July 15, 2024: Minimum fees for reusable or paper 
shopping bags and restrictions on shopping bags, 
food service ware and oxo-degradable plastics.
3.	 July 1, 2028: Restrictions on PVC film wrap.
4.	 July 1, 2030: Restrictions on polystyrene foam  
meat trays.

Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report      11
Recycle BC continues to expand the range of materials 
accepted in the provincial Blue Box program. In 2025, 
Recycle BC launched a pilot curbside collection program 
for flexible plastics (known as the Pink Box program) in 
Maple Ridge and West Vancouver. In 2026, this program 
will expand to the City of Vancouver (multifamily only) and 
the Regional District of Central Okanagan.
Accepted flexible plastics include plastic bags, overwraps, 
outer bags, flexible packaging with plastic seals, woven plastic 
bags, zipper‑lock pouches, crinkly wrappers and protective 
cushion packaging. These materials are also accepted 
at depot locations, including Burnaby’s Eco‑Centre. As 
technologies and facilities improve, the range of recyclable 
items is expected to continue expanding.
Regional Waste Management System
Burnaby is one of 21 member municipalities collaborating 
with Metro Vancouver to develop and implement regional 
solid waste strategies. Metro Vancouver’s regional 
Integrated Solid Waste and Resource Management 
Plan (ISWRMP), approved by the provincial Ministry of 
Environment in 2011, provides long-term guidance for waste 
reduction, recycling and residual waste management.
Metro Vancouver is currently updating the ISWRMP 
to reflect modern needs and priorities. The update is 
expected to emphasize accelerated waste diversion, 
reduced GHG emissions, and a transition toward a 
circular economy—keeping materials circulating through 
repair, reuse and recycling for as long as possible. 
The plan is currently in its initial draft stage, and Metro 
Vancouver is seeking feedback from municipal partners.
Burnaby participates in two regional committees:
	» the REAC Solid Waste Subcommittee
	» the Regional Waste Reduction Coordinators 
Committee (RWRCC)
These groups support knowledge-sharing, coordination 
on regional initiatives, and communication between Metro 
Vancouver, local governments and the province. The 
Vancouver Landfill has also received a lifetime extension 
from 2030 to 2050, providing the region with additional 
capacity and time to plan for future disposal needs. 
In November 2023, the Province mandated zoning 
bylaw amendments to increase small-scale multi-unit 
(SSMUH) across British Columbia. As of July 1, 2024, 
Burnaby updated its Zoning Bylaw to allow additional 
density in R1 residential areas, enabling duplexes, 
multiplexes, rowhouses, laneway homes and up to six-
unit configurations. These changes will significantly affect 
future waste generation and collection practices.
With increased density comes the need for updated 
waste management strategies and bylaw adjustments. 
On December 11, 2023, Burnaby amended its Solid Waste 
and Recycling Bylaw No. 12875 to align the definition of 
“primary dwelling” with the new Zoning Bylaw No. 14630. 
The amendment also requires households to use the 
largest available receptacles before requesting additional 
containers, helping manage limited storage and staging 
space. Recycling bins continue to be provided at no cost 
to encourage waste diversion.
To prepare for increased small scale multi unit housing 
development, the City created Waste and Recycling 
Design Guidelines to support builders in planning 
storage and collection areas on densified properties. The 
guidelines recommend shared garbage and green waste 
bins, with individual recycling bins provided for each 
unit, ensuring adequate space and efficient curbside 
collection.
Development activity increased substantially in 2025, 
with 93 SSMUH development reviews completed and 24 
more under review, compared to only five in the final third 
of 2024. As new developments are completed, SW&R 
collection teams are actively adapting operations to 
support changing densities while maintaining high-quality 
service delivery.

3.1	 Curbside Collection Program
Burnaby’s in-house collection services have been a core component of the City’s SW&R 
program for more than 40 years. These services are delivered by approximately 146 staff and 
supported by a fleet of 86 vehicles. Our office team and field crews work collaboratively to 
provide efficient and reliable service, and to meet the evolving needs of the community.
Burnaby’s curbside collection program provides 
collection services for three key sectors:
	» Single-family residential
	» Multifamily residential
	» Industrial, commercial, institutional (ICI)
The City operates a multi-stream recycling system that 
aligns with Recycle BC’s residential printed paper and 
packaging program. 
Materials are collected using a colour coded system 
designed to maintain high-quality recyclables:

	» Yellow Bag/Box: mixed paper
	» Grey Box: glass jars and bottles
Organic materials are collected through the City’s Green 
Bin program, which accepts food scraps, yard trimmings 
and leaf debris. These materials are processed into 
compost at a contracted regional facility.

3.2 General Services Program
The General Services Program is responsible for several key operational services that support cleanliness, waste 
diversion, and the management of large and abandoned materials throughout the city. These services include the Large 
Item Pickup program, Litter and Public Space Collection, and Abandoned Waste Collection. The program is supported 
by dedicated equipment, including three rear loader trucks for large items and abandoned material collection, four 2 
tonne flat-deck trucks for curbside mattress and appliance collection, five split body trucks for public space recycling 
and waste collection, and a fleet of three mid-size and three full-size pickup trucks for litter collection activities.
Large Item Pickup
In addition to weekly curbside recycling and waste 
collection, the City offers residents a Large Item Pickup 
service for oversized or hard-to-dispose-of materials 
such as mattresses, appliances, and furniture. This 
program remains one of the most popular services 
provided by the City, valued for being offered at no 
cost and accommodating up to three items per pickup. 
Residents can request this service via phone or through 
the City’s online service request portal.
In 2025, the City received 11,723 Large Item Pickup 
requests, making the online form for this service the 
most frequently used form on the City’s website. To 
comply with the regional disposal ban on mattresses, all 
collected mattresses are transported to and processed 
at a City-contracted mattress recycling facility, ensuring 
proper diversion of these materials from the waste 
stream. 
Streetscape recycling and waste 
collection
Littering is a persistent challenge in dense, urban 
environments such as Burnaby, where it can detract 
from the appearance of city streets and the natural 
environment. To help reduce litter and promote proper 
disposal of waste in public areas, the City maintains a 
network of 630 waste receptacles located along major 
arterial roads, at bus stops, and in areas with high 
pedestrian traffic. The litter collection fleet services 
these receptacles on a regular schedule and performs 
ongoing litter pickup along streets and boulevards.
To further support recycling in public spaces, the City 
entered into an agreement with Creative Outdoor 
Advertising (COA) in 2010 to provide three stream

Abandoned Material Service
Abandoned large items—such as mattresses, furniture 
and appliances—continue to be a common issue 
throughout Burnaby, often appearing along roadsides, 
boulevards and lanes. The City’s abandoned materials 
collection crews respond to these items as they are 
identified by staff or reported by residents through 
service requests. In 2025, the City received 9,913 
service requests related to abandoned items, general 
litter and illegal dumping— representing a 30% increase 
compared to 2024.
Option for streetscape receptacles
Waste receptacle at a trail entrance
To help address illegal dumping in areas with persistent 
activity, the City continued its targeted education and 
outreach efforts. Residents in neighbourhoods with 
a history of frequent dumping received informational 
letters explaining the City’s Large Item Pickup program 
and encouraging the reporting of abandoned materials. 
In 2025, the City distributed 1,157 information letters 
to residents in these areas. This outreach may have 
contributed to both improved reporting and increased 
Large Item Pickup requests.
To strengthen enforcement and deter illegal dumping, 
since 2021 the City has been deploying a mobile 
camera unit at known dumping hotspots across 
Burnaby. Prior to each deployment, notification letters are 
sent to residential properties within 50 metres of the unit to 
inform them of its purpose and provide contact information 
for questions or concerns. In 2025, 345 notification letters 
were issued. The unit is equipped with lighting, flashing 
indicators, and signage stating, “This area is under 24 
hour video surveillance.” While deployed, the camera 
has proven to be an effective deterrent at problem 
locations and has supported investigations by helping 
identify individuals responsible for illegal dumping, as 
many do not initially recognize the unit’s function.
Unusual items removed from city streets this period 
included 20 abandoned newspaper boxes belonging 
to various newspaper companies. Many of the boxes 
were in poor condition, no longer serving their intended 
purpose, and had become receptacles for garbage. 
Each company was notified about the condition of 
its boxes; however, after numerous complaints from 
residents and business owners, the unclaimed units 
were removed from the streets and transported to the 
Eco-Centre for temporary storage.

As Burnaby continues to grow, the Environmental 
Services team has expanded its role in the development 
review process to ensure new buildings are designed 
to support effective long term waste management. At 
the early design stage, the team reviews proposed 
developments to confirm that adequate space is 
provided for SW&R collection, bulky item storage, and a 
staging area for materials. This proactive planning helps 
prevent future limitations related to space, access and 
collection efficiency, supporting the City’s broader waste 
diversion goals.
To facilitate coordinated, well-designed developments, 
Environmental Services participates as one of several 
technical reviewers for a range of applications, 
including Preliminary Plan Approvals (PPAs), Rezoning 
applications (REZ), Building Permits (BP) and small-
scale multi-unit housing residential building permits 
(R BP). Reviews may involve creating, modifying or 
relocating SW&R rooms, enclosures, loading zones 
and collection access routes for both new and existing 
developments.
Through this work, the team ensures that new 
construction aligns with City standards and is equipped 
with the infrastructure needed to support efficient waste 
collection and high waste diversion performance for 
years to come.
3.4 Environmental Services
Development Reviews
The Solid Waste and Recycling Bylaw (Bylaw No. 12875) 
requires that all residents and businesses in Burnaby 
manage their waste responsibly by properly separating 
recyclables, green waste and non-hazardous garbage. 
Despite this, contamination remains a recurring 
challenge, often due to lack of awareness or disregard 
for proper disposal practices.
When contamination is identified by City staff, the 
first step is education. A contamination sticker is 
placed on the affected bin the first two instances, and 
the property owner receives an educational letter 
explaining the issue and outlining proper diversion 
options. If a third contamination event occurs, a 
service report is generated, prompting Environmental 
Services staff to conduct an on site inspection of the 
bin or bins in question. Staff then work with occupants, 
property owners or property managers to ensure they 
understand the requirements and corrective actions 
needed.
For locations where contamination persists despite 
multiple educational interventions, the issue escalates 
to enforcement. A Bylaw Violation Notice— commonly 
referred to as a ticket—may be issued in accordance 
with the Solid Waste and Recycling Bylaw.
Illegal dumping cases, when traceable to a specific 
individual or property, also result in enforcement actions. 
Depending on the nature of the items and whether 
immediate collection is possible, staff may issue a letter, 
a ticket, or both.
In 2025, the City issued:
	» 25 tickets related to illegal dumping
	» 33 tickets for ongoing contamination of recycling or 
green waste receptacles
These efforts support waste diversion goals by 
reinforcing proper disposal practices and encouraging 
long-term behavior change and compliance.
