Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report      21
In 2025, Burnaby continued to see positive progress in 
waste diversion, with the volume of garbage collected 
decreasing year over year and corresponding increases 
in both recycling and green waste tonnage. This 
trend reflects continued improvement in community 
participation and diversion behaviour across the city.
The City collected approximately 74,489 tonnes of total 
material—including garbage, recyclables and green 
waste—from residential and commercial sources. This 
represents a 1% increase compared to the previous year. 
The largest gain occurred in recycling, which increased 
by 447 tonnes, followed by a modest rise in green waste. 
Garbage volumes declined further, reinforcing the long 
term shift toward higher diversion rates.
Despite improvements in material separation, overall 
waste generated per household increased slightly. In 
2025, the average household produced 808 kg of total 
waste, an increase of 10 kg per household from the 
previous year. While this is a minor year over year rise, 
per household waste generation remains below levels 
observed during the COVID-19 period, when household 
waste peaked due to changes in work and living patterns.
These results demonstrate that, although total waste 
generation fluctuates with population growth and 
behavioural changes, the City continues to make steady 
gains in recycling performance and diversion outcomes.
4.0 Performance
The diagram below highlights Burnaby’s disposal and 
diversion performance for 2025. The City’s overall 
diversion rate remained stable at 61%, reflecting 
continued progress in separating recyclable and 
compostable materials from the garbage stream.
The Burnaby Eco-Centre played a significant role in 
maintaining this diversion rate. In 2025, the facility 
recorded increased volumes across several key material 
categories compared to 2024. Notable growth occurred 
in the collection of scrap metal, used oil, donated 
clothing, polystyrene (Styrofoam), electronics, small 
appliances, paint and mattresses. These increases 
demonstrate strong resident participation and the 
ongoing effectiveness of the City’s recycling and 
stewardship partnerships.

Residents diverted 5,103 tonnes of recyclable material 
and 19,407 tonnes of green waste, both holding 
consistent with the previous year’s levels.
Overall, Burnaby’s single family diversion rate remained 
stable at 68%, demonstrating continued strong 
participation in recycling and organics programs despite 
fluctuations in garbage volumes.

contributing to the overall rise in per household waste 
generation.

Institutional (ICI) Diversion
In 2025, diversion performance among Burnaby’s 
multifamily and ICI sectors continued to improve. Recycling 
tonnage increased significantly, rising 6% to 4,638 tonnes 
collected. Garbage collection remained stable at 16,595 
tonnes, as did green waste collection at 3,486 tonnes.
The combined diversion rate for the multifamily and ICI 
sectors increased to 33%, as shown in Figure 3. This 
improvement reflects the City’s increased efforts to 
address recycling contamination, including expanded 
educational outreach and targeted waste audits 
conducted over the final three quarters of the year.
While overall diversion trends in both sectors show 
gradual year over year improvement, continued focus 
on contamination reduction, education, and compliance 
will be essential to accelerating progress and achieving 
higher diversion outcomes in future years.
In 2025, multifamily households generated an average of 
603 kg of total waste per household, an increase of 10 kg 
compared to the previous year. Most of this increase came 
from recycling, which rose by 7 kg to 113 kg per household. 
Garbage generation remained stable at 405 kg per 
household, and green waste remained consistent at 85 kg 
per household.
These results demonstrate modest improvement in 
recycling participation within multifamily buildings, while 
overall waste generation patterns remained relatively 
unchanged.

4.4 Construction and Demolition Waste 
Diversion Bylaw 
In June 2022, the City adopted a Construction and 
Demolition (C&D) Waste Diversion Bylaw that requires all 
buildings being demolished to divert C&D materials from 
the landfill through reuse and recycling. Construction 
and demolition waste accounts for a third of all waste 
generated in the Metro Vancouver region. Increased 
reuse and recycling building materials reduces energy 
consumption and production of GHG emissions, 
conserves landfill space and supports a circular 
economy. 
The C&D Bylaw applies to all buildings being 
demolished, requires submission of a Waste Diversion 
Plan, payment of a Waste Diversion Deposit and 
submission of a Compliance Report post-demolition. 
To receive a full deposit refund, a minimum of 70% of 
materials must be diverted through reuse and recycling.
Table 8 summarizes demolition permits completed 
between 2023 and 2025 by building type. Single- and 
two-family dwellings accounted for most demolitions 
each year, increasing from 19 in 2023 to 156 in 2025. 
Demolition of accessory buildings also increased over 
this period, while non-residential demolitions showed a 
slight increase in 2025 compared to 2024.  
In 2025, 26,972 tonnes of C&D materials were diverted 
from the landfill through reuse and recycling. This brings 
the cumulative total materials diverted from landfill since 
the bylaw’s implementation to 44,592 tonnes.
Figure 8 illustrates the percentage of materials diverted 
and landfilled, as well as the diversion rate achieved 
for each building type. In 2025, all building types met 
or exceeded the 70% diversion target, with an average 
diversion rate of 91% across all demolitions.

What do I do if I see a bear?
If you see a bear in a residential area, please report 
your sighting to a BC Conservation Oﬃ  cer by calling 
1-877-952-7277. The reports can help the City better 
understand where bear activity may need to be
monitored more closely.
Do the rules for setting out and
removing bins apply year round?
Yes. While most bears go into a deep sleep from
November to April, some bears may not hibernate
and could still be present within the Bear Area.
What is a bear-resistant enclosure?
A bear-resistant enclosure is a fully enclosed
structure that is strong enough to prevent access
by bears. This may include a garage, shed, fenced 
structure or a structure approved by Burnaby’s
Engineering Department.
Are there ﬁ nes for failing to comply
with the Bear Area requirements?
Our goal is to minimize human-bear interactions,
and we need your cooperation. If a bear is spotted
in your neighbourhood, we may issue a notiﬁ cation
letter. Failure to comply may result in a penalty of 
either $200 or $500.
BURNABY
BEAR AREA
We share the environment with black bears.
They move through local forests, mountains and 
wilderness areas in search of food. Unfortunately, 
when bears associate food with human activities, 
they lose their fear of people.
To keep wildlife and the community safe, the
City of Burnaby has established a Bear Area.
This includes places where black bears have been 
spotted, such as areas around Burnaby Mountain, 
Burnaby Lake, Deer Lake, Robert Burnaby Park 
and other parklands.
BEAR AREA
REQUIREMENTS
By following some simple rules in the Bear Area, we
can reduce the potential for conﬂ ict. Properly store
your waste bins (garbage, recycling and green bins)
to minimize bear attractants.
Single or two-family properties must: 
» Set out waste bins between 5:30am and 7am on
 collection day and remove waste bins by 8pm.
» Securely store waste bins within your property on  
 non-collection days.
Multi-family properties must:
» Set out waste bins between 5:30am and 7am on
 collection day and remove waste bins the same day.
» Store garbage, yard waste, food scraps and
 recycling in bear-resistant bins or store all bins
 in a bear-resistant enclosure on the property.
Industrial, commercial and institutional
properties must:
» Store all garbage, yard waste, food scraps and
 recycling in bear-resistant bins or store all bins
 in a bear-resistant enclosure on the property.

Solid Waste and Recycling  2025 Annual Report      31
In 2019, City Council approved the establishment of 
a designated Bear Area within the SW&R Bylaw to 
reduce human wildlife conflicts and improve waste 
management practices in areas with higher bear activity. 
Within this zone, single- and two-family properties must 
follow specific morning set out times for collection, while 
multifamily and ICI properties are required to secure 
their receptacles or store them in a bear resistant 
enclosure.
The boundaries of the Bear Area are updated annually 
based on reported wildlife activity. Previously, these 
reports relied on data from the BC Conservation Officer 
Service but that data is no longer available in a format 
accessible to external organizations. Instead, Burnaby’s 
SW&R division collaborated with the City’s Geographic 
Information Systems (GIS) team to develop the Burnaby 
Wildlife Tracking App. This new tool enables staff across 
multiple departments to enter and access wildlife 
sightings, calls and complaints directly on a shared GIS 
map. The system supports the tracking of bear, coyote 
and other wildlife activity, improving the City’s ability to 
allocate resources effectively and reduce human wildlife 
interactions.
In 2024, increased bear activity in several areas of 
northeast Burnaby prompted an expansion of the Bear 
Area. Figure 9 highlights the updated boundaries, with 
the affected zones outlined in red.
As of 2025, Burnaby’s Bear Area regulations have been 
in effect for five full years, supporting the City’s efforts 
to reduce human/bear conflicts by limiting access to 
attractants such as household garbage and food scraps. 
To prepare residents for the annual “bear season” (May 
to November), the City may distribute bear awareness 
information packages to all households within the Bear 
Area, providing reminders on set out times, proper 
storage practices and strategies for securing attractants.
In 2025, Environmental Services staff undertook 
targeted monitoring in neighbourhoods experiencing 
higher levels of bear activity. This work involved 
collaboration with BC Conservation Officers, direct 
5.0 Bear Area
Figure 9 - Bear Area - 2024
communication with property owners regarding non 
compliance, and proactive education on best practices. 
Over the course of the year, the City issued 1,418 bear 
awareness letters to notify residents of increased 
activity in their area and to reinforce expectations 
around proper set out times and attractant management.
Some areas— most notably Burnaby Mountain— 
continue to experience elevated bear activity and 
require enhanced measures. Metal bear-resistant 
toppers installed on steel garbage bins have proven 
effective, and additional units have been ordered to 
strengthen attractant control in these zones. The City 
also trialed new bear-resistant food scrap bins in 2024, 
which successfully deterred bears. These containers, 
however, present some operational challenges that staff 
are actively working to resolve.
Despite these improvements, the most effective long 
term solution remains clear—multifamily customers 
must secure their receptacles or store them in a bear 
resistant enclosure. Ensuring compliance with these 
requirements is essential to minimizing bear attractants 
and reducing the risk of human wildlife conflict across 
the Bear Area.

6.0 Public Outreach and Education Programs
Spring Cleanup Event
The City hosted its second Spring Cleanup Event on 
June 14, 2025, providing residents with a one-stop 
opportunity to drop off household garbage, recyclables 
and reusable items at the Still Creek Works Yard. With 
strong support from City staff and local partners, the 
event welcomed 1,693 vehicles—an increase of 560 
from 2024—and collected 73,060 kg of garbage, 
representing 12,435 kg more waste than the previous 
year. The event also diverted 229 mattresses, with all 
recyclable materials transferred to the Burnaby Eco-Centre.
Reusable items were collected by Big Brothers of 
Greater Vancouver, totaling 6,951 kg, more than double 
the 2024 total. This included:
	» 3,429 kg of clothing, shoes, accessories, bedding 
and towels
	» 1,555 kg of small household items (kitchenware, toys, 
decorations, small equipment)
	» 533 kg of books
	» 1,434 kg of larger reusable items such as furniture 
and décor
Community litter cleanups
From June to September 2025, the City partnered with 
Green Teams of Canada to host four community litter 
cleanup events near Burnaby’s four major town centres: 
Edmonds, Lougheed, Brentwood and Metrotown. These 
events engaged 340 volunteers and removed 874 kg of 
litter, an increase of 199 kg from 2024.
The August 16 event at Edmonds Community Centre 
achieved the highest litter collection of any City cleanup 
event to date, gathering 313 kg with only 80 volunteers. 
These events also drew participation from residents 
in neighbouring municipalities, broadening regional 
awareness of civic environmental initiatives.
General outreach and support
In 2025, the SW&R team participated in 38 community 
and City-hosted events, providing:
	» public education on recycling and waste reduction
	» on-site garbage and recycling collection support
	» event streetside barriers and traffic coordination
	» assistance for six resident led community cleanups, 
supplying equipment and collection services
Additional outreach efforts included:
	» 3 outreach presentations to elementary schools, 
businesses and a nonprofit group
	» 2 guided tours of the Burnaby Eco-Centre
	» participation in 3 community events with Burnaby 
Neighbourhood House to provide waste education
	» partnered with Burnaby Crime Prevention to host a 
Data Shredding and Electronics Recycling Event for 
Fraud Prevention Month at the Burnaby Eco-Centre”

Adopt-a-Street Program
Launched in 2022, the Adopt-a-Street Program 
continued to grow and now includes more than 155 
active volunteers. To better support participants, the 
City launched an improved volunteer database in 2024 
along with an updated webpage, application process 
and interactive street map. These enhancements have 
strengthened volunteer coordination and expanded 
cleanup activities across City boulevards, streets and 
laneways. Coordination with the Parks, Recreation 
and Culture Department has also improved, allowing 
volunteers to contribute to litter cleanups in parks and 
City facilities.
Seasonal waste diversion campaigns
Throughout the year, the City promoted seasonal 
waste reduction messages, with a notable focus on the 
Holiday Waste Reduction Campaign during November 
and December. A dedicated webpage provided tips 
for reducing holiday packaging waste, managing food 
scraps and properly recycling Christmas trees. This 
information was also included on the curbside collection 
calendar, the City’s mobile app, and through holiday 
push notifications to ensure broad public reach.
Repair Café partnership
In 2025, the City strengthened its collaboration with 
Burnaby Neighbourhood House, a community non profit 
organization, to support repair cafés—free events where 
residents can bring damaged household items to be 
repaired by skilled volunteers. These cafés promote 
reuse and waste reduction by extending the lifespan of 
household goods. Commonly repaired items include:
	» small appliances (coffee makers, rice cookers, 
kettles, air fryers)
	» electronics (phones, laptops, audio devices)
	» bicycles
	» clothing and textiles
Burnaby Neighbourhood House launched the initiative 
in March 2025 and held six repair cafés throughout the 
year. The City funded and promoted two of these events 
and plans to support additional cafés moving forward.

Waste collection app
Burnaby residents living in single family homes can 
stay informed and avoid missed collection days by 
using the City’s free Recycle Coach waste collection 
app. The app provides convenient access to the most 
current information on garbage, recycling and green 
bin services, and allows residents to set personalized 
reminders for their collection schedules.
Key app features include:
	» Collection day reminders, including alerts for holiday 
schedule changes and weather related disruptions.
	» Program details outlining accepted materials and 
preparation requirements for garbage, recycling and 
green bin collection.
	» Notifications about changes to recycling programs and 
accepted materials, including updates from Recycle BC.
	» A “What Goes Where?” searchable database 
offering drop off depot information and proper 
disposal instructions.
	» A “Report a Problem” tool for missed collections, 
illegal dumping and other service concerns.
Recycle Coach is available for both Apple and Android 
devices and can also be accessed through the 
Recycling & Garbage pages on the City’s website.
In 2025, the app had 21,221 total users and recorded 
4,362,150 interactions, equivalent to 142,554 interactions 
per 1,000 households. Usage increased across both the 
mobile and web platforms, reflecting strong engagement 
with the City’s waste services information.

Recycling and garbage services continue to be among the most frequently accessed topics

awareness through consistent social media outreach and updated visual messaging on 
collection vehicles. Key highlights from the year include:

“Oops” Sticker Program

contact information for additional support. This two step 
approach remained a valuable method in reducing 
contamination and increasing waste diversion.

7.0 Looking Ahead to 2026
In 2026, the City plans to:
	» Host the third annual Spring Cleanup Event, continuing to 
provide residents with expanded opportunities to properly 
dispose of garbage, recyclables and reusable items.
	» Adapt and optimize waste and recycling operations to support 
new small-scale multi-unit housing developments and ensure 
the highest level of service consistency across all housing 
types.
	» Monitor the flexible plastics collection pilot in partnership with 
Recycle BC to determine its long term viability for Burnaby and 
evaluate the potential for broader implementation.
	» Continue supporting repair cafés and local reuse initiatives 
in collaboration with community partners to promote waste 
reduction through repair and material reuse.
	» Expand waste diversion and recycling outreach to additional 
schools, neighbourhoods and community groups to 
strengthen public awareness and participation.
	» Launch the “Recycling Rock Star” recognition initiative, 
offering positive reinforcement to residents who consistently 
demonstrate excellent recycling practices.
	» Continue research and testing of bear-resistant equipment 
compatible with the City’s collection vehicles to further reduce 
wildlife attractants and improve community safety.
	» Expand the three-stream streetscape bin program at transit 
stops and explore enclosure designs that provide flexibility 
in limited spaces while remaining compatible with automated 
collection trucks.
	» Grow the Adopt-a-Street program, encouraging more 
community volunteers to help keep Burnaby’s boulevards, 
lanes and public spaces clean.
	» Continue conducting waste composition audits, using the 
findings to refine education priorities and target contamination 
and diversion performance.
Staff at Spring Clean Up event
	» Plan for mattress and large item drop-off options at the Eco-
Centre to improve efficiency and increase diversion of bulky 
materials.
	» Implement emission reduction strategies for waste collection 
vehicles to support the City’s broader climate and carbon 
reduction goals.
	» Undertake industry engagement on proposed amendments 
to the Construction and Demolition Waste Diversion Bylaw, 
including:
•	 increasing the diversion target from 70% to 90%
•	 introducing a lumber salvage requirement for single  and 
two family homes built prior to 1950
These initiatives will help Burnaby continue making meaningful 
progress toward its waste reduction and sustainability goals while 
responding to the needs of a growing and changing community.
The City of Burnaby has consistently met emerging environmental challenges through innovation, 
strategic planning and a strong commitment to environmental protection. Looking ahead to 2026, 
the City will continue to strengthen its waste reduction and diversion programs through a series of 
planned initiatives and service enhancements. These efforts will build on the successes of recent 
years while supporting the City’s long term sustainability objectives.

SUBJECT: 
SU:ENHANCING FOOD SUSTAINABILITY IN BURNABY 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide an operational update on food sustainability measures 
within the City of Burnaby.  
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “SU: Enhancing Food Sustainability in Burnaby” dated April 
13, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
This report aligns with the Corporate Strategic Plan (2022) and Social Sustainability 
Strategy (2011). 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
On March 31, 2025, Environment Committee meeting a motion was adopted requesting 
staff to explore the feasibility of more plant-forward food options in city facilities and 
report back on new ways of informing, promoting and incentivizing patrons to choose 
more sustainable food options. The city operates several cafes and restaurants within 
civic facilities and parks, and over previous years have already introduced a range of 
practical measures to improve the availability of plant-based, vegetarian, vegan, and 
dairy-free options. 
At the same time, food-service operations must remain customer-focused, operationally 
sound, and financially responsible. For that reason, menu development continues to 
reflect customer demand while providing a variety of dietary options. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
Staff have adopted a balanced and operationally practical approach to food 
sustainability within City food-service operations. This approach focuses on maintaining 
customer choice, responding to demonstrable demand, and introducing or refining 
plant-based and vegetarian offerings where it is operationally feasible. 
675

Subject: SU: Enhancing Food Sustainability in Burnaby  
Open EC Meeting April 13, 2026 Page 2 
 
3.1.1 Current Status 
Plant-based and vegetarian options have already been introduced at Riverway, Mintara 
a top Burnaby Mountain (“Mintara”) and other service locations. These offerings are 
included within regular menu planning so that customers have meaningful choice 
alongside traditional menu items. 
Additional vegetarian, vegan and dairy-free selections are also available through café 
and concession operations, including the Passion Coffee program. Where customers 
request dairy-free, vegetarian, or vegan accommodation that is not specifically listed on 
a menu, staff make reasonable efforts to address those requests when service 
conditions permit. 
Staff also continue to pursue local sourcing opportunities where feasible and to make 
operational choices that support sustainability objectives, including the use of an electric 
roasting system within the coffee program. 
3.1.2 Operating Approach 
Staff’s approach is deliberate and measured. The role of the food service operation is to 
provide customer choice and respond to demonstrated demand, while ensuring services 
remain operationally sound and financially responsible. 
Staff will continue to introduce, retain, and refine plant-based and vegetarian offerings 
where there is clear customer uptake, operational fit and acceptable menu performance. 
Staff will also continue to monitor demand and item performance by location, adjust 
offerings based on sales and feedback, accommodate reasonable dietary requests 
where feasible, and add further options incrementally where demand supports 
continued expansion. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
No specific communication or engagement is required as part of this report. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The city’s Food Services operations provide food and beverage options at civic facilities 
in a financially responsible manner while meeting customer demand. Staff have 
introduced plant-based food options across facilities at level aligned with current 
demand and continue to monitor interest in these offerings. As with any menu 
adjustments, expanding or promoting additional plant-forward options require careful 
consideration of potential impacts on revenue and costs. Food Services reviews menu 
performance and customer preferences to ensure an appropriate balance between 
financial sustainability and evolving dietary trends and will continue to evaluate 
opportunities accordingly.

REPORT

678

 
 
 
Meeting April 8, 2026 
File: TM-06 
COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
TRANSPORTATION COMMITTEE (TC) 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER ENGINEERING  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
CENTRAL BOULEVARD AND PARKADE INTERSECTION SAFETY 
IMPROVEMENTS  
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide Council with an overview of the proposed safety 
improvements at the intersection of Central Boulevard and Metropolis 
at Metrotown underground parkade access. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Central Boulevard and Parkade Intersection Safety 
Improvements” dated April 8, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The proposed safety improvements at the intersection of Central Boulevard and 
Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade access support the City of Burnaby’s 
Corporate Strategic Plan and goals for a safe, connected, healthy, and dynamic 
community. The project aligns with Council-adopted goals, policies, and targets 
identified in the Burnaby Transportation Plan including Vision Zero, and the City’s 
Climate Action Framework that supports the development of a sustainable, safe, and 
accessible transportation network. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Metrotown is one of Burnaby’s Town Centres and a key destination in the region. The 
growing residential population and sustained commercial activities have contributed to 
high pedestrian activities in the area. The signalized intersection of Central Boulevard 
and the Metropolis underground parkade access (the “project location”) provides a key 
connection between the SkyTrain station, bus loop and shopping centre for pedestrians 
and transit riders. The high pedestrian and vehicle traffic volumes have resulted in traffic 
safety concerns raised by the public and Metropolis. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
At the project location, the main pedestrian safety concerns pertain to conflicts between 
crossing pedestrians and turning vehicles. To mitigate these concerns, this project 
includes a new exclusive pedestrian signal phase (pedestrian scramble) as well as 
other intersection enhancements. Together, these improvements will enhance the safety 
environment at this location such that the measures are intuitive and inclusive for all 
users, as detailed below. 
679

Subject: Central Boulevard and Parkade Intersection Safety Improvements  
TC Meeting April 8, 2026 Page 2 
 
 
3.1 Pedestrian Scramble Signal Phase 
A pedestrian scramble phase is a type of traffic signal operation where all vehicular 
movements are stopped. During this signal phase, pedestrians can cross the 
intersection in all directions – including diagonally – while all vehicles are stopped. 
Vehicles can then proceed after the phase without any potential conflicts with 
crossing pedestrians. This time separation allows pedestrians to cross without 
conflicting vehicle movements and simplifies driver decision-making. 
3.2 Intersection Improvements 
Geometric enhancements are proposed to improve safety and accessibility for all 
road users. Curb ramp accessibility and pedestrian waiting areas will be improved 
together with more direct crossing alignments. The right-turn island on the northeast 
corner will be removed, which reduces the number of crossings needed for 
pedestrians, minimizing the exposure to traffic. Accessible pedestrian signals (APS) 
will also feature updated audible messaging to clearly indicate that pedestrians may 
cross in all directions during the pedestrian scramble signal phase. 
3.3 Project Timeline 
Detailed design is expected to be completed in April 2026. Construction is scheduled 
to commence shortly after with the completion targeted for Summer 2026. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
The project team will be providing stakeholders, including Metropolis at Metrotown, with 
notification ahead of construction. The project team will continue to address queries and 
concerns as they arise. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The funding for this project has been committed in the Engineering 5-year capital plan, 
2026 – 2030. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
May Phang, P.Eng., General Manager Engineering

This report was prepared by Martin To, P.Eng., Transportation Engineer, and Keera 
Wang, P.Eng., PMP, Transportation Engineer, and reviewed by Eric Tam, P.Eng., PTOE, 
Senior Manager Transportation, and Amy Choh, P.Eng., PMP, Director Engineering 
Transportation.

CITY OF BURNABY 
 
 
BYLAW NO. 14811 
 
A BYLAW to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961 
 
 
The Council of the City of Burnaby ENACTS as follows: 
 
1. 
This Bylaw may be cited as BURNABY STREET AND TRAFFIC BYLAW 1961, 
AMENDMENT BYLAW NO. 1, 2026. 
 
2. 
Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961, as amended, is further amended: 
(a) 
at Section 2(2), by adding the following definitions in alphabetical order: 
 
““Cycle” means a device having any number of wheels that is propelled by human 
power and on which a person may ride and includes a motor assisted cycle, but does 
not include any device that is gas powered, a skateboard, roller skates or inline 
roller blades. 
 
“Electric Kick Scooter” has the meaning set out in the Electric Kick Scooter Pilot 
Project Regulation. 
 
“Crossride Pavement Markings” means a series of white rectangular or square 
pavement markings that delineates a crossing area where cycling is permitted. 
 
“Major Arterial” means a highway that provides primary mobility for people and 
goods through the City, connecting town centres and major destinations, and 
accommodates regional, inter-municipal, and intra-municipal trips, as generally 
shown in Burnaby’s Transportation Plan. 
 
 
 
 
 
690

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 2 of 5 
 
“Major Road Network” has the meaning set out in the South Coast British 
Columbia Transportation Authority Act. 
 
“Minor Arterial” means a highway that provides primary mobility for people and 
goods between major activity centres within the City, and accommodates both 
inter-municipal and intra-municipal trips, as generally shown in Burnaby’s 
Transportation Plan. 
 
“Motor Assisted Cycle” has the meaning set out in the Motor Vehicle Act. 
 
“Multi-Use Pathway” means a street, or portion of a street, designed for use by 
pedestrians, cycles, electric kick scooters, motor assisted cycles, and other non-
motorized rolling conveyances which is physically separated from a roadway used 
for vehicles. 
 
“Protected Cycle Lane” means a portion of a street, designed and designated 
exclusively for the use of cycles, electric kick scooters and motor assisted cycles 
which is separated from vehicle traffic by physical elements, signing, striping, 
and/or crossride pavement markings. 
 
“Shared Electric-Bike” means a motor assisted cycle that is publicly accessible and 
owned by a shared micromobility operator. 
 
“Shared Electric Kick Scooter” means an electric kick scooter that is publicly 
accessible and owned by a shared micromobility operator. 
 
“Shared Micromobility Device” means a shared electric-bike or shared electric kick 
scooter. 
 
“Shared Micromobility Parking Zone” means the area or space on a roadway 
designated by a traffic control device and established by the Engineer in accordance 
with Section 18A(1). 
 
 
 
691

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 3 of 5 
 
“Shared Micromobility Operator” means a legal entity: 
 
(a) 
whose principal business objective is to provide its members, for a fee,  
 
 
with access to a fleet of shared micromobility devices; and 
  
(b) 
which has a valid agreement with the City to authorize the shared   
 
 
micromobility operator to allow its members to park shared  
 
 
 
micromobility devices on a street.” 
(b) 
by adding the following after Section 18(2): 
 
“SHARED MICROMOBILITY DEVICES 
 
18A. (1) 
The Engineer may: 
(a) 
establish shared micromobility parking zones for the use of 
a shared micromobility operator and the parking of shared 
micromobility devices within the City;  
(b) 
designate such micromobility parking zones and the use of 
such micromobility parking zones by way of a traffic 
control device; and  
(c) 
enter into agreements with micromobility operators to 
regulate and control the operation of shared micromobility 
operators. 
(2) 
Shared micromobility parking zones shall be used exclusively for 
the parking of shared micromobility devices. 
(3) 
No person shall park a shared micromobility device, except within 
a shared micromobility parking zone. 
 
 
 
692

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 4 of 5 
 
 
CYCLES, MOTOR ASSISTED CYCLES AND ELECTRIC KICK SCOOTERS  
 
 
 
18B. 
No person shall ride a cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick scooter: 
 
(1) 
upon any sidewalk except where permitted by a traffic control 
device; 
 
(2) 
upon a street while wearing headphones, or any other  
manufactured device capable of transmitting sound, over or in close 
proximity to both ears, except that this prohibition shall not apply to 
the wearing of a device designed and worn for the purpose of 
improving the person’s ability to hear sounds emanating from 
outside of the device; 
 
(3) 
upon a street unless the cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick  
scooter is equipped with a bell capable of being used as a warning;  
or 
 
(4) 
in a marked crosswalk, unless it is also marked by crossride  
pavement markings on one or both sides of the crosswalk, or where 
cycles are permitted by a traffic control device. 
 
 
 
18C. 
(1) 
Subject to subsection (2), a person may ride a cycle, motor assisted  
 
 
cycle or electric kick scooter in an unmarked crosswalk. 
 
 
 (2) 
A person riding a cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick scooter 
through or out of a marked or unmarked crosswalk, must yield the 
right of way to pedestrians who are entering into, walking in or 
walking out of the crosswalk and for the purposes of this subsection, 
a marked crosswalk includes the area of the crosswalk delineated by 
crossride pavement markings. 
 
 
 
693

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 5 of 5 
 
 
18D. 
A person must not operate an electric kick scooter: 
 
(1) 
while on a street without due care and attention, or without 
reasonable consideration for other persons using the street; 
 
(2) 
in contravention of the “Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Project 
Regulation”; or 
(3) 
on a major road network or arterial road, except along a 
protected cycle lane, or multi-use pathway that is located within 
or adjacent to a major road network, major arterial or minor 
arterial.”

Purpose: to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1964 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program) (Item 7.5., Administrative Report, Council April 14, 2026)

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 1 of 5 
 
 
CITY OF BURNABY 
 
 
BYLAW NO. 14811 
 
A BYLAW to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961 
 
 
The Council of the City of Burnaby ENACTS as follows: 
 
1. 
This Bylaw may be cited as BURNABY STREET AND TRAFFIC BYLAW 1961, 
AMENDMENT BYLAW NO. 1, 2026. 
 
2. 
Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961, as amended, is further amended: 
(a) 
at Section 2(2), by adding the following definitions in alphabetical order: 
 
““Cycle” means a device having any number of wheels that is propelled by human 
power and on which a person may ride and includes a motor assisted cycle, but does 
not include any device that is gas powered, a skateboard, roller skates or inline 
roller blades. 
 
“Electric Kick Scooter” has the meaning set out in the Electric Kick Scooter Pilot 
Project Regulation. 
 
“Crossride Pavement Markings” means a series of white rectangular or square 
pavement markings that delineates a crossing area where cycling is permitted. 
 
“Major Arterial” means a highway that provides primary mobility for people and 
goods through the City, connecting town centres and major destinations, and 
accommodates regional, inter-municipal, and intra-municipal trips, as generally 
shown in Burnaby’s Transportation Plan. 
 
 
 
 
 

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 2 of 5 
 
“Major Road Network” has the meaning set out in the South Coast British 
Columbia Transportation Authority Act. 
 
“Minor Arterial” means a highway that provides primary mobility for people and 
goods between major activity centres within the City, and accommodates both 
inter-municipal and intra-municipal trips, as generally shown in Burnaby’s 
Transportation Plan. 
 
“Motor Assisted Cycle” has the meaning set out in the Motor Vehicle Act. 
 
“Multi-Use Pathway” means a street, or portion of a street, designed for use by 
pedestrians, cycles, electric kick scooters, motor assisted cycles, and other non-
motorized rolling conveyances which is physically separated from a roadway used 
for vehicles. 
 
“Protected Cycle Lane” means a portion of a street, designed and designated 
exclusively for the use of cycles, electric kick scooters and motor assisted cycles 
which is separated from vehicle traffic by physical elements, signing, striping, 
and/or crossride pavement markings. 
 
“Shared Electric-Bike” means a motor assisted cycle that is publicly accessible and 
owned by a shared micromobility operator. 
 
“Shared Electric Kick Scooter” means an electric kick scooter that is publicly 
accessible and owned by a shared micromobility operator. 
 
“Shared Micromobility Device” means a shared electric-bike or shared electric kick 
scooter. 
 
“Shared Micromobility Parking Zone” means the area or space on a roadway 
designated by a traffic control device and established by the Engineer in accordance 
with Section 18A(1). 
 
 
 

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 3 of 5 
 
“Shared Micromobility Operator” means a legal entity: 
 
(a) 
whose principal business objective is to provide its members, for a fee,  
 
 
with access to a fleet of shared micromobility devices; and 
  
(b) 
which has a valid agreement with the City to authorize the shared   
 
 
micromobility operator to allow its members to park shared  
 
 
 
micromobility devices on a street.” 
(b) 
by adding the following after Section 18(2): 
 
“SHARED MICROMOBILITY DEVICES 
 
18A. (1) 
The Engineer may: 
(a) 
establish shared micromobility parking zones for the use of 
a shared micromobility operator and the parking of shared 
micromobility devices within the City;  
(b) 
designate such micromobility parking zones and the use of 
such micromobility parking zones by way of a traffic 
control device; and  
(c) 
enter into agreements with micromobility operators to 
regulate and control the operation of shared micromobility 
operators. 
(2) 
Shared micromobility parking zones shall be used exclusively for 
the parking of shared micromobility devices. 
(3) 
No person shall park a shared micromobility device, except within 
a shared micromobility parking zone. 
 
 
 

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 4 of 5 
 
 
CYCLES, MOTOR ASSISTED CYCLES AND ELECTRIC KICK SCOOTERS  
 
 
 
18B. 
No person shall ride a cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick scooter: 
 
(1) 
upon any sidewalk except where permitted by a traffic control 
device; 
 
(2) 
upon a street while wearing headphones, or any other  
manufactured device capable of transmitting sound, over or in close 
proximity to both ears, except that this prohibition shall not apply to 
the wearing of a device designed and worn for the purpose of 
improving the person’s ability to hear sounds emanating from 
outside of the device; 
 
(3) 
upon a street unless the cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick  
scooter is equipped with a bell capable of being used as a warning;  
or 
 
(4) 
in a marked crosswalk, unless it is also marked by crossride  
pavement markings on one or both sides of the crosswalk, or where 
cycles are permitted by a traffic control device. 
 
 
 
18C. 
(1) 
Subject to subsection (2), a person may ride a cycle, motor assisted  
 
 
cycle or electric kick scooter in an unmarked crosswalk. 
 
 
 (2) 
A person riding a cycle, motor assisted cycle or electric kick scooter 
through or out of a marked or unmarked crosswalk, must yield the 
right of way to pedestrians who are entering into, walking in or 
walking out of the crosswalk and for the purposes of this subsection, 
a marked crosswalk includes the area of the crosswalk delineated by 
crossride pavement markings. 
 
 
 

Bylaw No. 14811 
Page 5 of 5 
 
 
18D. 
A person must not operate an electric kick scooter: 
 
(1) 
while on a street without due care and attention, or without 
reasonable consideration for other persons using the street; 
 
(2) 
in contravention of the “Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Project 
Regulation”; or 
(3) 
on a major road network or arterial road, except along a 
protected cycle lane, or multi-use pathway that is located within 
or adjacent to a major road network, major arterial or minor 
arterial.”

Recommendation: THAT the Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee meeting be scheduled on Wednesday, May 27, 2026, at 5:00 pm in the Council Chamber.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to replace 60-year-old Zoning Bylaw with new Zoning Bylaw 2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on repealing the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replacing it with a new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, proposed to take effect July 1, 2026. The new bylaw would introduce height-based zoning districts, new Development Permit Area guidelines for building design, streamside protection, and tenant protection, updated parking rules, and reassign zoning designations to properties city-wide.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed new zoning map and rules at burnaby.ca — your property's zoning designation may change on July 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 proposed effective date — takes effect
Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 transition period for in-progress PPAs expires

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Council to consider: PST expansion on professional services referred to staff
briefs.summary: At the April 14, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, a letter from the Canadian Federation of Independent Business urging Council to oppose a proposed Provincial Sales Tax (PST) expansion on professional services was referred to city staff for further review. Staff will report back to Council — meaning no decision has been made yet, but the issue is now on Council's radar and could lead to Burnaby taking a formal position on the provincial proposal.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the staff report back to Council — this PST expansion could raise costs for your professional services.
briefs.industries: Professional Services, Finance & Insurance, Labour & Employment

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Replace 60-Year-Old Zoning Bylaw with New 2026 Rules
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on replacing Burnaby's Zoning Bylaw 1965 — the rulebook that controls what can be built and where — with a brand-new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026. If approved, the new bylaw would take effect July 1, 2026, introducing updated rules on building heights, parking, green space, tenant protections, and design standards across the city.
briefs.action_required: Check if your property's zoning district is changing — the new zoning map takes effect July 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 proposed effective date — new rules take effect
Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 PPA transition deadline — old approvals expire
In-progress rezoning bylaws inconsistent with Burnaby 2050 OCP — adoption deadline

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to vote on transferring $2M to Burnaby Housing Authority for Year 2 operations
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving a $2 million funding transfer to the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA) — a city-owned company set up to build and manage affordable and market rental housing. The money would come from the City's Operating Housing Reserve and cover the BHA's day-to-day costs for its second year of operation.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you work in Burnaby's housing or development sector.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — BHA Year 2 funding approval public meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby's 2026 property tax rates for all property classes
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the 2026 property tax rates for municipal, regional, and Business Improvement Area (BIA) purposes. A proposed bylaw would set the combined municipal tax rate for businesses (Class 6) at $6.39 per $1,000 of assessed value — reflecting a 2.9% general increase and a 1.9% growth infrastructure increase over 2025 levels.
briefs.action_required: Check your property class and review the proposed rates — your 2026 tax bill will reflect these if approved.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — 2026 property tax rates approval
Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — statutory deadline to pass by
Hastings BIA tax — first remittance (30%) deadline
Hastings BIA tax — second remittance (70%) deadline
MVRD regional tax remittance to Metro Vancouver deadline

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Council to vote on appointing a new Approving Officer for Burnaby subdivisions
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on appointing Mark Norton, Manager of Development, as an additional Approving Officer — the city official authorized to approve subdivision applications (requests to divide or re-divide land). Adding a fourth Approving Officer is meant to keep subdivision applications moving quickly and ensure coverage when other officers are unavailable.
briefs.action_required: If you have a subdivision application pending in Burnaby, watch for this approval — it may speed up your file.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Approving Officer appointment — Council vote date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to vote on approving oversized retaining walls at 8012 11th Avenue, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a Development Variance Permit (DVP) — a special approval that allows a property to bend a specific zoning rule — for 8012 11th Avenue. The permit would allow retaining walls on the east and west sides of the property to stand taller than the 1.2 m limit set by the Zoning Bylaw. The eastern wall reaches 2.43 m and the western wall reaches 2.06 m, and both walls also extend slightly onto neighbouring properties.
briefs.action_required: If you own or live near 8012 11th Avenue, attend the April 28 Council meeting to have your say.

key_dates.label: Burnaby DVP #26-01 retaining wall variance — Council vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby's $4 million insurance contract renewals for 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving roughly $4 million in insurance premiums across 12 contracts for the 2026 policy year, covering city property, liability, cybersecurity, and more. The overall cost is proposed to drop about 4% (around $180,000) compared to last year, with coverage renewing May 1, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Watch the April 28 council vote — no direct action needed for most businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby 2026 insurance programs renewal takes effect

briefs.title: Council to vote on: $4,000 for two Heritage Commission members to attend Squamish conference
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on spending up to $4,000 to send two members of the Community Heritage Commission to the 2026 Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC (May 4–6, 2026). The funds would cover registration, hotel, meals, mileage, and parking, and are already budgeted under the city's Advisory Body, Boards and Commission budget.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1M+ contract for Burnaby's core networking software licenses
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth approximately $1,047,545 (including taxes) to Insight Canada Inc. for Cisco DNA software licenses — the digital tools that keep the City's internet, Wi-Fi, payment systems, and communications running. The proposed contract would run for three years, with the option to extend for up to two additional one-year terms.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision — this contract affects City digital infrastructure services.

key_dates.label: Burnaby networking software contract award — council vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1.96M furniture contract for five new Burnaby civic facilities
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth roughly $1.96 million (plus a $196,260 contingency) to Heritage Office Furnishings Ltd. to supply, deliver, and install furniture across five new community facilities opening over the next five years. The facilities include the James Cowan Theatre, Community Safety Building, Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre, Cameron Community Centre and Library, and Brentwood Community Centre.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you supply furniture or fit-out services to the City.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — civic furniture contract award vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on $47,000 contract increase for Burnaby's external audit services
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on adding $47,000 to its existing contract with KPMG LLP for external audit services. The extra cost covers additional audit work triggered by new public sector accounting rules that were not part of the original 2021 contract — rules that the City is legally required to follow.
briefs.action_required: No direct action needed — this is an internal City finance matter with no impact on most businesses.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $384,893 contract increase for Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex ground testing
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on approving an extra $384,893 (including taxes) for WSP Canada Inc. to continue geotechnical consulting — that is, expert soil and ground testing — on the Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex project. Unexpected poor ground conditions were found during construction, so additional engineering work is needed to find safe design solutions. Council will also consider a $73,500 contingency (backup fund) in case more ground problems come up.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if your business works in construction or site services near this project.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex geotechnical contract change

briefs.title: Council to vote on $215,000+ in Spring 2026 community grants for Burnaby nonprofits
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving 31 community grants totalling over $215,000 for the Spring 2026 intake. The grants would support local nonprofits across five categories: recreational sports, operating costs, community initiatives, subsidized use of city facilities, and food security programs.
briefs.action_required: If your nonprofit applied, check whether it appears on the eligible or ineligible list before the April 28 vote.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Spring 2026 Community Grant applications — Council vote

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update recreation, food service, and hotel fees mid-2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed changes to Burnaby's fee schedule for Parks, Recreation and Culture services. A proposed bylaw would rename the Food Services schedule to 'Burnaby Hospitality' and add room rates for Hotel Burnaby (effective June 1, 2026), while a separate proposed bylaw would replace the full Recreation and Culture fee schedule — including lowering the free Seniors Lifetime Card eligibility age from 90 to 80 — effective September 1, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Check the updated fee schedules on the Burnaby city website once adopted — new rates take effect June 1 and September 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Hospitality (Schedule F1) fee amendments take effect
Burnaby Recreation and Culture (Schedule F3) fee amendments take effect

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 — Council to vote on new renter rules
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed Tenant Protection Bylaw that would replace the current Tenant Assistance Policy and extend renter protections to redevelopments that don't require rezoning — meaning more displaced tenants would be covered. The proposed bylaw, targeted to take effect July 1, 2026, would give the City new enforcement tools, including fines up to $500 and the ability to stop construction if a developer breaks the rules.
briefs.action_required: If you own or manage rental property in Burnaby, review the proposed bylaw before July 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 proposed effective date — takes effect
Burnaby Council vote on Tenant Protection Bylaw — public meeting

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Council to vote on Southeast Bikeway Upgrade: new bike lanes, signals, and traffic changes
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving proposed improvements for the Southeast Bikeway Upgrade Project. If approved, the plan would add protected bike lanes, upgrade traffic signals, create multi-use pathways (shared paths for cyclists and pedestrians), and change traffic flow on parts of 12th Avenue — with the goal of making cycling safer across southeast Burnaby and into Vancouver and New Westminster.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near 12th Avenue, North Fraser Way, or Armstrong Avenue, review the proposed changes now.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Southeast Bikeway Upgrade proposed improvements

briefs.title: Council to vote on safer intersection design for Burnaby Town Centres
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on updating the Town Centre Design Standards to include 'protected intersection geometry' — a road design that keeps cyclists and pedestrians physically separated from turning vehicles all the way through an intersection, not just along the street. If endorsed, the new design rules would apply to newly built intersections in Brentwood, Metrotown, Lougheed, and Edmonds as those areas are redeveloped, with costs covered by developers rather than the City.
briefs.action_required: If you develop or own property in a Burnaby Town Centre, watch for this decision — it may affect future site design requirements.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards amendment — Council vote

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Brentwood Park Cannot Be Exempted from Provincial Multi-Unit Housing Rules
briefs.summary: Council will consider a staff report explaining that Burnaby cannot legally exempt Brentwood Park from provincial small-scale multi-unit housing (SSMUH) rules — rules that require cities to allow triplexes, fourplexes, and small apartment buildings on most residential lots. Staff looked at whether old private property restrictions (called Restrictive Covenants and Statutory Building Schemes — legal agreements registered on a property's title that limit how it can be used) could be used to block this housing, but found no legal way to do so for one neighbourhood only.
briefs.action_required: If you own property in Brentwood Park or hold a Restrictive Covenant, watch for the April 28 council decision.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — Brentwood Park SSMUH staff report considered

briefs.title: Council to Receive: Burnaby Building Permit Activity Report for March 2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a routine information report covering all building permits issued in Burnaby between March 1 and March 31, 2026. No decisions or new rules are being made — this report simply keeps Council informed about construction activity happening across the city during that period.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Heritage Documentation for Forest Lawn Memorial Park Chapel Before Demolition
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to receive — for information only — an as-found report and a Statement of Significance for heritage resources at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue (Forest Lawn Memorial Park). The as-found report documents the 1962 Chapel building before it is demolished as part of a larger redevelopment. A Statement of Significance identifies what makes the memorial park historically important and which features should be protected going forward.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near or connected to Forest Lawn Memorial Park, watch for the rezoning final adoption later in 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — heritage documentation report received for information

briefs.title: Council to Consider Burnaby's 2025 Solid Waste and Recycling Annual Report
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive the 2025 Solid Waste and Recycling Annual Report for information at its April 28, 2026 meeting. The report shows the city collected 74,489 tonnes of waste and recyclables in 2025, hit a 61% diversion rate (meaning 61% of waste was kept out of the landfill), and outlines upcoming plans like flexible plastics pilots, more cleanup events, and expanded recycling outreach.
briefs.action_required: Review the report if your business generates construction waste or recyclables — new diversion threshold changes may be coming.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's Update on Plant-Based Food Options at City Facilities
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive an update on food sustainability efforts at city-run cafes and restaurants. The report — up for consideration at the April 28, 2026 meeting — outlines how staff have been adding plant-based, vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free menu options at locations like Riverway and Mintara, while keeping an eye on customer demand and financial performance.
briefs.action_required: If you supply food or services to Burnaby city facilities, watch for shifts in menu demand toward plant-based options.
briefs.industries: Food Service, Grocery & Food Retail, Agriculture

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Pedestrian Scramble Signal and Safety Upgrades at Metrotown Parkade Intersection
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to receive a report on proposed safety improvements at the busy intersection of Central Boulevard and the Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade entrance. The plan would add a 'pedestrian scramble' signal phase — where all traffic stops so people can cross in any direction, including diagonally — along with upgraded curb ramps, better pedestrian waiting areas, and the removal of a right-turn island to reduce pedestrian exposure to traffic.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near Metrotown, watch for construction notices starting Spring 2026 that may affect access.

key_dates.label: Central Boulevard parkade intersection construction completion target

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 — Three New Rezoning Bylaws Up for First and Second Reading
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on first and second reading of three proposed rezoning bylaws at the April 28, 2026 meeting. The proposals would permit a hotel and restaurant amendment to the Lougheed mixed-use towers (REZ #24-23), a 12-storey mixed-use residential building on Kingsway (REZ #25-09), and a five-storey self-storage building on Norland Avenue (REZ #25-21).
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed rezonings — if your business or property is near these sites, watch for the council decision.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 repeal and replacement takes effect
Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 in-progress PPA transition period deadline

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on adding hotel, restaurant uses and 362 units at Lougheed towers
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend an already-approved rezoning for two high-rise towers at 3900 Grand Promenade and 6950 Austin Road. The changes would swap some planned office space for hotel and restaurant uses, add 362 more residential units, make both towers taller, and shift an affordable housing requirement to a nearby site called Carrigan.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this could affect your plans if you operate or lease space near the Lougheed development.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 7, 2026 — council consideration date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on 12-storey mixed-use development at 6535 Kingsway
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed zoning bylaw (Bylaw No. 14809) that would rezone the property at 6535 Kingsway to allow a 12-storey building with 290 residential units, ground-floor commercial spaces facing Kingsway, and underground parking. If approved, any development on the site must follow the approved building plan prepared by Chris Dikeakos Architects Inc. for developer Intracorp.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the Burnaby Council meeting on April 28, 2026 — this rezoning could affect nearby commercial tenants or competitors.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 8, 2026 (6535 Kingsway) — Council vote

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby rezoning at 2757 Norland Ave to allow five-storey self-storage building
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed zoning bylaw (Bylaw No. 14810) that would rezone the property at 2757 Norland Avenue in Burnaby. If approved, the rezoning would allow a developer to build a five-storey enclosed self-storage facility on the site.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the Burnaby Council meeting on 2026-04-28 for the vote outcome on this rezoning.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 14810 — Council vote date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — Council to vote on 2026 property tax rates
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on the Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026, which would establish municipal, regional, and Business Improvement Area (BIA) property tax rates for 2026. The proposed bylaw reflects a 2.9% general municipal tax rate increase and a 1.9% growth infrastructure investment rate increase, and must be adopted before May 15, 2026 as required by the Community Charter.
briefs.action_required: Check your 2026 property tax notice once the bylaw is adopted — rates have increased.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — must be adopted by this date (Community Charter deadline)

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Retail

briefs.title: Council to vote on new e-bike and electric kick scooter rules for Burnaby streets
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would set clear rules for riding e-bikes, motor-assisted cycles, and electric kick scooters on city streets. The bylaw would ban scooters from major roads unless there is a protected bike lane or multi-use pathway, require riders to yield to pedestrians at crosswalks, and create designated parking zones for shared rental devices like dockless e-bikes and scooters.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if you use or operate shared e-bikes or scooters for your business, new parking and riding rules may apply.

key_dates.label: Burnaby e-bike and electric kick scooter bylaw — council vote scheduled

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw would regulate e-bikes and electric scooters in city parks
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update Burnaby's parks rules to officially include e-bikes (motor assisted cycles) and electric kick scooters. If passed, these devices could only be ridden or parked in parks where the City has specifically set aside space for them — all other park areas would be off-limits.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if your business involves e-bikes or scooters in Burnaby parks.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to set $200 fines for e-bikes and e-scooters in non-designated park areas
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would update the fine structure for riding or parking e-bikes and electric kick scooters in Burnaby parks where such use is not permitted. The proposed bylaw would set a full fine of $200 (or $160 if paid early) for anyone caught using these devices outside designated areas.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this could affect how you or your staff use e-bikes or e-scooters in Burnaby parks.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on 2026 property tax rates for all property classes
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would set the 2026 property tax rates for all taxable land and buildings in Burnaby. A 'mill rate' is the amount of tax charged per $1,000 of a property's assessed value — and under this proposal, businesses in Class 2 (Utility) and Class 4 (Major Industry) would face the highest rates, while Class 6 (Business and Other) properties on the Hastings corridor would also pay an additional Business Improvement Area (BIA) levy.
briefs.action_required: Check which property class your business falls under and calculate your estimated 2026 tax bill before the bylaw is adopted.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — Council vote at meeting
Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — deadline for adoption under Community Charter

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Council to consider scheduling Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee meeting for May 2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on scheduling a Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee (PRCC) meeting for Wednesday, May 27, 2026, at 5:00 pm in the Council Chamber. This is a procedural item to set the date for an upcoming committee meeting — no policy changes are being decided at this time.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the PRCC meeting agenda for May 27 if your business is affected by parks, recreation, or culture policy.
